topics.title: Waste to Energy Technology: Thermo-Chemical Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Waste to Energy Technology: Thermo-Chemical Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermo-Chemical Conversion (TCC)** of waste is a class of energy recovery processes in which the chemical energy stored in the molecular bonds of organic waste materials (biomass, municipal solid waste, industrial sludge, plastics, etc.) is converted into usable thermal energy, combustible gases, liquid fuels, or solid char through the application of heat and controlled chemical reactions.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, thermo-chemical conversion is formally defined under three primary technological routes, each distinguished by the prevailing **oxygen stoichiometry** and **operating temperature regime**:

| Route | Oxygen Availability | Temperature Range (°C) | Primary Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Combustion / Incineration** | Excess air ($\lambda > 1$) | 800 – 1200 | Heat (flue gas) |
| **Gasification** | Sub-stoichiometric ($0.2 < \lambda < 0.4$) | 700 – 1200 | Producer gas (syngas) |
| **Pyrolysis** | Anaerobic ($\lambda \approx 0$) | 300 – 800 | Bio-oil, char, gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is mapped to **CO 2** – "Identify and apply appropriate sustainable technologies for waste management and energy recovery." It is a high-weightage topic in Module 2 of the OESFT721 syllabus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine the **three stages of lighting a campfire in your kitchen stove**:

- **Combustion (the roaring fire with full air):** You open the stove dampers wide. The wood burns with a bright yellow flame, leaves behind only a pinch of white ash, and heats the room. This is **complete oxidation** — every carbon and hydrogen atom in the wood is "eaten" by oxygen to release the maximum possible heat.

- **Gasification (the smouldering coal-bed with limited air):** You choke the stove of air. The wood turns red, gives off a pungent blue flame, and you hear a hissing gas emerging. That gas is **synthetic fuel** (CO + H₂) — it can be piped away and burned later in a furnace or engine.

- **Pyrolysis (the sealed Dutch oven in the oven):** You place wood in an airtight container and heat it. No flame appears. Inside, the wood transforms into charcoal (solid), a tar-like liquid dripping from the lid, and gases. These are the three products of **thermal decomposition in absence of air**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The single most important variable that separates these three technologies is the **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** = (Actual air supplied) ÷ (Stoichiometric air required). Combustion: ER > 1; Gasification: ER $\approx$ 0.25–0.40; Pyrolysis: ER $\approx$ 0.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics govern all thermo-chemical calculations:

- **Standard Enthalpy of Combustion of Carbon (C → CO₂):** $\Delta H_c = -393.5$ **kJ/mol** (or $-32.8$ **MJ/kg**)
- **Standard Enthalpy of Combustion of Hydrogen (H₂ → H₂O):** $\Delta H_c = -285.8$ **kJ/mol** (or $-141.8$ **MJ/kg**)
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** of typical MSW: **10 – 15 MJ/kg**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of typical MSW: **8 – 12 MJ/kg**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** $T = 273.15$ **K**, $P = 101.325$ **kPa**

> [!NOTE]
> **HHV vs LHV:** HHV includes the latent heat recovered when water vapour condenses. LHV assumes water leaves as vapour. For Indian MSW (high moisture, ~40–60%), **LHV is the operationally correct parameter** to use in design calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Three Routes Explained

### 2.1.1 Combustion (Incineration)

Combustion is the **complete oxidation** of combustible matter in waste. The governing global reaction for a generic waste of empirical formula $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e$ is:

$$\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e + \left(a + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2} + e\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow a\text{CO}_2 + \frac{b}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \frac{d}{2}\text{N}_2 + e\text{SO}_2$$

**Why it matters:**
- It is the **most mature and widely deployed** WtE technology in India (e.g., Timarpur-Okhla, Delhi).
- Provides **volume reduction up to 90%** and **mass reduction up to 75%**.
- Net thermal efficiency: **20 – 30%** (with combined heat and power) up to **35%**.

**Process Components:**
1. **Waste Receiving & Storage Pit** (3–5 days capacity)
2. **Feeding System** (grab crane + hopper)
3. **Combustion Chamber / Grate Furnace** (moving or rotary)
4. **Boiler / Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)**
5. **Air Pollution Control (APC)** — scrubbers, ESP, fabric filters
6. **Ash Handling** — bottom ash (~25%) and fly ash (~5%)
7. **Stack Emission Monitoring** — CEMS for CO, NO$_x$, SO$_2$, PM, dioxins

### 2.1.2 Gasification

Gasification is the **partial oxidation** of carbonaceous feedstock to produce a **producer gas** (also called syngas when the feedstock is clean biomass). The product gas has a calorific value of **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³**.

**Key sub-reactions inside a gasifier (in sequence of temperature zones):**

$$\text{Drying} \quad \text{Wet waste} \xrightarrow{100-150°C} \text{Dry waste} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$$

$$\text{Pyrolysis} \quad \text{Dry waste} \xrightarrow{300-500°C} \text{Char} + \text{Tar} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CO} + \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_x\text{H}_y$$

$$\text{Oxidation (Combustion Zone)} \quad \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{Reduction Zone} \quad \text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol (endothermic — Boudouard reaction)}$$

$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol (Water-gas reaction)}$$

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol (Water-gas shift)}$$

$$\text{Methanation} \quad \text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Why it matters:**
- Produces a versatile **gaseous fuel** usable in IC engines, gas turbines, or Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.
- Higher electrical efficiency (**30 – 40%**) compared to direct combustion.
- Lower NO$_x$ emissions due to reducing (oxygen-starved) atmosphere.

**Types of Gasifiers:**
- **Fixed-bed (downdraft, updraft, crossdraft)**
- **Fluidized-bed (bubbling, circulating)**
- **Entrained-flow** (for high-ash Indian coal)

### 2.1.3 Pyrolysis

Pyrolysis is the **thermal decomposition** of organic matter in the **complete absence of oxygen** (or with oxygen limited to $\lambda < 0.05$). The process yields three product phases whose proportions depend critically on the **heating rate** and **residence time**.

**Product Distribution by Pyrolysis Mode:**

| Pyrolysis Mode | Heating Rate | Temperature (°C) | Residence Time | Char | Bio-oil | Gas |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow (Carbonization)** | Low | 300 – 500 | Hours – Days | 35% | 30% | 35% |
| **Fast** | High | 500 – 800 | 1 – 2 s | 12% | 75% | 13% |
| **Flash** | Very High | 800 – 1000 | < 1 s | 10% | 80% | 10% |
| **Vacuum** | Medium | 400 – 600 | Seconds | 20% | 60% | 20% |

**Why it matters:**
- Bio-oil is a **renewable liquid fuel** — can substitute fuel oil in boilers and furnaces.
- Biochar is a high-value **soil amendment** (carbon sequestration potential: 2.1 Gt CO₂eq/yr globally).
- The only thermo-chemical route that yields a **condensable liquid** as the major product.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Memorize the table below — it covers approximately 80% of all numerical questions asked in KTU ESE on this topic. Always state units explicitly in your final answer; unit errors account for ~30% of marks lost.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand (SOD) | $\text{O}_2^{st} = \left(a + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2} + e\right) \times \frac{32}{12a + b + 16c + 14d + 32e}$ | kg O₂ / kg waste |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air Requirement (SAR) | $\text{Air}_{st} = \frac{\text{O}_2^{st}}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg waste (21% O₂ by vol, 23.2% by mass) |
| 3 | Equivalence Ratio | $\text{ER} = \frac{(\text{Air/Fuel})_{actual}}{(\text{Air/Fuel})_{stoich}}$ | Combustion: ER > 1; Gasification: ER $\approx$ 0.25–0.40; Pyrolysis: ER $\approx$ 0 |
| 4 | Higher Heating Value (HHV) — Dulong's Formula | $\text{HHV} = 33800\,\text{C} + 144200\left(\text{H} - \frac{\text{O}}{8}\right) + 9270\,\text{S}$ | kJ/kg; C, H, O, S in mass fractions |
| 5 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2442\left(\frac{9\text{H}}{100} + \text{M}\right)$ | kJ/kg; M = moisture fraction |
| 6 | Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass (AFR) | $\text{AFR} = \frac{1}{\text{ER}} \times \frac{28.97 \times (a + b/4 - c/2 + e)}{12a + b + 16c + 14d + 32e}$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 7 | Mass of Flue Gas | $m_{flue} = m_{waste} + \text{Air}_{actual} - m_{ash}$ | kg / kg waste |
| 8 | Energy Recovery Efficiency (Boiler) | $\eta_{boiler} = \frac{m_{steam}(h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})}{m_{waste} \times \text{LHV}_{waste}}$ | Decimal; typical 0.80 – 0.85 |
| 9 | Overall WtE Plant Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator}$ | Decimal; typical 0.20 – 0.30 |
| 10 | Gasifier Cold Gas Efficiency | $\eta_{CGE} = \frac{\dot{m}_{gas} \times \text{LHV}_{gas}}{m_{waste} \times \text{LHV}_{waste}}$ | Typical 60 – 80% |
| 11 | Heat of Pyrolysis (Lumped) | $Q_{pyro} = \sum_i m_i c_{p,i} \Delta T + m_{char}\Delta H_{carbonization}$ | kJ |
| 12 | Volume of Producer Gas (Ideal Gas) | $V_{gas} = \frac{nRT}{P}$ | m³ at STP |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in (i) designing municipal incinerators for the Smart Cities Mission, (ii) sizing gasifiers for rice-husk-to-power projects in Punjab & Tamil Nadu, and (iii) evaluating pyrolysis units for plastic-to-fuel compliance with the PWM (Plastic Waste Management) Rules, 2022.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement (SAR) for Generic Waste

**Problem Statement:** A municipal solid waste sample has the following ultimate (elemental) analysis by mass: C = 40%, H = 6%, O = 35%, N = 1%, S = 0.5%, Moisture = 8%, Ash = 9.5%. Calculate the (a) Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand, (b) Stoichiometric Air Requirement, and (c) Actual Air Required at 50% excess air.

### Step 1 — Write the Per-Mole Combustion Equation

For a fuel with empirical formula $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e$ (on a 1-kmol basis), complete combustion in air requires:

$$\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e + \alpha(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \rightarrow a\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{b}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \frac{d}{2}\text{N}_2 + e\,\text{SO}_2$$

where the stoichiometric oxygen coefficient is:

$$\alpha_{st} = a + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2} + e$$

### Step 2 — Balance Carbon (C)

$$a = 1 \text{ kmol of C per kmol of "fuel unit"}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Numerical Values

The molar quantities (per kg of waste) are computed from mass fractions divided by atomic masses:

$$a = \frac{0.40}{12.011} = 0.0333 \text{ kmol C/kg}$$

$$b = \frac{0.06}{1.008} = 0.0595 \text{ kmol H/kg}$$

$$c = \frac{0.35}{15.999} = 0.0219 \text{ kmol O/kg}$$

$$d = \frac{0.01}{14.007} = 0.000714 \text{ kmol N/kg}$$

$$e = \frac{0.005}{32.065} = 0.000156 \text{ kmol S/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand (SOD)

$$\alpha_{st} = 0.0333 + \frac{0.0595}{4} - \frac{0.0219}{2} + 0.000156$$

$$\alpha_{st} = 0.0333 + 0.01488 - 0.01094 + 0.000156$$

$$\alpha_{st} = 0.0374 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$

Converting to mass of O₂:

$$\text{SOD} = 0.0374 \times 32 = 1.197 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Stoichiometric Air Requirement (SAR)

By mass, dry air contains **23.2% oxygen** (since air is 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume, with molar masses 32 and 28):

$$\text{SAR} = \frac{\text{SOD}}{0.232} = \frac{1.197}{0.232} = 5.16 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$$

### Step 6 — Apply 50% Excess Air

For complete combustion, actual air supplied exceeds stoichiometric air:

$$\text{Air}_{actual} = 1.50 \times \text{SAR} = 1.50 \times 5.16 = 7.74 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** The theoretical air for a hydrocarbon with C/H ratio of $\approx 6.7$ should be in the range 5–6 kg air/kg fuel. Our result of **5.16 kg/kg** is internally consistent.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Balance on a Combustion Chamber

For a steady-state, adiabatic combustion chamber (no heat loss), the energy balance reduces to:

$$\sum_{\text{in}} m_i h_i = \sum_{\text{out}} m_j h_j$$

The **combustion efficiency** is the ratio of heat released to the heating value of the fuel. For the same waste at 25°C reference:

$$Q_{released} = m_{waste} \times \text{LHV}_{waste} \times \eta_{combustion}$$

A typical Indian MSW with the composition above has LHV $\approx 13.5$ **MJ/kg**. The energy released per kg of waste:

$$Q_{released} = 1 \times 13.5 \times 10^3 \times 0.95 = 12{,}825 \text{ kJ/kg waste}$$

This energy is partitioned as: **sensible heat of flue gas (60–70%)**, **latent heat of water vapour (10–15%)**, and **heat loss + unburnt carbon (15–25%)**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Combustion vs Gasification vs Pyrolysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a guaranteed Part A (3-mark) or short Part B sub-question in KTU 2024 ESE.** Memorize the qualitative contrasts.

| Parameter | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen / Air | Excess | Sub-stoichiometric (limited) | None / Trace |
| Operating Temperature (°C) | 800 – 1200 | 700 – 1200 | 300 – 800 |
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | > 1 | 0.25 – 0.40 | $\approx$ 0 |
| Primary Product | Heat (steam) | Producer gas (CO + H₂) | Bio-oil + Char + Gas |
| Energy Recovery Form | Thermal → Electrical | Chemical → Electrical/Mechanical | Chemical (Liquid fuel) |
| Flue Gas Volume | Very High | Moderate | Very Low |
| NO$_x$ Formation | High (thermal + fuel) | Low (reducing atmosphere) | Negligible |
| SO$_x$ Formation | High | Moderate | Low (S retained in char) |
| Dioxin / Furans | Possible (must control) | Very Low | Negligible |
| Net Thermal Efficiency | 20 – 30% | 30 – 40% | 50 – 70% (energy in product) |
| Indian Regulatory Status | Mature (CPCB Guidelines 2017) | Emerging (MNRE pilots) | Research / Pilot |
| Typical Plant Capacity | 100 – 3000 TPD | 1 – 100 TPD | 0.5 – 50 TPD |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Thermo-Chemical Conversion Family

This Mermaid diagram maps the **decision tree** of thermo-chemical conversion routes based on oxygen supply and temperature — a frequent 7-mark sub-question in Part B.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organic Waste Feedstock] --> B{Waste Type and Goal}
    B -->|Heat and Power from MSW| C[Air Supply Selection]

    C --> C1{Equivalence Ratio ER}
    C1 -->|ER greater than 1| F[Combustion Chamber 800 to 1200 C]
    C1 -->|ER 0.25 to 0.40| G[Gasifier 700 to 1200 C]
    F --> F1[Hot Flue Gas to HRSG Boiler]
    F1 --> F2[Steam to Turbine Generator]
    F2 --> F3[Electricity and Condensate Return]
    F1 --> F4[Air Pollution Control APC Train]
    F4 --> F5[Stack and CEMS Monitoring]
    G --> G1[Producer Gas CO and H2]
    G1 --> G2[Gas Cleaning Cyclone and Scrubber]
    G2 --> G3[Engine or Turbine Combustion]
    G3 --> F2
    D --> H[Pyrolysis Reactor 300 to 800 C Anaerobic]
    H --> H1[Quench Condenser]
    H1 --> H2[Bio oil Liquid Fuel Storage]
    H --> H3[Non Condensable Gases Recycle to Heater]
    H --> H4[Bio char Collection and Packaging]
    E --> I[Slow Pyrolysis Carbonization]
    I --> I1[Charcoal for Soil Amendment]
    I --> I2[Pyrolytic Gas to Combustor]
    I --> I3[Condensable Tars to Bio oil Tank]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the path from any feedstock (A) to its end-product (electricity, fuel, or soil amendment) to identify the required reactor type, temperature, and oxygen condition.

## 4.2 Combustion Plant (Mass-Burn Incinerator) — Block Architecture

This schematic is the **standard KTU exam model** for an Indian MSW incinerator with energy recovery.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Waste Collection Vehicle] --> P2[Weighbridge and Reception]
    P2 --> P3[Storage Pit 3 to 5 days]
    P3 --> P4[Grab Crane Feeding]
    P4 --> P5[Feed Hopper]
    P5 --> P6[Grate Furnace Combustion Chamber]
    P6 --> P7[Primary Combustion Air Fan]
    P6 --> P8[Secondary Combustion Air Overfire]
    P8 --> P9[Secondary Chamber 850 C 2 sec residence]
    P9 --> P10[Heat Recovery Steam Generator HRSG]
    P10 --> P11[Steam Turbine Generator STG]
    P11 --> P12[Transformer and Grid]
    P10 --> P13[Economiser and Air Preheater]
    P13 --> P14[Flue Gas to APC]
    P14 --> P15[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP]
    P15 --> P16[Wet Scrubber for SO2 and HCl]
    P16 --> P17[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR for NOx]
    P17 --> P18[Fabric Filter Baghouse for PM and Dioxins]
    P18 --> P19[Induced Draft Fan IDF]
    P19 --> P20[Stack with CEMS]
    P6 --> P21[Bottom Ash Hopper Water Quench]
    P18 --> P22[Fly Ash Silo Hazardous]
    style P6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style P9 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style P10 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    style P18 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style P22 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Downdraft Gasifier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Biomass Feed Hopper Top] --> G2[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    G2 --> G3[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 600 C]
    G3 --> G4[Oxidation Zone 800 to 1200 C Air Entry]
    G4 --> G5[Reduction Zone 700 to 900 C]
    G5 --> G6[Char Bed and Ash Discharge]
    G6 --> G7[Producer Gas Outlet Hot]
    G7 --> G8[Cyclone Particulate Removal]
    G8 --> G9[Water Scrubber Tar and Dust]
    G9 --> G10[Gas Filter and Blower]
    G10 --> G11[IC Engine or Burner]
    style G4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    style G5 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style G11 fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In a downdraft gasifier, air and gas flow co-currently downward. This configuration naturally passes the producer gas through the hot reduction zone, **cracking tars** and yielding a cleaner gas with **calorific value 4–6 MJ/Nm³**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between combustion, gasification, and pyrolysis as waste-to-energy technologies. List the equivalence ratio for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** | $> 1$ (excess air) | $0.25 - 0.40$ (limited air) | $\approx 0$ (no air) |
| **Temperature (°C)** | 800 – 1200 | 700 – 1200 | 300 – 800 |
| **Primary Product** | Heat (steam) | Producer gas (CO + H₂) | Bio-oil + char + gas |
| **Oxygen Condition** | Oxidizing | Reducing | Anaerobic |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for explicit mention of ER ranges with units.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, SAMPLE]

**Q: Define Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a waste fuel. Why is LHV preferred for Indian MSW?**

**Model Answer:**

- **HHV (Gross Calorific Value, GCV):** The total heat released when **1 kg of fuel is completely combusted** and the products of combustion are cooled back to the original temperature, allowing the **latent heat of condensed water vapour** to be recovered. **Unit: kJ/kg or MJ/kg.**

- **LHV (Net Calorific Value, NCV):** The total heat released when **1 kg of fuel is completely combusted** and the products are allowed to leave the system **with water in the vapour phase**. **Unit: kJ/kg or MJ/kg.**

- **LHV is preferred for Indian MSW** because Indian municipal solid waste has **moisture content of 40–60%** and the flue gas leaves the boiler at 150–250°C, well above the dew point of water (55°C). Hence, the latent heat of water vapour **cannot be practically recovered**, and using HHV would overestimate the recoverable energy by 8–12%.

*(1.5 marks each for definitions, 1.5 marks for the reasoning specific to Indian MSW.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions of Module 2 carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The model solutions below are calibrated to the 2024 marking scheme with explicit step-weightage.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** A municipal solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 38%, H = 5%, O = 30%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.4%, Moisture = 15%, Ash = 10.4%. Calculate the **(i)** stoichiometric air required for complete combustion per kg of waste, and **(ii)** the actual air supplied if 80% excess air is used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **moving grate incinerator** for energy recovery from MSW. List the key air pollution control devices used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Data (per kg of waste):**

$$\text{C} = 0.38 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{H} = 0.05 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{O} = 0.30 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{N} = 0.012 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{S} = 0.004 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 1 — Convert Mass Fractions to Moles**

$$n_C = \frac{0.38}{12.011} = 0.03164 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_H = \frac{0.05}{1.008} = 0.04960 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_O = \frac{0.30}{15.999} = 0.01875 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_S = \frac{0.004}{32.065} = 0.0001248 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stoichiometric Oxygen Formula**

$$\text{O}_2^{st} = n_C + \frac{n_H}{4} - \frac{n_O}{2} + n_S$$

$$\text{O}_2^{st} = 0.03164 + \frac{0.04960}{4} - \frac{0.01875}{2} + 0.0001248$$

$$\text{O}_2^{st} = 0.03164 + 0.01240 - 0.009375 + 0.0001248$$

$$\text{O}_2^{st} = 0.03479 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ Moles to O₂ Mass**

$$m_{O_2} = 0.03479 \times 32 = 1.113 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg waste}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Stoichiometric Air Requirement**

$$\text{SAR} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{1.113}{0.232} = 4.798 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 80% Excess Air**

$$\text{Air}_{actual} = (1 + 0.80) \times \text{SAR} = 1.80 \times 4.798 = 8.637 \text{ kg air/kg waste}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\text{SAR} = 4.80 \text{ kg air/kg waste} \quad ; \quad \text{Air}_{actual} = 8.64 \text{ kg air/kg waste}}$$

**Marking Weightage:** Step 1 (1 m), Step 2 (2 m), Step 3 (1 m), Step 4 (1.5 m), Step 5 (1 m), Final boxed answer with units (0.5 m).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Working of a Moving Grate Incinerator — Stepwise Description:**

1. **Waste Reception and Storage (1 m):** Waste is delivered to a sealed pit (3–5 days capacity) where a **grab crane** mixes and feeds the refuse to the charging hopper. Mixing homogenizes moisture and calorific value.

2. **Feeding and Grate (1.5 m):** The hopper feeds waste onto a **moving (reciprocating or roller) grate** that is inclined 25–30° from horizontal. The grate transports the waste through the furnace in **5 distinct zones**: drying, ignition, combustion, burnout, and ash discharge.

3. **Combustion Air Supply (1 m):** **Primary air** (60–80% of stoichiometric) is supplied below the grate; **secondary air** (overfire air, 20–40%) is injected above the bed at high velocity to ensure complete burnout of CO and hydrocarbons in the post-combustion zone held at **850°C for 2 seconds** (the "**2-second rule**" mandated by CPCB).

4. **Heat Recovery (1 m):** Hot flue gases (1000–1200°C) pass through a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** producing steam at 40–60 bar, 400–450°C, which drives a **steam turbine generator (STG)** for power generation.

5. **Air Pollution Control Train (1.5 m):** Sequence — **Dry Lime Scrubber** (for HCl, SO₂) → **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** (for NO$_x$) → **Fabric Filter / Baghouse** (for PM, dioxins, furans, heavy metals) → **Induced Draft Fan** → **Stack with CEMS** (Continuous Emission Monitoring System).

6. **Ash Handling (0.5 m):** **Bottom ash** (20–25%, non-hazardous, used in road construction) is quenched in water. **Fly ash** (3–5%, hazardous per BMW Rules 2016) is separately stored and sent to **TSDF** (Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility).

7. **Block Diagram (0.5 m):** Neat, labelled flowchart showing grate → HRSG → APC → Stack with ancillary streams.

**Key APC Devices:** Dry scrubber, SCR unit, ESP / Fabric filter, CEMS (1 mark reserved for explicit listing of at least 4 devices).

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, SAMPLE]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **working principle of a downdraft gasifier** for biomass-to-energy applications. Discuss the **four functional zones** inside the reactor with their respective temperature ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slow pyrolysis reactor is charged with **1000 kg/day of dry coconut shell** (proximate analysis: VM = 78%, FC = 19%, Ash = 3%). If the typical product yields are **35% char, 30% bio-oil, and 35% non-condensable gas by mass**, calculate the **daily production** of each product. Also compute the **LHV of producer gas** if its molar composition is 20% CO, 20% H₂, 5% CH₄, and 55% N₂ (by volume). Take LHV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/Nm³, H₂ = 12.1 MJ/Nm³, CO = 12.6 MJ/Nm³, N₂ = 0. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle of a Downdraft Gasifier:**

A downdraft gasifier is a **fixed-bed reactor** in which biomass feedstock and oxidizing agent (air or oxygen) move **co-currently in a downward direction**. The hot producer gas exiting at the bottom passes through the high-temperature reduction zone, which **thermally cracks tars and oils**, yielding a relatively clean gas suitable for direct use in IC engines. *(1 mark for principle, 1 mark for co-current flow explanation.)*

**Four Functional Zones (4 marks):**

| Zone | Process | Temperature (°C) | Key Reactions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Drying Zone** | Moisture evaporation | 100 – 200 | $\text{Wet biomass} \rightarrow \text{Dry biomass} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$ |
| **2. Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone** | Thermal decomposition | 200 – 600 | $\text{Biomass} \rightarrow \text{Char} + \text{Tar} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CO} + \text{CH}_4 + \text{C}_x\text{H}_y$ |
| **3. Oxidation / Combustion Zone** | Partial combustion of char | 800 – 1200 | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ (exothermic, supplies heat) |
| **4. Reduction Zone** | CO₂ and H₂O reduced to CO and H₂ | 700 – 900 | $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ (Boudouard); $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ (Water-gas) |

**Additional Elements (1 mark):**
- **Air entry nozzles** at the oxidation zone; **grate** at the bottom for ash removal; **gas outlet** below the reduction zone.
- **Advantage of downdraft:** Self-purging of tars → clean gas with **calorific value 4–6 MJ/Nm³**.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher pressure drop, sensitivity to feed moisture (must be < 20%).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Daily Mass Distribution of Pyrolysis Products (3 marks)**

$$\text{Char} = 1000 \times 0.35 = 350 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$\text{Bio-oil} = 1000 \times 0.30 = 300 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$\text{Non-condensable gas} = 1000 \times 0.35 = 350 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Volume Fractions to Mass Fractions (1.5 marks)**

Average molecular weight of gas mixture:

$$M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.20)(28) + (0.20)(2) + (0.05)(16) + (0.55)(28)$$

$$M_{mix} = 5.6 + 0.4 + 0.8 + 15.4 = 22.2 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Weighted LHV Formula (2 marks)**

$$\text{LHV}_{gas} = \sum y_i \times \text{LHV}_i$$

$$\text{LHV}_{gas} = (0.20)(12.6) + (0.20)(12.1) + (0.05)(55.5) + (0.55)(0)$$

$$\text{LHV}_{gas} = 2.52 + 2.42 + 2.775 + 0$$

$$\text{LHV}_{gas} = 7.715 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3$$

**Final Answer (boxed, 0.5 m):**

$$\boxed{\text{Char} = 350 \text{ kg/day} \;;\; \text{Bio-oil} = 300 \text{ kg/day} \;;\; \text{Gas} = 350 \text{ kg/day} \;;\; \text{LHV}_{gas} = 7.72 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Missing Units:** Writing "5.16" without "kg air/kg waste" loses **0.5 mark** per occurrence. **Always box units.**
> 2. **Using 21% instead of 23.2%:** Converting air volume to mass using 0.21 instead of 0.232 is a frequent error. **Memorize:** volume % × (32/28.97) for O₂, and overall air is 23.2% O₂ by mass.
> 3. **Forgetting N₂ from air:** Combustion equations MUST include $3.76 \text{N}_2$ for every O₂. Partial marking is awarded, but full credit requires the complete equation.
> 4. **Mixing HHV and LHV:** Using Dulong's HHV formula and then comparing to LHV-based efficiency is a common conceptual error. **State which value you are using explicitly.**
> 5. **Block Diagram without labelled streams:** In Part B sub-parts, an unlabelled block diagram scores 0. Every arrow must indicate **mass/energy flow direction** and **stream name**.
> 6. **ER confusion:** Writing ER = 1 for "ideal combustion." Actually ER = 1 is **stoichiometric** (theoretical), but real combustors operate at **ER = 1.5 to 2.0** (i.e., 50–100% excess air) to ensure complete burnout.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Master these for 100% score on this topic.**

- **The 3 Routes:** Combustion (excess air, heat), Gasification (limited air, syngas), Pyrolysis (no air, bio-oil + char).
- **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the master discriminator: ER > 1 (combustion), ER 0.25–0.40 (gasification), ER $\approx$ 0 (pyrolysis).
- **Stoichiometric O₂ formula:** $\text{O}_2 = a + b/4 - c/2 + e$ (kmol O₂ per kmol of fuel unit).
- **Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass** (not 21% — that's by volume).
- **HHV includes latent heat of water; LHV does not.** LHV is operationally correct for Indian MSW.
- **Dulong's formula** is the most-tested HHV estimator in KTU numericals.
- **Combustion produces** CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, NO$_x$, and ash. Requires APC for SO₂, NO$_x$, dioxins, PM.
- **Gasification key reactions** (in order): Drying → Pyrolysis → Boudouard → Water-gas → Water-gas-shift → Methanation.
- **Downdraft gasifier** = clean gas; **Updraft gasifier** = high tar; **Fluidized-bed** = uniform temperature, large scale.
- **Pyrolysis modes:** Slow (max char), Fast (max bio-oil), Flash (max gas), Vacuum.
- **Biochar** is a stable carbon product with soil-amendment and carbon-sequestration value.
- **MSW composition in India:** 40–60% moisture, 40–60% biodegradable, 10–30% inert, 5–15% recyclables. High moisture is the **single biggest design challenge** for Indian WtE plants.
- **CPCB norms (2017):** Combustion temperature $\geq$ 850°C, residence time $\geq$ 2 s, CO $\leq$ 50 mg/Nm³, dioxins $\leq$ 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.
- **MNRE thrust areas:** Biomass gasifiers for rural electrification (e.g., Karnataka, Tamil Nadu projects), pyrolysis for plastic-to-fuel (PWM Rules 2022 compliance).
- **Typical plant efficiencies:** Combustion WtE 20–30%, Gasification 30–40%, Pyrolysis 50–70% (energy in product).
- **Net carbon benefit:** Combustion of MSW avoids landfill methane (CH₄ has 28× the GWP of CO₂ over 100 years).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biochemical Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biochemical Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biochemical Conversion (KTU 2024 Definition):** Biochemical conversion refers to the controlled biological transformation of organic feedstocks (biomass, municipal solid waste, agricultural residues, animal manure, energy crops) into useful energy carriers (biogas, bioethanol, biodiesel, hydrogen) and value-added products (compost, organic acids, enzymes) through the metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria, archaea, fungi, yeast) under defined environmental conditions (temperature, pH, moisture, redox potential, C/N ratio).

In the context of KTU's OESFT721 syllabus, **biochemical conversion** is grouped under the broader category of *biomass-to-energy pathways* and is studied in direct contrast with **thermochemical conversion** (combustion, gasification, pyrolysis). The principal biochemical routes are:

| Route | Primary Output | Microbial Class |
|---|---|---|
| Anaerobic Digestion (AD) | Biogas ($\text{CH}_4$ + $\text{CO}_2$) | Bacteria + Archaea |
| Fermentation | Bioethanol, Biobutanol | Yeast (S. cerevisiae), Zymomonas |
| Composting | Humus / Soil Conditioner | Mixed aerobic microbiota |
| Transesterification (microbial lipase) | Biodiesel (FAME) | Lipase-producing fungi |
| Microbial Electrolysis | Biohydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) | Exoelectrogens |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen at dinner rush. The head chef (microorganisms) cannot eat a whole chicken leg in one bite. First, the prep-cook (hydrolytic bacteria) chops it into small pieces (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). Then the sauce-maker (acidogens) turns those into vinegar-like intermediates (volatile fatty acids). The line cook (acetogens) further refines these into a simple stock (acetic acid, $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$). Finally, the pastry chef (methanogens) bakes the final dish — **biogas** ($\text{CH}_4$). If oxygen leaks into the kitchen, the chefs leave (aerobic conditions = composting instead). Biochemical conversion is simply engineering a *perfect kitchen* — temperature, pH, and feed rate — so the chefs stay productive.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Operational Constants

The following values are the **standard benchmarks** that KTU examiners expect students to memorize for calculations on this topic:

- **Standard Methane Calorific Value (LCV):** $\text{LHV}_{\text{CH}_4} = 50 \text{ MJ/kg} = 35.88 \text{ MJ/m}^3$
- **Biogas Composition (typical AD):** $\text{CH}_4 = 50\text{–}65\%$, $\text{CO}_2 = 30\text{–}45\%$, trace $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Mesophilic Optimum:** $T = 35\text{–}40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Thermophilic Optimum:** $T = 50\text{–}55\,^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Optimal pH for Methanogens:** $6.5 \text{ – } 7.5$
- **Optimal C/N Ratio:** $20:1 \text{ – } 30:1$
- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** $15 \text{ – } 30 \text{ days}$ (mesophilic)
- **Theoretical Methane Yield (Buswell Equation):** $0.373 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$ (from glucose)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 expects students to differentiate biochemical conversion from thermochemical conversion and to identify the *four sequential stages* of anaerobic digestion. Memorize the **Buswell Equation** — it is a high-yield topic in the university exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

Anaerobic Digestion (AD) is the **flagship biochemical conversion route** for wet biomass. It proceeds in four biologically distinct, sequential, and syntrophic stages. Each stage is performed by a *different* class of microorganism.

### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis
- **Substrate:** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids).
- **Microbes:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus* (facultative + strict anaerobes).
- **Key Enzymes:** Cellulase, amylase, protease, lipase.
- **Reaction Type:** Extracellular enzymatic depolymerisation.
- **Output:** Monomers — sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids (LCFAs).
- **Bottleneck:** Hydrolysis of **lignocellulose** (cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin) is the rate-limiting step for woody biomass.

### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)
- **Substrate:** Monomers from Stage 1.
- **Microbes:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Enterobacter*.
- **Output:** Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) — acetate, propionate, butyrate; plus alcohols, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2$.
- **Operational Risk:** If VFAs accumulate faster than methanogens consume them, **pH crashes below 6.0**, inhibiting methanogens. This is the most common digester failure mode.

### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis
- **Substrate:** VFAs and alcohols (especially propionate, butyrate).
- **Microbes:** *Syntrophobacter wolinii*, *Syntrophomonas*.
- **Output:** Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$), $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$.
- **Thermodynamic Constraint:** This stage is **endergonic under standard conditions** ($\Delta G^0 > 0$). It only proceeds if $\text{H}_2$ partial pressure is kept low by methanogens — a classic *interspecies hydrogen transfer* (IHT) mechanism.

### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis
- **Substrate:** Acetate (≈ 70% of $\text{CH}_4$ produced), $\text{H}_2/\text{CO}_2$ (≈ 30%).
- **Microbes:** *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta* (aceticlastic); *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus* (hydrogenotrophic).
- **Two Pathways:**
  - **Aceticlastic:** $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$
  - **Hydrogenotrophic:** $\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Sensitivity:** Most pH- and temperature-sensitive stage; recovery after shock takes days to weeks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sequencing matters:** All four stages must operate *simultaneously* in the same digester, but the slowest stage dictates the **overall Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**. This is why AD design is kinetics-driven, not stoichiometry-driven.

## 2.2 Aerobic Biochemical Conversion — Composting

Composting is the **aerobic counterpart** of anaerobic digestion. Here, $\text{O}_2$ is supplied (forced aeration or windrow turning) and the dominant pathway is **oxidative mineralization** to $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and stabilized **humus**.

- **Mesophilic phase:** $20\text{–}40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (first 1–3 days)
- **Thermophilic phase:** $40\text{–}70\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (days 3–15) — kills pathogens and weed seeds
- **Curing/Maturation phase:** weeks to months — humification.

## 2.3 Ethanol Fermentation (Bioethanol Pathway)

- **Microbe:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (most common), *Zymomonas mobilis*.
- **Reaction (Glycolysis + Pyruvate Decarboxylation):**
  $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{CO}_2$$
- **Theoretical Yield:** $0.511 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose} = 0.568 \text{ L ethanol / kg glucose}$
- **Practical Yield:** ~ 90–95% of theoretical (process losses to cell mass).
- **Feedstocks:** Sugarcane juice (1G), lignocellulose hydrolysate (2G), algae (3G).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for biochemical conversion problems. KTU examiners often test *one* derivation-style question and *one* numerical from these.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buswell Equation (General) | $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2$$ | mol | Theoretical gas yield from any organic |
| 2 | Buswell — Glucose (n=6, a=12, b=6) | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{CO}_2$ | mol | Gives 0.373 m³ CH₄/kg VS |
| 3 | Theoretical Ethanol Yield | $Y_{EtOH} = 0.511 \cdot m_{glucose}$ | g/g | Stoichiometric max from glucose |
| 4 | Volumetric Methane Yield | $V_{CH_4} = Y_{s} \cdot m_{VS} \cdot f_{d}$ | m³ | $Y_s$=0.373, $f_d$=degradability |
| 5 | Biogas Energy Content | $E_{biogas} = V_{CH_4} \cdot \text{LHV}_{CH_4}$ | MJ | $LHV = 35.88 \text{ MJ/m}^3$ |
| 6 | C/N Ratio | $\text{C/N} = \dfrac{m_C / 12}{m_N / 14}$ | dimensionless | Optimal: 20–30 |
| 7 | Alkalinity Ratio | $\text{IA/PA} = \dfrac{[\text{Intermediate Acids}]}{[\text{Partial Alkalinity}]}$ | mg/L $\text{CaCO}_3$ | Stability: $\vert \text{IA/PA} \vert \le 0.3$ |
| 8 | HRT | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{Q}_{feed}}$ | days | AD design |
| 9 | OLR (Organic Loading Rate) | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{VS}}{V_{reactor}}$ | $\text{kg VS / m}^3 \cdot \text{d}$ | Sizing parameter |
| 10 | Gompertz Kinetic Model | $M(t) = M_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu_m \cdot e}{M_0}(\lambda - t) + 1\right)\right)$ | mL CH₄/g VS | Biogas potential (BMP) test |
| 11 | Free Ammonia Nitrogen | $\text{FAN} = \dfrac{[\text{TAN}]}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}$, $pK_a = 0.09018 + \dfrac{2729.92}{T(K)}$ | mg/L | Inhibition threshold: $\vert \text{FAN} \vert > 700$ mg/L |
| 12 | Methanogenesis Free Energy | $\Delta G^0_{CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4} = -31.0$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | Spontaneity check |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Solid Waste Management:** AD plants in Kerala (e.g., Brahmapuram-style facilities) convert urban food waste + sewage sludge to **biogas for grid electricity** + **digestate as biofertilizer** — closing the nutrient loop.
- **Agriculture:** Cattle dung-based family-size biogas plants (e.g., **KVIC model**, 1–6 m³/day) provide cooking fuel and slurry.
- **Transport Sector:** Bioethanol is blended with petrol under India's **E20 policy** (20% ethanol blending target).
- **Industrial:** Anaerobic effluent treatment in **sugar distilleries, breweries, dairy plants** — net energy-positive wastewater treatment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buswell Equation (General Molecular Balance)

The Buswell Equation is a stoichiometric tool that gives the **theoretical maximum methane and carbon dioxide yield** from any organic compound of formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b$. It is derived from the **principle of atomic conservation** (C, H, O atoms must balance) combined with the **methanogenic stoichiometry**.

**Step 1 — Write the half-balanced template.** Let the unknown stoichiometric coefficients be $x$, $y$, $z$, $w$ for the four compounds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b \;+\; x\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; y\,\text{CH}_4 \;+\; z\,\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Carbon balance** (C atoms in = C atoms out):
- C in: $n$ (from organic)
- C out: $y + z$ (1 in $\text{CH}_4$ + 1 in $\text{CO}_2$)
- Therefore: $y + z = n$   ... (Eq. A)

**Step 3 — Apply the Hydrogen balance** (H atoms in = H atoms out):
- H in: $a$ (from organic) + $2x$ (from water)
- H out: $4y$ (from $\text{CH}_4$)
- Therefore: $a + 2x = 4y \Rightarrow x = 2y - \dfrac{a}{2}$   ... (Eq. B)

**Step 4 — Apply the Oxygen balance** (O atoms in = O atoms out):
- O in: $b$ (from organic) + $x$ (from water)
- O out: $2z$ (from $\text{CO}_2$)
- Therefore: $b + x = 2z \Rightarrow z = \dfrac{b + x}{2}$   ... (Eq. C)

**Step 5 — Solve the system** using Eq. A as the master relation. From (A): $z = n - y$. Substitute into (C):
$$
n - y = \frac{b + x}{2} \Rightarrow 2n - 2y = b + x
$$
Substitute $x$ from (B):
$$
2n - 2y = b + 2y - \frac{a}{2} \Rightarrow 2n - b + \frac{a}{2} = 4y
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\,}
$$

Then back-substitute:
$$
\boxed{\,z = n - y = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\,}
$$
$$
\boxed{\,x = 2y - \frac{a}{2} = n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form of the Buswell Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical Buswell expression that the KTU examiner expects as a direct recall answer.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Methane Yield from Glucose (Application)

Glucose has formula $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$. Substitute $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$ into the Buswell formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \\
z &= \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3 \\
x &= 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the balanced reaction is:
$$
\boxed{\;\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 + 3\,\text{CO}_2\;}
$$

**Numerical conversion to volumetric yield:**
- Molar mass of glucose: $180 \text{ g/mol}$
- Moles of $\text{CH}_4$ per mole glucose: $3$
- At STP: $1 \text{ mol gas} = 22.4 \text{ L}$, so $3 \text{ mol} = 67.2 \text{ L CH}_4$
- Per kg glucose: $\dfrac{1000 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} \times 67.2 \text{ L/mol} = 373.3 \text{ L CH}_4 / \text{kg} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3 / \text{kg}$

$$
\boxed{\;Y_{CH_4,\,glucose} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is "VS" specific:** Volatile Solids (VS) represent the organic fraction of biomass. The same formula gives the methane yield from VS of any substrate by treating VS as if it were glucose-equivalent (using the substrate's elemental C, H, O composition).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric Ethanol Yield (Gay-Lussac → Pasteur)

The overall ethanol fermentation reaction (after glycolysis and pyruvate decarboxylation) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}}\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Molar mass balance:**
- 1 mol glucose = $180 \text{ g}$
- 2 mol ethanol = $2 \times 46 = 92 \text{ g}$

**Step 2 — Mass yield ratio:**
$$
Y = \frac{92 \text{ g EtOH}}{180 \text{ g Glucose}} = 0.511 \text{ g EtOH / g Glucose}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric form** (density of pure ethanol $\rho_{EtOH} = 0.789 \text{ kg/L}$):
$$
V = \frac{0.511 \text{ g/g} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg}}{789 \text{ g/L}} = 0.648 \text{ L/kg Glucose}
$$

But this is **ethanol-only**. For a typical fermentation broth at 10% w/v glucose consumed, the **distillation yield** is:
$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{theoretical} = 0.511 \text{ g EtOH / g glucose consumed}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical yield (accounting for cell mass ~ 5%):**
$$
\eta_{practical} = 0.90 \times 0.511 = 0.460 \text{ g/g} \approx 46\% \text{ w/w}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — Energy Recovery from Food Waste (14-mark style)

**Problem:** A canteen generates $500 \text{ kg/day}$ of food waste with $80\%$ moisture and $90\%$ VS (on dry basis). Calculate the daily biogas production in $\text{m}^3$, its $\text{CH}_4$ content, and the equivalent electrical energy output (generator efficiency = 35%).

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Daily VS available:**
$$
m_{VS} = 500 \times (1 - 0.80) \times 0.90 = 500 \times 0.20 \times 0.90 = 90 \text{ kg VS/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical $\text{CH}_4$ yield (assume Buswell-typical = 0.373 m³/kg VS, but food waste is less degradable, use 80% degradability):**
$$
V_{CH_4} = 90 \times 0.373 \times 0.80 = 26.86 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Biogas total volume (assume $\text{CH}_4$ fraction = 60%):**
$$
V_{biogas} = \frac{26.86}{0.60} = 44.77 \text{ m}^3 \text{ biogas/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy in methane:**
$$
E_{CH_4} = 26.86 \times 35.88 = 963.7 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Electrical output:**
$$
E_{elec} = 963.7 \times 0.35 = 337.3 \text{ MJ/day} = \frac{337.3}{3.6} = 93.7 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Daily output} \approx 94 \text{ kWh}_{e}\;}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Biochemical Conversion Routes (Master Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK] --> B{Feedstock Type}

```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Complex Polymers Proteins Lipids Carbohydrates] --> S1
    S1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis Cellulase Protease Lipase] --> P2
    P2[Monomers Sugars Amino Acids LCFA] --> S2
    S2[Stage 2 Acidogenesis Clostridium Bacteroides] --> P3
    P3[Volatile Fatty Acids Acetate Propionate Butyrate] --> S3
    S3[Stage 3 Acetogenesis Syntrophobacter Syntrophomonas] --> P4
    P4[Acetate plus H2 plus CO2] --> S4
    S4[Stage 4 Methanogenesis Methanosarcina Methanosaeta] --> P5

```

graph TD

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Biochemical vs Thermochemical

```mermaid
graph TD
    BM[Biomass] --> SPLIT{SConversion Pathway}
    SPLIT --> BIO[Biochemical Route]
    SPLIT --> THER[Thermochemical Route]
    BIO --> BIO1[Anaerobic Digestion]
    BIO --> BIO2[Fermentation]
    BIO --> BIO3[Composting]
    THER --> THER1[Combustion]
    THER --> THER2[Gasification]
    THER --> THER3[Pyrolysis]
    BIO1 --> OUT1[Output Biogas plus Digestate]
    BIO2 --> OUT2[Output Bioethanol plus CO2]
    BIO3 --> OUT3[Output Compost plus CO2]
    THER1 --> OUT4[Output Heat plus Ash]
    THER2 --> OUT5[Output Syngas]
    THER3 --> OUT6[Output Bio oil plus Char plus Gas]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The KTU examiner often asks students to *draw* or *describe* the difference between biochemical and thermochemical conversion. Memorize three differences:
> 1. **Temperature** — biochemical: ambient to 55 °C; thermochemical: 300–1200 °C.
> 2. **Reaction Time** — biochemical: days to weeks; thermochemical: seconds to minutes.
> 3. **Moisture Tolerance** — biochemical: loves moisture; thermochemical: needs drying.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define biochemical conversion. Differentiate it from thermochemical conversion.

**Model Answer:**

> **Biochemical conversion** is the controlled biological transformation of organic matter into useful energy products (biogas, bioethanol) by microorganisms (bacteria, archaea, yeast) operating in defined environmental conditions (T, pH, moisture).
>
> **Differences (3 marks worth — must list at least 3):**
>
> | Parameter | Biochemical | Thermochemical |
> |---|---|---|
> | Operating Temperature | 25–55 °C | 300–1200 °C |
> | Reaction Time | Days to weeks | Seconds to hours |
> | Moisture Tolerance | High (loves wet biomass) | Low (requires drying < 20%) |
> | Catalytic Agent | Microbial enzymes | Heat / catalyst / O₂ |
> | Output | Biogas, ethanol, compost | Heat, syngas, bio-oil, char |
> | Capital Cost | Moderate (CSTR) | High (reactors, gas cleaning) |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State and explain the Buswell Equation. Calculate the methane yield from 1 kg of acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$).

**Model Answer:**

The Buswell Equation gives the theoretical maximum $\text{CH}_4$ and $\text{CO}_2$ produced from any organic compound of formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b$:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2
$$

For acetic acid $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$: $n=2, a=4, b=2$.

$$
y = \frac{2}{2} - \frac{4}{8} + \frac{2}{4} = 1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1 \text{ mol CH}_4
$$

So $1 \text{ mol CH}_3\text{COOH}$ ($60 \text{ g}$) produces $1 \text{ mol CH}_4$ ($22.4 \text{ L at STP}$). Per kg:

$$
V_{CH_4} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{60 \text{ g/mol}} \times 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 373.3 \text{ L/kg} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion**. For each stage, write the substrate, the microbial group, the enzymes involved, and the products formed. Draw a labelled flow diagram.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A dairy produces $2000 \text{ kg/day}$ of cheese whey. The COD of whey is $60{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$ and density is $1.03 \text{ kg/L}$. Using a UASB reactor (OLR = $10 \text{ kg COD/m}^3 \cdot \text{day}$, 80% COD removal, $0.35 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg COD}_{removed}$), calculate (i) the daily volume of whey, (ii) the reactor volume needed, (iii) the daily methane production, and (iv) the energy equivalent in kWh.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step (i) — Daily whey volume:**
$$
\dot{V}_{whey} = \frac{2000 \text{ kg/day}}{1.03 \text{ kg/L}} = 1941.7 \text{ L/day} = 1.942 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step (ii) — Daily COD load:**
$$
\dot{m}_{COD} = 60{,}000 \text{ mg/L} \times 1941.7 \text{ L/day} = 1.165 \times 10^{11} \text{ mg/day} = 116.5 \text{ kg COD/day}
$$

**Step (iii) — Reactor volume:**
$$
V_{reactor} = \frac{\dot{m}_{COD}}{\text{OLR}} = \frac{116.5}{10} = 11.65 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step (iv) — Methane production:**
$$
V_{CH_4} = 116.5 \times 0.80 \times 0.35 = 32.62 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

**Step (v) — Energy:**
$$
E = 32.62 \times 35.88 = 1170.4 \text{ MJ/day} = 325.1 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Daily output} \approx 325 \text{ kWh}\;}
$$

### Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Buswell Equation** with derivation. Apply it to determine the theoretical methane yield from 1 kg of **stearic acid** ($\text{C}_{18}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2$).

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Write notes on (i) **aerobic composting** — phases and process parameters, (ii) **ethanol fermentation** — reaction stoichiometry, microbe, and theoretical yield. Compare biochemical vs thermochemical conversion.

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

For stearic acid $\text{C}_{18}\text{H}_{36}\text{O}_2$: $n = 18, a = 36, b = 2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{18}{2} - \frac{36}{8} + \frac{2}{4} = 9 - 4.5 + 0.5 = 5 \text{ mol CH}_4 \\
z &= \frac{18}{2} + \frac{36}{8} - \frac{2}{4} = 9 + 4.5 - 0.5 = 13 \text{ mol CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Molar mass of stearic acid = $284.5 \text{ g/mol}$. Moles per kg:
$$
n_{stearic} = \frac{1000}{284.5} = 3.515 \text{ mol}
$$

Methane volume per kg stearic acid:
$$
V_{CH_4} = 3.515 \times 5 \times 22.4 = 393.7 \text{ L/kg} \approx 0.394 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Y_{CH_4, \, stearic} = 0.394 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting atomic balance** — many students skip the C-balance step in the Buswell derivation. Always show all three balances (C, H, O).
> 2. **Unit mismatch in numericals** — converting mg/L × L/day to kg/day requires dividing by $10^6$. Common error: forgetting the factor of $10^6$.
> 3. **Confusing "kg" vs "kg VS"** — methane yield formula uses **Volatile Solids**, not total solids. Apply moisture and VS fractions *first*.
> 4. **Wrong microbe name** — it is *Methanosarcina* (aceticlastic), not *Methanococcus* (which is hydrogenotrophic).
> 5. **Missing the C/N ratio** in the composting answer — must mention the optimum range **20–30** for active composting.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Biochemical conversion = microbial transformation of biomass into fuels/products. Three pillars: **Anaerobic Digestion, Fermentation, Composting**.
- **Anaerobic Digestion — 4 stages:** **H**ydrolysis → **A**cidogenesis → **A**cetogenesis → **M**ethanogenesis (mnemonic: **HAAM**).
- **Rate-limiting stage:** Hydrolysis (for lignocellulose) and Acetogenesis (for propionate).
- **Methanogenic microbes:** *Methanosarcina* (aceticlastic, dominant), *Methanobacterium* (hydrogenotrophic).
- **Buswell Equation:** Master formula for theoretical $\text{CH}_4$ yield from any $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b$.
- **Key Constant:** $0.373 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$ (glucose) — multiply by **degradability factor** for real feedstocks.
- **Energy Content:** $\text{LHV}_{CH_4} = 35.88 \text{ MJ/m}^3$.
- **Optimum AD Conditions:** $T = 35\text{–}40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (mesophilic), $pH = 6.5\text{–}7.5$, $\text{C/N} = 20\text{–}30$, $\text{HRT} = 15\text{–}30 \text{ days}$.
- **Composting Phases:** Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Curing. Final product is **humus**.
- **Ethanol Fermentation:** $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$; theoretical yield = **0.511 g/g glucose**; practical ~ 0.46 g/g.
- **Bio vs Thermo (3 differences):** Temperature (low vs high), Time (slow vs fast), Moisture (high vs low).
- **Two Methanogenic Pathways:** Aceticlastic (70%) + Hydrogenotrophic (30%).
- **Syntrophic IHT:** Acetogenesis needs low $\text{H}_2$ partial pressure; methanogens "pull" the reaction forward.
- **KTU Numerical Hot-Spots:** (i) Buswell application to a new organic compound, (ii) UASB/CSTR sizing with OLR and HRT, (iii) Energy-from-methane calculation.
- **Real-World Kerala Context:** KVIC biogas plants, Brahmapuram waste-to-energy, E20 blending policy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Environmental Issues: Resource Degradation: Deterioration of Water Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Global Environmental Issues: Resource Degradation: Deterioration of Water Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Resource Deterioration** refers to the progressive decline in the *quantity* (scarcity, depletion) and *quality* (pollution, contamination, salinization) of available freshwater resources, rendering them unfit for intended human, ecological, or industrial uses. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, it encompasses the **physical, chemical, biological, and ecological degradation** of surface water (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and groundwater (aquifers) caused by anthropogenic stressors and natural geochemical processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> "Resource Degradation – Deterioration of Water Resources" is treated as a *qualitative and quantitative* problem, linking global drivers (population, industrialization, agriculture) to specific *symptoms* (eutrophication, salinization, heavy metal contamination, groundwater mining).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Family Bank Account of Planet Earth"**
> Imagine Earth has only **one freshwater bank account** shared by **8+ billion people**. The *deposits* are rainfall, snowmelt, and river inflow. The *withdrawals* are agriculture, industry, and households. Now, on top of the overdraft, we are *throwing garbage into the account itself* (pollution). This is exactly **water resource deterioration** — running a deficit (scarcity) *and* contaminating the remaining balance (quality loss).

### 1.3 Key Categories of Water Deterioration

1. **Quantitative Depletion (Scarcity)**
   - Over-extraction of rivers beyond natural replenishment
   - Groundwater mining (extraction >> recharge)
   - Climate change altering precipitation patterns
2. **Qualitative Degradation (Pollution)**
   - **Physical**: Thermal pollution, suspended solids, turbidity
   - **Chemical**: Heavy metals, pesticides, nutrients (N, P), pH shifts
   - **Biological**: Pathogens (E. coli, *Vibrio cholerae*), algal blooms
3. **Geogenic / Induced Degradation**
   - Salinization, fluoride/arsenic contamination from natural + induced pathways

### 1.4 Critical Global Metrics (as per UN/UN-Water 2024 framing)

- Global freshwater availability ≈ **2.5% of total water**; of this, only ≈ **0.3% is accessible** in rivers, lakes, and shallow aquifers.
- **2 billion people** lack safely managed drinking water (UN SDG 6 baseline).
- **80% of global wastewater** is discharged untreated (UN-Water).
- Agriculture consumes ≈ **70%** of global freshwater withdrawals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> **Water Stress Index (WSI)** = (Total Freshwater Withdrawal) / (Total Renewable Freshwater Resources) × 100.
> A country with **WSI > 25%** is considered *water-stressed*; **> 40%** is *severely water-stressed*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Triple-Whammy" Framework (Why & How)

The deterioration of water resources is driven by three mutually reinforcing pressures:

| Driver | Why It Happens | How It Degrades Water | Engineering/Ecological Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Population Growth** | More people → more withdrawal | Increased sewage volume, untreated discharge | Eutrophication, pathogen load |
| **Industrialization** | Demand for cheap cooling & process water | Discharge of heavy metals, acids, dyes, heat | Thermal pollution, bioaccumulation, acid mine drainage |
| **Intensive Agriculture** | Green Revolution → fertilizer/pesticide dependence | Nutrient (N, P) runoff, pesticide leaching | Algal blooms, dead zones, groundwater NO₃⁻ contamination |

### 2.2 The Water Quality Cascade (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Pollutant enters source** (river/lake/aquifer) via point or non-point source.
2. **Physical/Chemical changes** alter pH, DO, turbidity, TDS — making water unfit.
3. **Biological response** — sensitive species die; tolerant species (algae, *E. coli*) proliferate.
4. **Bioaccumulation** — heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd) move up the food chain (*biomagnification*).
5. **Ecosystem collapse** — dead zones, fish kills, loss of biodiversity; loss of *ecosystem services*.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used inside the table below. Absolute values use `\vert` and "such that" uses `\mid` to protect markdown syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) | $BOD_5 = DO_i - DO_f$ | mg/L; 5-day standard test at 20°C |
| 2 | Dilution BOD (for strong wastes) | $BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) - DF \times (DO_i - DO_f)_{dilution\ water}$ | DF = Dilution Factor (decimal) |
| 3 | BOD Reaction Rate (1st order) | $BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | $L_0$ = Ultimate BOD (mg/L); $k$ = deoxygenation constant (day⁻¹); $t$ = time |
| 4 | Streeter–Phelps DO Sag | $D_t = \dfrac{k L_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{-k t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0\,e^{-k_2 t}$ | $D_t$ = oxygen deficit (mg/L); $k_2$ = reaeration constant (day⁻¹) |
| 5 | Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | $TH = 2.5 \left[Ca^{2+}\right] + 4.12 \left[Mg^{2+}\right]$ | All concentrations in mg/L; Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ as the element |
| 6 | Water Stress Index (WSI) | $WSI = \dfrac{\text{Total Withdrawal}}{\text{Total Renewable}} \times 100$ | % — threshold 25% / 40% |
| 7 | Per Capita Water Demand | $q = \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | $Q$ = demand (L/day), $P$ = population |
| 8 | Population Forecast (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}$ | $r$ = decadal growth rate % |
| 9 | Water Footprint (Hoekstra) | $WF_{total} = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}$ | L/kg of product |
| 10 | Carbonate vs Non-carbonate Hardness | $Non-Carbonate\ H. = Total\ H. - Alkalinity$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 11 | BOD/COD Ratio | $Ratio = BOD_5 / COD$ | Indicates biodegradability (>0.5 = biodegradable) |
| 12 | Safe Yield of Aquifer | $Y = P - D$ | $P$ = recharge, $D$ = draft (m³/yr); if negative → mining |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil/Environmental Engineering**: BOD and Streeter–Phelps equations are used in the **design of municipal sewage treatment plants** (STP) and in predicting *dissolved oxygen* levels downstream of effluent outfalls (e.g., Ganga, Yamuna).
- **Water Supply Engineering**: Hardness formulas guide the design of **softening units** (ion-exchange, lime-soda) for boiler feed water and drinking water.
- **Hydrology/Irrigation**: Population forecasts determine *reservoir capacity* using mass-curve analysis; safe yield governs *tube-well density* in command areas.
- **Sustainability (SDG 6)**: Water Footprint links **industrial supply chains** to virtual-water trade; a B.Tech graduate evaluating product lifecycle must use this metric.

> [!TIP]
> **Most-tested topic in KTU ESE:** *BOD / DO / Hardness numericals + a 7-mark descriptive on Eutrophication or Salinization.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: BOD Reaction Kinetics (First-Order)

We start from the principle that microbial oxidation of organic matter follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining oxygen-demanding substrate.

**Step 1: Define the rate of BOD exertion**

$$
\dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k\,L_t
$$

where $L_t$ is the BOD remaining to be exerted at time $t$ (mg/L), and $k$ is the deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹).

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate from 0 to $t$**

$$
\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3: Perform the integration**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k t
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $L_t$**

$$
L_t = L_0\, e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 5: Define the BOD exerted up to time $t$ as $\left(L_0 - L_t\right)$**

$$
BOD_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0\left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)
$$

**Step 6: For the standard 5-day test, the formula becomes**

$$
BOD_5 = L_0\left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)
$$

This is the **KTU-standard** BOD formula used in every numerical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Streeter–Phelps Oxygen Sag Equation

The DO sag in a river downstream of a discharge point is governed by **two competing processes**:
- *Deoxygenation* due to BOD exertion (rate constant $k$)
- *Reaeration* from the atmosphere (rate constant $k_2$)

**Step 1: Write the net rate of change of oxygen deficit $D$**

$$
\dfrac{dD}{dt} = k\,L_t - k_2\,D_t
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $L_t = L_0\, e^{-k t}$ from Derivation 1**

$$
\dfrac{dD}{dt} + k_2 D_t = k\,L_0\, e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 3: This is a first-order linear ODE. The integrating factor is $e^{k_2 t}$**

$$
\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(D_t\, e^{k_2 t}\right) = k\,L_0\, e^{(k_2 - k)t}
$$

**Step 4: Integrate from 0 to $t$ (with initial deficit $D_0$)**

$$
D_t\,e^{k_2 t} - D_0 = k L_0 \int_0^t e^{(k_2 - k)\tau}\, d\tau
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the integral**

$$
D_t\,e^{k_2 t} = D_0 + \dfrac{k L_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{(k_2 - k)t} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $D_t$**

$$
D_t = \dfrac{k L_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{-k t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0\,e^{-k_2 t}
$$

This is the **Streeter–Phelps Equation** — directly used in KTU numericals to find the *critical deficit* and *critical time* $t_c$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical 1: BOD Calculation (Typical KTU Pattern)

> A wastewater sample is diluted by a factor of 1:100. Initial DO of diluted sample = 8.0 mg/L. DO after 5 days = 4.0 mg/L. Initial DO of dilution water = 9.0 mg/L. Find $BOD_5$ of the original wastewater.

**Step 1: Identify the BOD of the *diluted* sample**

$$
BOD_{5,diluted} = DO_i - DO_f = 8.0 - 4.0 = 4.0\ mg/L
$$

**Step 2: Apply the dilution BOD formula**

$$
BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) - DF \times (DO_i - DO_f)_{dilution\ water}
$$

**Step 3: The BOD of dilution water itself**

$$
BOD_{dilution} = 9.0 - 4.0 = 5.0\ mg/L \text{ (approx — but typically considered 0 for fresh water; here use given data)}
$$

Using the standard 5.0 - 4.0 interpretation is incorrect; the correct interpretation is that *dilution water's own oxygen drop* over 5 days must be subtracted:

Assume dilution water DO drop over 5 days = $9.0 - 8.5 = 0.5$ mg/L (typical; given problem implies negligible → use directly):

$$
BOD_5 = 4.0 - (0.01)(0.5) = 4.0 - 0.005 \approx 3.995\ mg/L \ (\text{diluted})
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by Dilution Factor (DF = 100) to get original strength**

$$
BOD_{5,original} = 3.995 \times 100 \approx 400\ mg/L
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, the cleaner expression is $BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) \times DF$ when dilution water BOD is neglected. $BOD_5 = 4.0 \times 100 = 400$ mg/L.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical 2: Total Hardness (Asking for Calcium + Magnesium)

> A water sample contains Ca²⁺ = 40 mg/L and Mg²⁺ = 12 mg/L. Compute total hardness (as CaCO₃) and classify it.

**Step 1: Apply the hardness formula**

$$
TH = 2.5 \times \left[Ca^{2+}\right] + 4.12 \times \left[Mg^{2+}\right]
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
TH = 2.5 \times 40 + 4.12 \times 12
$$

**Step 3: Multiply each term**

$$
TH = 100.0 + 49.44
$$

**Step 4: Add**

$$
TH = 149.44\ mg/L\ \text{as CaCO}_3
$$

**Step 5: Classify (BIS / WHO Standard)**

| Range (mg/L) | Classification |
|---|---|
| 0 – 60 | Soft |
| 61 – 120 | Moderately hard |
| 121 – 180 | Hard |
| > 180 | Very hard |

→ **149.44 mg/L → "Hard" water** → softening treatment (lime-soda / ion exchange) is required.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical 3: Water Footprint of a Product

> Producing 1 kg of rice requires 1500 L of green water, 300 L of blue water, and 200 L of grey water. Compute the *total water footprint* of rice.

**Step 1: Apply the Hoekstra equation**

$$
WF_{total} = WF_{blue} + WF_{green} + WF_{grey}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$
WF_{total} = 300 + 1500 + 200
$$

**Step 3: Result**

$$
WF_{total} = 2000\ L/kg\ \text{of rice}
$$

This is consistent with the globally reported value (~2000–2500 L/kg for rice).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Symptom–Cause–Mitigation

| Symptom | Primary Cause | Sector Responsible | Sustainable Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eutrophication** | Excess N, P runoff | Agriculture (fertilizer), sewage | Constructed wetlands, riparian buffers, precision fertigation |
| **Groundwater Mining** | Over-pumping of aquifers | Agriculture, industry | Managed Aquifer Recharge (MAR), drip irrigation |
| **Salinization** | Sea-water ingress + irrigation salts | Coastal + semi-arid farming | Salt-tolerant crops, sub-surface drainage |
| **Heavy Metal Toxicity** | Industrial effluents, mining | Tannery, electroplating | Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD), phytoremediation |
| **Fluoride / Arsenic** | Geogenic + over-extraction | Drinking water supply | Defluoridation units, As-removal filters |
| **Thermal Pollution** | Cooling water from power plants | Power sector | Cooling towers, closed-loop cooling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Drivers → Impacts → Consequences (Cause–Effect Chain)

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
*Define the term "Water Stress Index" (WSI). State the threshold values for a region to be classified as water-stressed and severely water-stressed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Water Stress Index (WSI)** is the ratio of total freshwater withdrawal to total renewable freshwater resources, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$
> WSI = \dfrac{\text{Total Freshwater Withdrawal}}{\text{Total Renewable Freshwater Resources}} \times 100
> $$
>
> **Threshold Classification (UN/UNEP convention):**
> - WSI < 10% → Low water stress
> - 10–25% → Low to moderate
> - **> 25% → Water-stressed region**
> - **> 40% → Severely water-stressed region**
> - > 80% → Extremely water-stressed (essentially mining of water)
>
> A WSI > 25% indicates that demand is approaching the renewable limit; > 40% is a serious sustainability red flag (e.g., India, Middle East).
> *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – formula, 1 mark – thresholds with examples)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
*Differentiate between "blue water footprint" and "grey water footprint" with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Blue Water Footprint | Grey Water Footprint |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Volume of *surface* and *groundwater* consumed (evaporated or incorporated into a product) | Volume of freshwater required to *assimilate* pollutants to meet ambient water-quality standards |
| **Source** | Rivers, lakes, aquifers (fresh) | The same — but used as a *dilution sink* |
| **Example** | Irrigation pumping from a canal for wheat | Freshwater needed to dilute fertilizer-laden runoff in a river to acceptable NO₃⁻ levels |
| **Indicator of** | *Scarcity pressure* on quantity | *Pollution pressure* on quality |

> *(1.5 marks for definitions, 1.5 marks for example + distinction)*

---

### 5.2 PART B (14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Explain the phenomenon of **eutrophication** in lakes and reservoirs. Discuss its causes, stages, and consequences on water quality. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of a water body with nutrients (mainly nitrogen and phosphorus), leading to accelerated growth of algae and subsequent deterioration of water quality and ecosystem health.

**2. Causes (2 marks):**
- *Anthropogenic*: agricultural fertilizer runoff, domestic sewage (detergents contain phosphates), industrial effluents, aquaculture waste.
- *Natural (Cultural Eutrophication)*: the human-driven acceleration of a naturally slow process.

**3. Stages (2 marks):**
1. *Oligotrophic* — clear, low-nutrient, high DO, diverse fish.
2. *Mesotrophic* — moderate nutrients, plant growth begins.
3. *Eutrophic* — high nutrients, algal blooms, low DO, fish kills.
4. *Hypertrophic* — extreme blooms, scum, anoxic bottom, mass mortality.

**4. Consequences (2 marks):**
- Reduced dissolved oxygen → fish kills.
- Algal toxins (microcystins) → unsafe drinking water.
- Loss of biodiversity, dead zones, foul odours (H₂S).
- Clogging of water-treatment filters; high BOD/COD.

**5. Control:** Constructed wetlands, riparian buffer strips, phosphate-free detergents, advanced wastewater treatment, precision agriculture.

---

**Q. (b)** A town has a present population of 50,000 with an average water demand of 135 Lpcd. Assuming a geometric growth rate of **2.5% per decade**, estimate:
1. The population after 20 years.
2. The total water demand (in MLD) for the design year. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the data**
- $P_0 = 50{,}000$, $r = 2.5\%$ per decade, $n = 20$ years = 2 decades.
- Per capita demand $q = 135$ Lpcd.

**Step 2: Apply the geometric growth formula**

$$
P_n = P_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
P_{20} = 50000 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{2.5}{100}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4: Compute**

$$
P_{20} = 50000 \times (1.025)^{2} = 50000 \times 1.050625
$$

**Step 5: Result**

$$
P_{20} = 52{,}531\ (\text{approx})
$$

**Step 6: Compute total daily demand**

$$
Q = P_n \times q = 52531 \times 135\ L/day
$$

**Step 7: Convert to MLD (Million Litres per Day)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{52531 \times 135}{10^6} = \dfrac{7{,}091{,}685}{10^6} \approx 7.09\ MLD
$$

*(1 mark – data identification, 2 marks – population forecast, 2 marks – demand calculation, 2 marks – units and final answer)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Describe the major **types and sources of water pollution** with suitable examples. Add a note on **thermal pollution** from power plants. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Classification (3 marks):**
- **Point-source pollution** — discharge at a definite location (e.g., a pipe from a factory). Easier to monitor and regulate.
- **Non-point (diffuse) pollution** — from multiple, spread-out sources (e.g., agricultural runoff, urban stormwater). Harder to control.

**2. Types of Pollutants (2 marks):**
- *Biological*: pathogens (E. coli, *Salmonella*, *Vibrio cholerae*) from sewage.
- *Chemical*: heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr⁶⁺), pesticides, hydrocarbons.
- *Physical*: suspended solids, thermal discharge, oil, plastics.
- *Nutrients*: nitrates, phosphates → eutrophication.

**3. Thermal Pollution (2 marks):**
Power plants use river/reservoir water for once-through cooling. The heated water, returned to the source, has:
- Lower DO solubility → aquatic stress.
- Accelerated metabolic rates of fish → oxygen demand exceeds supply.
- Thermal shock kills sensitive species.
- *Mitigation*: cooling towers, cooling ponds, closed-loop systems.

---

**Q. (b)** The initial dissolved oxygen (DO) of a river is **8.5 mg/L**. A municipal discharge adds an organic load causing an ultimate BOD $L_0$ of **40 mg/L**. The deoxygenation constant $k = 0.25$/day and the reaeration constant $k_2 = 0.40$/day. The initial oxygen deficit $D_0$ is **1.5 mg/L**. Calculate:
1. The oxygen deficit at the **critical point** ($D_c$).
2. The **critical time** ($t_c$). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall the Streeter–Phelps equation**

$$
D_t = \dfrac{k L_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{-k t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0\,e^{-k_2 t}
$$

**Step 2: Find the critical time $t_c$ by setting $\dfrac{dD}{dt} = 0$**

$$
\dfrac{dD}{dt} = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ e^{(k_2 - k)t_c} = \dfrac{k_2}{k} \left(1 - D_0 \dfrac{k_2 - k}{k L_0}\right)
$$

(For simplicity in KTU problems, when $D_0$ is small compared to $kL_0/(k_2 - k)$, we use the simplified formula.)

**Step 3: Apply the simplified formula for $t_c$ (when $D_0 \approx 0$)**

$$
t_c = \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Substitute values**

$$
t_c = \dfrac{1}{0.40 - 0.25}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.40}{0.25}\right) = \dfrac{1}{0.15}\ln(1.6)
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate**

$$
t_c = \dfrac{0.4700}{0.15} \approx 3.13\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 6: Find $D_c$ using the critical-point formula**

$$
D_c = \dfrac{k L_0}{k_2}\,e^{-k t_c}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute**

$$
D_c = \dfrac{0.25 \times 40}{0.40}\,e^{-0.25 \times 3.13}
$$

**Step 8: Compute**

$$
D_c = 25 \times e^{-0.7825} = 25 \times 0.4573 \approx 11.43\ mg/L
$$

**Step 9: Critical DO at that point**

$$
DO_c = DO_{sat} - D_c \approx 8.5 - 11.43 \ \text{(negative, indicating anoxia!)}
$$

This means the discharge is **lethal** to the river — the river will go anoxic at the critical point, about 3.13 days downstream. Design a regional STP before discharge.

*(1 mark – formula recall, 2 marks – $t_c$ derivation, 2 marks – $D_c$ evaluation, 2 marks – interpretation and design recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Hardness units confusion:** Express Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ *in mg/L as the element*, not as CaCO₃. Many students wrongly divide by 2.5 and 4.12 instead of multiplying — leading to a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **BOD dilution numericals:** Always state the **dilution factor as a decimal** ($1/100 = 0.01$), not as a percentage. A common error: using $BOD_5 = DO_i - DO_f$ *without* multiplying by DF.
> 3. **Streeter–Phelps formula:** Examiners deduct heavily if the **simplification assumption** ($D_0 \approx 0$) is not stated explicitly.
> 4. **Descriptive answers on Eutrophication:** Students list causes but forget the *stages (Oligotrophic → Eutrophic → Hypertrophic)* — losing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Water Footprint:** Many confuse *green water* (rain) with *blue water* (surface/ground). Use a quick 1-line definition in the answer.
