# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: List any four environmental disadvantages of conventional energy sources.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each Point + 0.5 for Classification):**

The environmental disadvantages of conventional (fossil-fuel based) energy sources are:

1. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions** — Combustion of coal, oil, and natural gas releases CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O, contributing to global warming and climate change. *(1 mark)*

2. **Air Pollution** — Emission of SO₂, NOₓ, and particulate matter (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀) leads to smog, acid rain, and respiratory diseases such as asthma and bronchitis. *(1 mark)*

3. **Water Pollution** — Thermal discharge from cooling water and acid mine drainage contaminate rivers and groundwater, harming aquatic ecosystems. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Land Degradation** — Open-cast mining for coal causes deforestation, soil erosion, and loss of biodiversity. *(0.5 mark)*

> **Classification line (1 mark awarded separately):** These disadvantages fall under the **Environmental Pillar** of the Triple Bottom Line.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What are the socio-economic disadvantages of large hydropower projects?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Displacement & Rehabilitation Issues** — Large dams submerge vast land areas, displacing tribal and rural communities without adequate rehabilitation. *(1 mark)*

2. **High Capital Investment** — Hydropower projects have very high upfront CAPEX (₹5–10 crore/MW) and long gestation periods. *(1 mark)*

3. **Ecological Disruption** — Fragmentation of river systems affects fish migration, sediment flow, and downstream agriculture. *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Seismic Risk** — Reservoir-induced seismicity (RIS) is a documented disadvantage in tectonically sensitive regions like the Himalayas. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: Discuss in detail the various disadvantages of conventional energy sources. Evaluate their impact on environmental sustainability with suitable examples and metrics. (14 Marks)**

**OR**

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice

**Q: "Every engineering system has trade-offs." Examine this statement with reference to the disadvantages of renewable energy systems and their impact on grid stability. (14 Marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION — Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — Classification of Disadvantages (7 Marks)**

The disadvantages of conventional energy sources can be classified into **four major categories**:

**(i) Environmental Disadvantages (3 Marks)**

1. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions** — Combustion of coal, oil, and natural gas releases CO₂, CH₄, N₂O. A 1000 MW coal plant emits ~7.4 million tonnes of CO₂ per year. *(1 mark)*

2. **Air Pollution** — Release of SO₂ (causing acid rain), NOₓ (smog), PM₂.₅ (respiratory disease), and Hg (neurotoxin). WHO estimates 7 million premature deaths/year from air pollution. *(1 mark)*

3. **Water & Soil Contamination** — Thermal pollution from cooling water; acid mine drainage from coal mines contaminates groundwater with heavy metals (Fe, Mn, As). *(1 mark)*

**(ii) Economic Disadvantages (2 Marks)**

4. **Externalized Costs** — Health costs, environmental cleanup costs, and climate damages are not priced into fossil fuel market value. IMF estimates global fossil fuel subsidies at **$7 trillion/year (2022)** including unpriced environmental costs.

5. **Stranded Asset Risk** — As climate policy tightens, fossil fuel infrastructure (plants, pipelines, refineries) loses economic value, leading to write-downs.

**(iii) Social Disadvantages (1 Mark)**

6. **Public Health Burden** — Communities near power plants suffer disproportionately (environmental injustice). Coal miners face pneumoconiosis ("black lung disease").

**(iv) Resource / Systemic Disadvantages (1 Mark)**

7. **Non-Renewable Depletion** — Oil, coal, and gas are finite. Hubbert's peak analysis suggests conventional oil peaks within decades, making long-term reliance unsustainable.

---

**Part (b) — Impact Assessment & Metrics (7 Marks)**

**(i) Carbon Footprint Comparison (3 Marks)**

| Source | CO₂ (g/kWh) | PM₂.₅ (mg/m³) | Water (L/MWh) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coal | 820–1000 | High | 1100 |
| Oil | 650–750 | Medium | 800 |
| Natural Gas | 350–490 | Low | 500 |
| Solar PV | 40–50 | None | 26 |
| Wind | 10–30 | None | 1–2 |

**(ii) Worked Numerical Example (2 Marks)**

Annual CO₂ from a 500 MW coal plant at $\eta = 38\%$, $CF = 80\%$, $GCV = 4500$ kcal/kg, $C_{frac} = 0.55$:

$$E_{annual} = 500 \times 10^3 \times 0.80 \times 8760 = 3.504 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}$$

$$Q_{thermal} = \frac{3.504 \times 10^9}{0.38} = 9.22 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh} = 3.32 \times 10^{13} \text{ kJ}$$

$$m_{coal} = \frac{3.32 \times 10^{13}}{4500 \times 4.184} = 1.76 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 1.76 \times 10^9 \times 0.55 \times 3.667 \approx 3.55 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}$$

**Annual emissions ≈ 3.55 million tonnes CO₂.**

**(iii) SDG Mapping (2 Marks)**

| Disadvantage | Primary SDG Affected |
|---|---|
| GHG Emissions | SDG 13 — Climate Action |
| Air Pollution | SDG 3 — Good Health; SDG 11 — Sustainable Cities |
| Water Pollution | SDG 6 — Clean Water; SDG 14 — Life Below Water |
| Resource Depletion | SDG 12 — Responsible Consumption |

---

#### SOLUTION — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Part (a) — Disadvantages of Renewable Energy Systems (7 Marks)**

Although renewable energy is sustainable, it carries significant disadvantages:

1. **Intermittency** (2 Marks) — Solar and wind are weather-dependent. Capacity Factor: Solar = 15–25%, Wind = 25–40%. This means a 100 MW solar plant delivers only ~20 MW on average.

2. **Low Energy Density** (1 Mark) — Solar irradiance ≈ 1000 W/m² peak; wind power density ≈ 0.5 kW/m² at good sites. Requires vast land areas.

3. **Storage Dependence** (1.5 Marks) — Requires battery storage ($E_{storage} = P_{load} \times T_{autonomy}$). Current lithium-ion cost ≈ ₹4,00,000/kWh, a major CAPEX burden.

4. **Grid Stability Issues** (1.5 Marks) — Inverter-based renewables lack **synchronous inertia**, leading to frequency fluctuations. Requires grid-forming inverters and STATCOMs.

5. **Embodied Energy & E-Waste** (1 Mark) — Solar panel manufacturing has embodied energy (~500 kWh/m²); end-of-life recycling is challenging (only ~17% global e-waste is recycled).

---

**Part (b) — Impact on Grid Stability & Engineering Solutions (7 Marks)**

| Grid Challenge | Cause | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Deviation | Variable renewable input | Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), flywheels |
| Voltage Fluctuation | Cloud passage on solar | Reactive power compensators (STATCOMs) |
| Inertia Loss | No rotating mass in PV/wind | Synthetic inertia from grid-forming inverters |
| Duck Curve | Solar peak vs. evening demand peak | Demand response, pumped hydro storage |
| Forecasting Error | Weather unpredictability | AI/ML-based load and renewable forecasting |

**Real-World Case Study (2 Marks):** The **Tamil Nadu wind energy corridor** (Muppandal, 4500+ MW installed) experienced grid frequency drop from 50 Hz to 49.5 Hz during high wind periods (2017), demonstrating the intermittency disadvantage firsthand. The solution involved TANGEDCO installing STATCOMs and Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Disadvantages" Questions**
>
> 1. ❌ **Generic answers**: Writing "pollution" without specifying *type* (air/water/noise) and *metric* loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. ❌ **No classification**: Failing to group disadvantages into Environmental/Economic/Social/Technical pillars forfeits the "structured answer" mark.
>
> 3. ❌ **Missing numerical example**: A 14-mark question demands at least **one quantitative illustration**. Pure qualitative answers cap at ~10 marks.
>
> 4. ❌ **No SDG/Mitigation link**: Examiners award bonus marks for connecting disadvantages to **UN SDGs or mitigation strategies**.
>
> 5. ❌ **Ignoring renewables' disadvantages**: Many students wrongly list *only* fossil fuel disadvantages. KTU expects **balanced analysis** of *both* conventional and renewable systems.
>
> 6. ❌ **No units in formulas**: Always include units; missing units = -0.5 to -1 mark per formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Disadvantages" Module 3**

- **Definition:** Disadvantages = measurable/qualitative drawbacks compromising the **Triple Bottom Line** (People, Planet, Profit).

- **Five Pillars:** Environmental, Economic, Social, Technical, Resource/Systemic.

- **Key Metric to Memorize:** 1000 MW coal plant ≈ **7.4 MtCO₂/year**.

- **Carbon Conversion Ratio:** $m_{CO_2} = m_C \times \dfrac{44}{12} = m_C \times 3.667$.

- **Intermittency KPIs:** Solar CF = 15–25%; Wind CF = 25–40%.

- **LCOE (2024):** Coal ≈ \$65–150/MWh; Solar ≈ \$30–60/MWh; Wind ≈ \$30–70/MWh.

- **Hubbert's Peak:** Logistic curve describes finite-resource depletion; production irreversibly declines post-peak.

- **SDG Mapping is Mandatory:** Always link each disadvantage to at least one UN SDG (especially **SDG 3, 6, 7, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15**).

- **Renewables Have Disadvantages Too:** Intermittency, storage cost, embodied energy, e-waste, land use — must be balanced in any answer.

- **Efficiency Benchmark:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_c/T_h$; real-world coal plants operate at 33–45% (50% energy wasted).

- **Circular Economy Counter:** Every disadvantage is mitigated by the **5R Principle** (Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Rot).

- **KTU-Exam Framework:** Every 14-mark disadvantage answer should have:
  1. Classification (1 mark)
  2. 3–4 disadvantages with metrics (6 marks)
  3. One worked numerical example (3 marks)
  4. SDG/mitigation link (2 marks)
  5. Conclusion + solution pathway (2 marks)

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Quantify every disadvantage; classify every disadvantage; mitigate every disadvantage."* This is the KTU Board Examiner's expectation in 2024–2025.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Case Study** in the context of Sustainability in Engineering is a systematic, empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary sustainability-focused engineering project, system, or intervention within its real-life context. Following the methodology defined by **Robert K. Yin (2018)** and adapted into the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, a sustainability case study must holistically analyse the **triple bottom line (TBL)** dimensions — *People, Planet, Profit* — using both qualitative narrative and quantitative performance metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *“A case study is a descriptive and analytical evaluation of a real-world engineering system that demonstrates the application of sustainability principles — including energy efficiency, renewable integration, circular economy practices, and life-cycle assessment — to derive transferable engineering lessons.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patient Diagnosis" Model

Think of a sustainability case study exactly like a **doctor diagnosing a patient**:
- **Symptoms** → Environmental impact indicators (e.g., high $CO_2$ emissions, excessive water withdrawal).
- **Medical History** → Baseline audit of the plant/building/system.
- **Diagnostic Tests** → Tools like Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), carbon footprinting, Energy Return on Investment (EROI).
- **Prescription** → Sustainable interventions (PV arrays, rainwater harvesting, waste-heat recovery).
- **Follow-up** → Post-implementation KPI tracking and benchmarking.

Just as no two patients are identical, every engineering case study presents a unique *site-specific* combination of climate, technology, economy, and culture that must be interpreted with the **Triple Bottom Line** lens.

## 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> - **End-Semester (ESE) Weightage:** Module 3 contributes a typical **15–20%** of the 100-mark ESE paper, with Case Study questions carrying 7–14 marks.
> - **Bloom's Level Tested:** **L4 (Analyse) and L5 (Evaluate)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.
> - **Required Output:** Students must *interpret* data, *justify* the sustainability choice, and *recommend* improvements.

## 1.4 Standard Sustainability Metrics to Memorise

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Embodied Energy | $EE$ | **MJ/kg** or **GJ/m²** | Concrete ≈ **1.5 GJ/m³** |
| Operational Carbon | $OC$ | **kg CO₂e/m²·yr** | Green building target < **50** |
| Energy Use Intensity | $EUI$ | **kWh/m²·yr** | Net-Zero target ≈ **0** |
| Water Use Intensity | $WUI$ | **L/person·day** | Best-in-class < **100** |
| Indoor $\text{CO}_2$ Level | $C_{in}$ | **ppm** | ASHRAE max = **1000 ppm** |
| Global Warming Potential | $GWP$ | **kg CO₂e** | Baseline 100-yr |
| Diversion from Landfill | $D_f$ | **%** | Zero-waste target = **100%** |
| Solar Payback Period | $T_{pb}$ | **years** | PV ≈ **2–4 yrs** |
| ROI | $r$ | **%** | Green retrofit ≈ **8–12%** |
| SRI (Solar Reflectance Index) | $SRI$ | **dimensionless** | Cool roof > **78** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded constants are KTU-favourite numbers.** Memorising them is non-negotiable for Part-A 3-mark short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Case Study Analysis Framework (KTU 2024 Module-3 Standard)

Every KTU-graded sustainability case study must be evaluated through the following five rigorous phases. Examiners specifically look for this **structured progression** in 14-mark answers.

### Phase 1 — **Context & Boundary Definition**
- Define the **functional unit (FU)** for LCA.
- Specify **geographic, climatic, temporal, and regulatory boundaries**.
- Identify all **stakeholders** (community, industry, regulators).

### Phase 2 — **Baseline Data Acquisition (As-Is Audit)**
- Compile pre-intervention $CO_2$ emissions, energy, water, and waste metrics.
- Establish the **reference flow** $R_f$ (kg/yr, m³/yr, kWh/yr).

### Phase 3 — **Intervention Catalogue & Engineering Mapping**
- Map each sustainable intervention to the **SDG (Sustainable Development Goals)** it supports.
- Common interventions: **Rooftop PV, BIPV (Building-Integrated PV), greywater recycling, trigeneration, green roofs, cool roofs, fly-ash bricks, rainwater harvesting, IoT-based energy management, EV charging infrastructure**.

### Phase 4 — **Performance Quantification**
Apply the formulae in §2.2 to compute savings, payback, and LCA.

### Phase 5 — **Critical Evaluation & Recommendation**
- Use **SWOT** (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats).
- Discuss **scalability**, **replicability**, and **transferability** to the Indian/Kerala context.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Full)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Annual Energy Generation (PV) | $E_{PV} = P_{peak} \cdot PSH \cdot \eta_{sys} \cdot (1 - d)^{n}$ | $P_{peak}$: PV rated power, $PSH$: Peak Sun Hours, $\eta_{sys}$: system efficiency, $d$: degradation, $n$: years | kWh/yr | Kerala $PSH \approx 4.5$–$5.0$ |
| 2 | Annual $\text{CO}_2$ Avoided | $\Delta CO_2 = E_{gen} \cdot EF_{grid}$ | $EF_{grid}$: emission factor | kg CO₂e | India avg $EF \approx 0.72$ |
| 3 | Simple Payback | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{I_0}{S_{ann}}$ | $I_0$: initial investment, $S_{ann}$: annual savings | years | KTU staple |
| 4 | Life-Cycle $\text{CO}_2$ | $LCCO_2 = \sum_{i} (Q_i \cdot EF_i) - \sum_{j} (Q_j \cdot EF_j)$ | $i$: input streams, $j$: avoided emissions | kg CO₂e | Cradle-to-grave |
| 5 | Embodied Energy | $EE_{total} = \sum_k (m_k \cdot EE_k)$ | $m_k$: material mass, $EE_k$: per-kg EE | MJ | Sum over all materials |
| 6 | Carbon Footprint | $CF = EE_{total} \cdot EF_{elec} + OC \cdot A \cdot t$ | $A$: floor area, $t$: lifetime | kg CO₂e | Lifetime basis |
| 7 | Net-Zero Verification | $\sum E_{gen} \geq \sum E_{dem}$ over $T$ | Energy balance | kWh/yr | $T$ = 1 yr minimum |
| 8 | Water Harvesting Yield | $V_{harv} = A_{c} \cdot P \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot C_{r}$ | $A_{c}$: catchment area, $P$: rainfall, $\eta_c$: collection eff., $C_r$: runoff coeff. | m³/yr | Kerala $P \approx 3000$ mm |
| 9 | Diversion Rate | $D_f = \dfrac{W_{diverted}}{W_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $W$: waste mass | % | Zero-waste $\to 100\%$ |
| 10 | Energy Use Intensity | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | $A_{floor}$: conditioned area | kWh/m²·yr | Green-building KPI |
| 11 | Solar Fraction | $f_{solar} = \dfrac{E_{PV}}{E_{demand}}$ | Ratio | dimensionless | Net-zero $\to 1$ |
| 12 | ROI | $r = \dfrac{S_{ann}}{I_0} \times 100\%$ | Annualised return | % | Compared to MARR |
| 13 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | $i$: discount rate | ₹ | $NPV \ge 0$ for go-decision |
| 14 | GWP-Weighted Emissions | $GWP_{tot} = \sum_x m_x \cdot GWP_{100,x}$ | $GWP_{100,x}$: 100-yr factor | kg CO₂e | $\text{CH}_4 = 28$, $\text{N}_2\text{O} = 265$ |
| 15 | EROI | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in,life}}$ | Useful out / life-cycle in | dimensionless | Wind $\approx 18$–$22$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Marking Hack:** When a question asks for *payback period* in a case study, write the formula **before** substituting values. Examiners award 1 mark for the correct expression even if arithmetic slips.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Built-Environment Engineers** use case studies to benchmark green buildings against **GRIHA / LEED / IGBC** ratings.
- **Process Engineers** identify **industrial symbiosis** opportunities (Kalundborg Symbiosis model).
- **Energy Planners** assess whether a state like **Kerala** (high hydro-share, low coal) can reach 100% renewable by 2030 using case-study transferability.
- **Policy Analysts** use cases to argue for subsidies, feed-in tariffs, and net-metering frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exemplar Case Study: **ITC Green Centre, Gurgaon** (India's First LEED Platinum Office)

> [!NOTE]
> This case study is one of the most frequently cited Indian sustainability case studies in KTU / NEP-aligned engineering curricula. We will derive every key sustainability metric step-by-step.

### Given Data (As-Is vs. To-Be)

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Floor Area (Conditioned) | $A_{floor}$ | **170,000** | m² |
| Annual Energy Demand (Pre-Retrofit Baseline) | $E_{base}$ | **9.5 × 10⁶** | kWh/yr |
| Annual Energy Demand (Post-Retrofit) | $E_{new}$ | **5.7 × 10⁶** | kWh/yr |
| Rooftop PV Installed Capacity | $P_{peak}$ | **1.4** | MWp |
| Grid Emission Factor (India CEA avg.) | $EF_{grid}$ | **0.72** | kg CO₂e/kWh |
| Annual Rainfall (Gurgaon) | $P$ | **700** | mm/yr |
| Catchment Area for RWH | $A_c$ | **12,000** | m² |
| Runoff Coefficient | $C_r$ | **0.85** | – |
| Collection Efficiency | $\eta_c$ | **0.80** | – |
| Initial Investment (PV + Efficiency Measures) | $I_0$ | **₹ 4.2 Crore** | ₹ |
| Tariff | $T$ | **₹ 7.5** | /kWh |

### Derivation 1 — Annual Energy Savings

The engineering postulation is that the energy reduction comes from:
- Chilled-beam cooling + under-floor air distribution (ΔE₁)
- T5 fluorescent + LED retrofit (ΔE₂)
- Daylight sensors + CO₂-based DCV (ΔE₃)
- VFD-driven pumps and fans (ΔE₄)

The total annual energy savings are:

$$
\Delta E = E_{base} - E_{new}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\Delta E = 9.5 \times 10^{6} - 5.7 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\Delta E = 3.8 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kWh/yr}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** A saving of $3.8$ GWh/yr corresponds to powering approximately **1,100 Indian households** continuously, validating the engineering efficacy of the integrated design.

### Derivation 2 — Annual PV Energy Generation

For Gurgaon latitude ($28.4°$ N), the equivalent **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** are approximately **5.0 hr/day**, and a state-of-the-art system efficiency is $\eta_{sys} \approx 0.78$.

The annual PV energy generation equation is:

$$
E_{PV} = P_{peak} \times PSH \times 365 \times \eta_{sys}
$$

$$
E_{PV} = 1{,}400 \;\text{kWp} \times 5.0 \;\text{hr/day} \times 365 \times 0.78
$$

$$
E_{PV} = 1{,}400 \times 5.0 \times 365 \times 0.78
$$

$$
E_{PV} = 1{,}400 \times 1{,}423.5
$$

$$
E_{PV} \approx 1.993 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kWh/yr} \approx 1.99 \;\text{GWh/yr}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** PV offsets roughly **35%** of the post-retrofit demand, moving the building significantly toward a **net-zero envelope**.

### Derivation 3 — Annual CO₂ Avoided

The CO₂ avoided each year from combined efficiency + PV is:

$$
\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \cdot EF_{grid} + E_{PV} \cdot EF_{grid}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = (3.8 \times 10^{6} + 1.99 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.72
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 5.79 \times 10^{6} \times 0.72
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 \approx 4.17 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 \approx 4{,}168.8 \;\text{tonnes CO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Equivalent to removing approximately **900 petrol cars** from the road, or sequestering carbon in **~67,000 tree seedlings** grown for 10 years (US-EPA equivalence).

### Derivation 4 — Simple Payback Period

The annual monetary savings are first computed as:

$$
S_{ann} = \Delta E \cdot T
$$

$$
S_{ann} = 3.8 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kWh} \times 7.5 \;\text{₹/kWh}
$$

$$
S_{ann} = 2.85 \times 10^{7} \;\text{₹/yr} = ₹ 2.85 \;\text{Crore/yr}
$$

Note: PV generation savings are *internal-offset*, not billable; therefore we conservatively ignore them in the *bill-saving* payback. The simple payback is:

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{I_0}{S_{ann}}
$$

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{7}}{2.85 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
T_{pb} \approx 1.47 \;\text{years}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Such a short payback — physically unusual — reflects the very high tariff paid by ITC for commercial grid power, *not* a typical residential economics. A student must clarify this when applying the formula to a Kerala domestic context.

### Derivation 5 — Rainwater Harvesting Yield

The classical mass-balance formula is:

$$
V_{harv} = A_c \cdot P \cdot C_r \cdot \eta_c
$$

First, convert $P = 700$ mm to metres, so $P = 0.700$ m.

$$
V_{harv} = 12{,}000 \;\text{m}^2 \times 0.700 \;\text{m} \times 0.85 \times 0.80
$$

$$
V_{harv} = 12{,}000 \times 0.476
$$

$$
V_{harv} = 5{,}712 \;\text{m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This volume satisfies the **non-potable demand of ~ 200 employees** (assuming 80 L/day for landscaping + flushing). Combined with sewage treatment plant (STP) recycling, the building reaches a **Water Performance Index** of < 40 L/person·day.

### Derivation 6 — EUI & Solar Fraction

The post-retrofit EUI is:

$$
EUI_{new} = \frac{E_{new}}{A_{floor}}
$$

$$
EUI_{new} = \frac{5.7 \times 10^{6}}{170{,}000} = 33.5 \;\text{kWh/m}^2\cdot\text{yr}
$$

The solar fraction is:

$$
f_{solar} = \frac{E_{PV}}{E_{new}} = \frac{1.99 \times 10^{6}}{5.7 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.349
$$

$$
f_{solar} \approx 34.9\%
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TBL & SDG Mapping

| Sustainability Dimension | TBL Pillar | Engineered Intervention | Quantified Outcome | Mapped SDG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Efficiency | Planet | T5/LED, VFD, daylight sensors | $\Delta E = 3.8$ GWh/yr | SDG 7, 13 |
| On-site Renewables | Planet | 1.4 MWp BIPV + Rooftop | $E_{PV} = 1.99$ GWh/yr | SDG 7, 11 |
| Carbon Reduction | Planet | Combined above | $\Delta CO_2 = 4{,}169$ t/yr | SDG 13 |
| Water Stewardship | Planet | RWH + STP dual plumbing | $V_{harv} = 5{,}712$ m³/yr | SDG 6 |
| Cost Reduction | Profit | Lower OPEX | $S_{ann} = ₹ 2.85$ Cr/yr | SDG 8 |
| Occupant Comfort | People | $\text{CO}_2$ DCV, daylight | $C_{in} \leq 800$ ppm | SDG 3 |
| Materials | Planet | Fly-ash bricks, recycled steel | $EE_{total} \downarrow 22\%$ | SDG 12 |
| Waste | Planet | 3R + composting | $D_f = 95\%$ | SDG 12 |

## 3.3 Critical SWOT Synthesis (Required in Every KTU Answer)

| Quadrant | Insight (Directly Transferable to Any 14-Mark Answer) |
|---|---|
| **Strengths** | Sub-2-year payback; **34.9%** solar fraction; IGBC & LEED Platinum; replicable design toolkit. |
| **Weaknesses** | High capex (₹ 4.2 Cr); performance drift without BMS; tariff sensitivity. |
| **Opportunities** | EV charging + storage; façade agrivoltaics; AI-based occupancy analytics. |
| **Threats** | Grid instability; policy rollback on net-metering; monsoon shading. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define a sustainability case study. List any four key metrics used to evaluate it.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *sustainability case study* is a **systematic investigation of a real-world engineering system** to assess its environmental, economic, and social performance using the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework.

Four key metrics:
1. **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** in kWh/m²·yr
2. **Carbon Footprint** in kg CO₂e
3. **Water Use Intensity (WUI)** in L/person·day
4. **Waste Diversion Rate ($D_f$)** in %

> [!NOTE]
> Award 1 mark for the definition, 0.5 mark each for the four metrics, and 0.5 mark extra for using a numeric benchmark.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Embodied Energy and Operational Carbon. State one formula for each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Embodied Energy ($EE$) | Operational Carbon ($OC$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Energy consumed during material extraction, manufacturing, and construction | Carbon emitted during the use-phase |
| **Stage** | Pre-operation (cradle-to-gate) | Operation & maintenance |
| **Formula** | $EE = \sum_k m_k \cdot EE_k$ | $OC = EUI \cdot A \cdot EF \cdot t$ |
| **Unit** | MJ or GJ | kg CO₂e |

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

> *"A 200,000 m² commercial complex in Kochi consumes **12 GWh/yr** of grid electricity and emits approximately **8,640 t CO₂e/yr**. The management proposes: (i) a 1.6 MWp rooftop PV system, (ii) full LED retrofit, (iii) a 1,500 m² rainwater harvesting system, and (iv) IoT-based BMS. Assume $PSH = 4.7$ hr/day for Kochi, $\eta_{sys} = 0.78$, $EF_{grid} = 0.72$ kg CO₂e/kWh, rainfall = 2,800 mm, $C_r = 0.85$, $\eta_c = 0.8$, total project cost = ₹ 5.0 Cr, tariff = ₹ 7.0/kWh, expected demand reduction post-LED + BMS = 30%."*

#### **Part (a)** — Compute (i) annual PV energy, (ii) annual $\text{CO}_2$ avoided, (iii) annual monetary savings, and (iv) simple payback. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual PV Energy (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_{PV} = P_{peak} \cdot PSH \cdot 365 \cdot \eta_{sys}
$$

$$
E_{PV} = 1{,}600 \cdot 4.7 \cdot 365 \cdot 0.78
$$

$$
E_{PV} = 1{,}600 \cdot 1{,}338.74
$$

$$
E_{PV} \approx 2.142 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kWh/yr} \approx 2.14 \;\text{GWh/yr}
$$

**(ii) Demand Reduction (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta E = 12 \times 10^{6} \times 0.30 = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kWh/yr}
$$

**(iii) Total CO₂ Avoided (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = (\Delta E + E_{PV}) \cdot EF_{grid}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = (3.6 + 2.14) \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.72
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 5.74 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.72
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 \approx 4.133 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} \approx 4{,}133 \;\text{t/yr}
$$

**(iv) Monetary Savings and Payback (3 marks):**

$$
S_{ann} = \Delta E \cdot T = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot 7.0 = 2.52 \times 10^{7} \;\text{₹/yr} = ₹ 2.52 \;\text{Cr/yr}
$$

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{I_0}{S_{ann}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{2.52 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.98 \;\text{years}
$$

#### **Part (b)** — Map the interventions to relevant UN-SDGs and evaluate the project using a SWOT framework. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**SDG Mapping (2 marks):**

| Intervention | SDG |
|---|---|
| 1.6 MWp Rooftop PV | **SDG 7** (Affordable & Clean Energy), **SDG 11** (Sustainable Cities) |
| LED + IoT BMS | **SDG 7**, **SDG 13** (Climate Action) |
| Rainwater Harvesting | **SDG 6** (Clean Water) |
| Combined Carbon Reduction | **SDG 13** (Climate Action) |

**SWOT (5 marks):**

| Quadrant | Content |
|---|---|
| **Strengths** | Sub-2-year payback; 4,133 t/yr CO₂ avoided; multiple SDG contributions; technology-proven. |
| **Weaknesses** | Monsoon cloud cover may drop PV yield 12–18%; IoT BMS requires skilled maintenance. |
| **Opportunities** | Add 500 kWh BESS for peak shaving; EV-charging hub; Kerala's net-metering policy enables full export. |
| **Threats** | Future tariff restructuring; salinity corrosion in coastal Kochi reduces PV panel life. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

> *"Critically evaluate a **green campus initiative** at any KTU-affiliated engineering college, with reference to: (i) baseline energy, water, and waste data, (ii) the four pillars of the Triple Bottom Line, (iii) at least three quantitative metrics with formulas, and (iv) recommendations for reaching net-zero."*

#### **Part (a)** — Discuss the TBL pillars with reference to a hypothetical green campus of 2,000 students consuming 1.2 GWh/yr grid power and generating 1.2 t/day municipal solid waste (MSW). **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. People Pillar (2 marks):**
- Indoor environmental quality via $C_{in} \le 800$ ppm (DCV).
- Daylight factor $\geq 2\%$ in classrooms.
- 1.2 t/day MSW → segregation at source; composting of 0.6 t/day organic waste.

**2. Planet Pillar (2.5 marks):**
- $EUI = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^6}{A_{floor}}$. Assuming $A = 30{,}000$ m², $EUI = 40$ kWh/m²·yr.
- Carbon footprint:
$$
CF = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.72 = 8.64 \times 10^5 \;\text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} = 864 \;\text{t/yr}
$$
- Rainwater harvested: $V_{harv} = 5{,}000 \cdot 2.8 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.8 = 9{,}520$ m³/yr.

**3. Profit Pillar (2.5 marks):**
- LED retrofit cost ₹ 50 lakh, savings ₹ 18 lakh/yr → payback = 2.78 years.
- Diversion rate $D_f = 0.6/1.2 = 50\%$ currently; achievable $D_f = 90\%$.

#### **Part (b)** — Recommend three net-zero pathways with quantitative justification. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Rooftop PV (2.5 marks):** 1.0 MWp × 4.5 PSH × 365 × 0.78 = **1.28 GWh/yr** → **Solar fraction $f_{solar} \approx 1.0$**, i.e., net-zero capable.
2. **Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) (2 marks):** 500 kWh Li-ion shaves 1.5 MW peak → **demand-charge saving ₹ 8 lakh/yr**.
3. **Biogas Plant for Canteen Waste (2.5 marks):** 200 kg/day food waste → 25 m³/day biogas → 50 kWh/day electricity → **18,250 kWh/yr** additional renewable + organic manure for campus garden.
$$
\text{Combined } f_{solar+biogas} = \frac{1{,}280{,}000 + 18{,}250}{1{,}200{,}000} = 1.08 > 1
$$
→ **Net-positive campus** certified.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Functional Unit (FU):** A case study *without* a stated FU loses **1 mark outright** in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in Payback:** Converting ₹ Crore to ₹ must be done before division. Many students write $T_{pb} = 4.2 / 2.85 = 1.47$ years — which is **dimensionally meaningless**. Always normalise.
> 3. **Confusing Embodied and Operational:** Board questions often give a 3-mark "compare" — students frequently swap the columns. Use the **pre- vs. use-phase** mnemonic.
> 4. **Ignoring Maintenance Cost:** KTU value keys deduct 0.5 mark if annualised O&M (≈ 1–2% of $I_0$) is excluded from $S_{ann}$.
> 5. **No Replicability Comment:** A 14-mark answer without a Kerala-transferability sentence is considered *incomplete* under NEP-2020.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **case study** is a **TBL-evaluated, real-world engineering investigation** with a defined **functional unit** and **boundary**.
- [x] Always quote the **five phases**: Boundary → Baseline → Intervention → Quantification → Recommendation.
- [x] The **17 UN-SDGs** must be *mapped*, not merely listed.
- [x] Memorise these constants: $PSH_{Kerala} = 4.5$–$5.0$ hr/day, $EF_{grid} = 0.72$ kg CO₂e/kWh, $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$, $GWP_{N_2O} = 265$, **ASHRAE $\text{CO}_2$ = 1000 ppm**.
- [x] Standard formulas: $E_{PV}$, $\Delta CO_2$, $T_{pb}$, $EUI$, $V_{harv}$, $D_f$, $NPV$, $EROI$.
- [x] **SWOT** is mandatory in every 14-mark answer (1+1+1+1+1 distribution).
- [x] **Net-Zero** is achieved when $\sum E_{gen} \ge \sum E_{dem}$ over a calendar year.
- [x] Always include **replicability** for the **Kerala context** (high rainfall, grid hydro-share, coastal corrosion).
- [x] Round-off to **two significant figures** in $T_{pb}$ and three in $CO_2$ — examiners are particular.
- [x] Use $A_{c}$ in m² and $P$ in **metres** in rainwater harvesting; mixing mm with m² is the #1 unit-blunder.
- [x] Cite **GRIHA / LEED / IGBC** rating for any Indian building case study.
- [x] LCA = **Cradle-to-Grave** (5 stages: Raw → Manufacture → Transport → Use → End-of-Life).
- [x] Industrial symbiosis: *Kalundborg* is the global exemplar; Kerala's Cochin Shipyard → HOCL cluster is a local analogy.
- [x] Green Campus = ISO 14001 + GRIHA 5 + at least one **BIPV** or **BESS** node.

> **End of Module 3 – Case Study Notes | OESFT721 – Sustainability in Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)
