# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — U-value of a Composite Wall (KTU Standard Numericals)

**Given:** A 230 mm brick wall is plastered with 15 mm cement plaster on the inside and 12 mm gypsum plaster on the outside. The thermal conductivities are:
- Brick: $k_b = 0.81$ W/m·K
- Cement plaster: $k_c = 0.72$ W/m·K
- Gypsum plaster: $k_g = 0.43$ W/m·K

Surface resistances: $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W (interior), $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²K/W (exterior).

**Find:** (a) Overall U-value. (b) Heat loss per m² for $\Delta T = 20$ K.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — List individual resistances.**

The wall consists of layers in series:

$$R_{\text{brick}} = \frac{d_b}{k_b} = \frac{0.230}{0.81}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{brick}} = 0.2839 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$$

$$R_{\text{cement}} = \frac{d_c}{k_c} = \frac{0.015}{0.72}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{cement}} = 0.0208 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$$

$$R_{\text{gypsum}} = \frac{d_g}{k_g} = \frac{0.012}{0.43}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{gypsum}} = 0.0279 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$$

**Step 2 — Sum total resistance (series addition).**

In a composite wall, since heat flows through layers sequentially, thermal resistances add in series:

$$R_{tot} = R_{si} + R_{gypsum} + R_{brick} + R_{cement} + R_{se}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.13 + 0.0279 + 0.2839 + 0.0208 + 0.04$$

$$\boxed{R_{tot} = 0.5026 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute U-value.**

By definition, the overall thermal transmittance is the reciprocal of total resistance:

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{tot}} = \frac{1}{0.5026}$$

$$\boxed{U = 1.990 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat loss per m².**

Heat flux is the product of U-value and temperature difference:

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 1.990 \times 20$$

$$\boxed{q = 39.79 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Without insulation, this wall loses ~39.8 W per square metre for every 20 K temperature difference. Adding just 50 mm of expanded polystyrene ($k = 0.034$ W/m·K, $R_{ins} = 1.47$ m²K/W) drops U-value to about **0.51 W/m²K**, an energy saving of **~74%**.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Cooling Load Estimation for a Conference Room

**Given:** Conference room 10 m × 8 m × 3 m. 25 occupants. Indoor 24 °C, Outdoor 38 °C. LPD = 10 W/m². Solar gain through west window (8 m², SHGC = 0.4) at peak: 700 W/m².

**Compute:** Total cooling load (sensible + latent).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Sensible heat from occupants.**

Each seated adult generates ~75 W sensible heat:

$$Q_{s,occ} = 25 \times 75 = 1875 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat from lighting.**

Total lighting power:

$$P_{lights} = 10 \times (10 \times 8) = 800 \text{ W}$$

Approximately 90% converts to heat inside the room:

$$Q_{s,light} = 0.9 \times 800 = 720 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Solar heat gain through window.**

$$Q_{s,solar} = A \cdot I \cdot \text{SHGC} = 8 \times 700 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{Q_{s,solar} = 2240 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 4 — Heat gain through envelope (walls + roof).**

Assume average U-value = 1.0 W/m²K for un-insulated construction:
- Net envelope area ≈ $2(10 \times 3) + 2(8 \times 3) + (10 \times 8) - 8 = 60 + 48 + 80 - 8 = 180$ m²
- $\Delta T = 38 - 24 = 14$ K

$$Q_{s,env} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 1.0 \times 180 \times 14$$

$$\boxed{Q_{s,env} = 2520 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 5 — Total sensible load.**

$$Q_{s,tot} = 1875 + 720 + 2240 + 2520 = 7355 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Latent load from occupants.**

Each person generates ~55 W latent heat:

$$Q_{l,occ} = 25 \times 55 = 1375 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Total cooling load.**

$$Q_{tot} = Q_{s,tot} + Q_{l,occ} = 7355 + 1375$$

$$\boxed{Q_{tot} = 8730 \text{ W} \approx 8.73 \text{ kW} \approx 2.5 \text{ TR}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion:** 1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW. Used to size the chiller/AC.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — EPI Calculation for a Commercial Building

**Given:** A 5000 m² office building consumes 350,000 kWh/year of grid electricity and has a 200 kWp rooftop solar plant generating 240,000 kWh/year.

**Find:** (a) Gross EPI. (b) Net EPI. (c) GRIHA rating category (GRIHA ≥ 1 star requires EPI ≤ 130 kWh/m²·yr for non-air-conditioned, ≤ 170 for AC).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Gross EPI (without solar).**

$$\text{EPI}_{gross} = \frac{E_{grid}}{A_{built}} = \frac{350{,}000}{5000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EPI}_{gross} = 70 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{·year}}$$

**Step 2 — Net EPI (with solar offset).**

$$\text{EPI}_{net} = \frac{E_{grid} - E_{PV}}{A_{built}} = \frac{350{,}000 - 240{,}000}{5000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EPI}_{net} = 22 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{·year}}$$

**Step 3 — GRIHA classification.**

- $\text{EPI}_{net} = 22$ kWh/m²·yr < 130 → easily satisfies **5-star GRIHA**.
- ECBC+ requires EPI < 90 → this building qualifies.
- A **Net-Zero** classification requires $\text{EPI}_{net} \leq 0$ — close but not achieved here.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numericals to Practice:** U-value of composite walls, cooling load estimation, EPI classification, SHGC through windows, COP comparison of chillers.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Energy Performance Index (EPI). State its significance in classifying buildings under ECBC.**

**Model Answer:**

EPI (Energy Performance Index) is defined as the **annual energy consumed per unit built-up area** of a building, expressed in **kWh/m²·year**:

$$\text{EPI} = \frac{E_{\text{annual (kWh)}}}{A_{\text{built-up (m}^2\text{)}}}$$

**Significance under ECBC:**
- **ECBC-compliant:** EPI = 90–120 kWh/m²·year (varies by climate zone).
- **ECBC+ (SuperECBC):** EPI ≤ 90 kWh/m²·year.
- **Net-Zero ready:** EPI approaches 0 with renewable offset.

EPI enables direct comparison of building performance across climate zones and is the **single most important metric** for benchmarking energy efficiency in Indian buildings.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any six passive design strategies for energy-efficient buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Building orientation** along East-West axis for tropical India.
2. **Shading devices** — horizontal projections, fins, louvers to block direct solar gain.
3. **Thermal mass** — use of brick/concrete to dampen indoor temperature swings.
4. **Natural ventilation** — cross-ventilation and stack-effect driven airflow.
5. **Daylighting** — skylights, clerestories, light shelves to reduce artificial lighting.
6. **Building insulation** — high-R-value walls and roofs to minimize conduction.
7. **Cool roofs** — high Solar Reflectance Index (SRI) coatings or reflective tiles.
8. **Wind/earth berming** — landscape integration to provide natural shading.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice between two questions (Q11 vs Q12, Q13 vs Q14, etc.). Each choice features two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below is a true module-internal choice — both questions test Module 3 but from different conceptual angles.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **building envelope** and discuss its role in energy-efficient building design. Discuss in detail the significance of **U-value** and **SHGC** in envelope design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composite wall consists of (from outside to inside): 12 mm gypsum plaster, 200 mm brick, 50 mm EPS insulation, 15 mm cement plaster. Thermal conductivities are: $k_{gyp} = 0.43$, $k_{brick} = 0.81$, $k_{EPS} = 0.034$, $k_{cem} = 0.72$ W/m·K. Surface resistances $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²K/W. Calculate the **U-value** of the wall and heat loss per m² for $\Delta T = 18$ K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **building envelope** is the physical boundary separating interior conditioned space from exterior ambient conditions. It includes:
- **Opaque elements:** walls, roof, floor, foundation.
- **Transparent elements:** windows, skylights, doors.
- **Construction joints and air barriers.**

**Role in energy-efficient design:**
1. **Reduces heating and cooling loads** by minimizing conductive, convective, and radiative heat transfer.
2. **Improves thermal comfort** by maintaining stable indoor temperatures.
3. **Reduces HVAC sizing** — smaller equipment → lower capital and operating cost.
4. **Controls daylight** — well-designed glazing admits light without excess heat.
5. **Minimizes moisture ingress** and condensation-related damage.

**Significance of U-value:**
- $U = 1/R_{tot}$ → measures how readily heat passes through the envelope.
- **Lower U-value = better insulation.**
- Typical targets: walls ≤ 0.45 W/m²K, roofs ≤ 0.35 W/m²K for ECBC+.

**Significance of SHGC:**
- Fraction of incident solar radiation transmitted through glazing.
- **Hot climates:** low SHGC (0.25–0.40) is preferred.
- **Cold climates:** higher SHGC (0.55–0.70) helps in passive solar heating.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OUT[Outdoor Conditions] -->|Heat Flow| ENV[Building Envelope]
    ENV -->|Controlled Flow| IN[Indoor Conditions]
    ENV -.->|U-value Control| OPAQ[Opaque Layers]
    ENV -.->|SHGC Control| TRAN[Transparent Layers]

    style OUT fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style IN fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style ENV fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute individual resistances.**

$$R_{gyp} = \frac{0.012}{0.43} = 0.0279 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{brick} = \frac{0.200}{0.81} = 0.2469 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{EPS} = \frac{0.050}{0.034} = 1.4706 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{cem} = \frac{0.015}{0.72} = 0.0208 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance.**

$$R_{tot} = R_{si} + R_{gyp} + R_{brick} + R_{EPS} + R_{cem} + R_{se}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.13 + 0.0279 + 0.2469 + 1.4706 + 0.0208 + 0.04$$

$$R_{tot} = 1.9362 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — U-value.**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{tot}} = \frac{1}{1.9362}$$

$$\boxed{U = 0.516 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss per m².**

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.516 \times 18$$

$$\boxed{q = 9.29 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

> This insulated wall meets **ECBC+ envelope standards** and reduces heat loss by ~76% compared to the un-insulated wall in §3.1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **passive design strategies** for energy efficiency in buildings. How does building orientation influence energy consumption in tropical India? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4000 m² commercial building in composite climate has the following annual energy consumption:
- Lighting: 80,000 kWh
- HVAC: 280,000 kWh
- Plug loads: 60,000 kWh
- Solar PV generation: 130,000 kWh/year

Calculate the **Gross EPI** and **Net EPI**. Comment on the building's ECBC compliance and suggest one improvement strategy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Passive design strategies** harness natural energy flows (sun, wind, daylight) without mechanical aid:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Energy Saving |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Minimize east/west wall exposure | 10–20% cooling load |
| Shading | Block direct beam radiation | 15–25% cooling |
| Thermal mass | Dampen daily temperature swings | 10–15% HVAC |
| Natural ventilation | Replace mechanical cooling | 20–80% in mild seasons |
| Daylighting | Reduce artificial lighting | 40–60% lighting load |
| Insulation | Reduce conduction | 25–35% HVAC |
| Cool roofs | Reflect solar radiation | 10–20% cooling |
| Landscaping | Microclimate moderation | 5–10% overall |

**Building Orientation in Tropical India:**
- The sun's path lies predominantly in the **southern sky** (in Northern Hemisphere).
- Therefore, **long axis oriented East-West** exposes only short North and South facades to direct sun.
- North and South walls receive **low-angle diffuse radiation** — easier to shade.
- East and West walls receive **high-angle direct radiation at sunrise and sunset** — most problematic.
- A building with **minimum East and West exposure** can reduce cooling load by **15–25%**.

**Other orientation-dependent factors:**
- Prevailing wind direction (for cross-ventilation).
- Views and daylighting (north-facing windows provide even, glare-free light).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Building Orientation] --> B[Long Axis East-West]
    A --> C[Short Axis North-South]
    B --> D[North Facade: Diffuse Light, Stable]
    B --> E[South Facade: Diffuse + Low Solar]
    C --> F[East Facade: Morning Solar Gain]
    C --> G[West Facade: Afternoon Solar Gain]
    F --> H[Use Vertical Fins]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Reduced Cooling Load]

    style A fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
    style I fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Total annual electricity consumption.**

$$E_{total} = 80{,}000 + 280{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 420{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Gross EPI.**

$$\text{EPI}_{gross} = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{built}} = \frac{420{,}000}{4000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EPI}_{gross} = 105 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{·year}}$$

**Step 3 — Net EPI (after solar PV offset).**

$$\text{EPI}_{net} = \frac{420{,}000 - 130{,}000}{4000} = \frac{290{,}000}{4000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EPI}_{net} = 72.5 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{·year}}$$

**Step 4 — ECBC compliance analysis.**

- ECBC threshold for composite climate: ~120 kWh/m²·year.
- ECBC+ threshold: ~90 kWh/m²·year.
- $\text{EPI}_{net} = 72.5$ → the building qualifies for **ECBC+ (SuperECBC)** status.

**Step 5 — Improvement suggestion.**

To reach **Net-Zero Energy Building (NZEB)** classification, the building needs to offset the remaining 290,000 kWh. Options include:
- **Expand rooftop solar PV** by an additional ~250 kWp (≈ 300,000 kWh/yr).
- **Upgrade to VRF HVAC** with COP > 4.5 (estimated 20% HVAC savings = 56,000 kWh).
- **Replace conventional lighting with LED + smart controls** (potential 40% saving = 32,000 kWh).

Combined, these measures can push $\text{EPI}_{net}$ below **40 kWh/m²·year**, approaching Net-Zero.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 3 — Energy Efficient Buildings):**
>
> 1. **Confusing U-value and R-value:** Some students write $U = R_{tot}$. This is **wrong**. $U = 1/R_{tot}$. Always state the units (W/m²K for U, m²K/W for R). **Lose 1 mark** if units are missing.
>
> 2. **Forgetting surface resistances:** In U-value calculations, omitting $R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$ leads to **erroneously high U-values**. Both must be added. **Lose 1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Wrong SHGC-SC conversion:** Some texts use $SHGC = SC$, others use $SHGC = 0.87 \times SC$. For **ECBC**, the Indian convention is $SHGC = 0.87 \times SC$. **Lose 1 mark** if conversion is missing.
>
> 4. **EPI without units:** Always write **kWh/m²·year**. A bare number is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Confusing COP and EER:** COP is dimensionless; EER has units Btu/W·h. Mixing them forfeits 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Ignoring both sensible and latent cooling loads:** A 7-mark sub-question on cooling load requires **both** sensible (people + lights + envelope + solar) and latent (moisture + people) components.
>
> 7. **Skipping diagrams:** Part-B answers without neat sketches/markdown tables/mermaid diagrams lose **2–3 marks** automatically for lack of visual aid.
>
> 8. **Confusing ECBC, ECBC+, and Net-Zero:** ECBC is baseline; ECBC+ is ~25% better; Net-Zero requires 100% renewable offset. Stating "ECBC-compliant" for a Net-Zero building costs marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Energy Efficient Buildings:**

- **Definition:** Energy-efficient buildings minimize energy use while ensuring comfort, using **passive + active + renewable** integration.
- **The 40-40-20 Rule:** 40% envelope, 40% systems, 20% renewables.
- **EPI (Energy Performance Index):** kWh/m²·year; the **single most important metric**.
  - ECBC: < 120 | ECBC+: < 90 | Net-Zero: ≈ 0.
- **U-value:** $U = 1/R_{tot}$ — lower is better. Walls ≤ 0.45, Roofs ≤ 0.35 W/m²K (ECBC+).
- **R-value:** $R = d/k$ — higher is better. Series addition for composite walls.
- **SHGC:** Low (0.25–0.40) for hot climates, high (0.55–0.70) for cold climates.
- **Decrement Factor:** Ratio of indoor to outdoor temperature amplitude.
- **Time Lag:** Delay between outdoor and indoor temperature peaks (ideal: 6–10 hrs).
- **Daylight Factor:** $E_{in}/E_{out} \times 100$; office target: 2–5%.
- **COP / EER / SEER:** HVAC efficiency; COP dimensionless, EER in Btu/W·h.
- **LPD:** Lighting Power Density, target ≤ 8 W/m² (offices).
- **EPS insulation:** $k = 0.034$ W/m·K; 50 mm can reduce wall U-value by ~75%.
- **Orientation:** East-West long axis in tropical India.
- **Cool Roof:** High SRI (> 78) reduces roof surface temperature by 10–15 °C.
- **LED lighting:** 100–150 lm/W vs 15 lm/W incandescent.
- **Solar PV EPBP:** 1.5–2.5 years for modern modules.
- **Building Management Systems (BMS):** 15–25% energy savings through automation.
- **Certifications:** GRIHA (India, 1–5 star), LEED (international), ECBC+ (Indian standard).
- **Net-Zero Energy Building (NZEB):** On-site renewables offset 100% of annual consumption.
- **Remember to include $R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$** in all wall U-value calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Cities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Cities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sustainable City** (also termed an *Eco-City* or *Low-Carbon City*) is an urban settlement designed, planned, and operated with the explicit objective of minimizing its ecological footprint while maximizing the quality of life, economic productivity, and social equity for its inhabitants across present and future generations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A sustainable city integrates **economic vitality, environmental stewardship, and social equity** (the three pillars of sustainability) into its urban fabric through compact land use, efficient resource circulation, low-carbon energy systems, and inclusive governance.

The United Nations conceptualizes sustainable cities through **Sustainable Development Goal 11 (SDG 11)**: *"Make cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable."*

### 1.2 Key Characteristics of a Sustainable City

| Dimension | Sustainability Attribute |
|---|---|
| **Environmental** | Net-zero carbon emissions, closed-loop water systems, biodiversity preservation |
| **Economic** | Green jobs, circular economy, resource-efficient industries |
| **Social** | Affordable housing, universal accessibility, participatory governance |
| **Infrastructure** | Smart grids, integrated public transit, green buildings |
| **Cultural** | Heritage preservation, community cohesion, place-making |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box – "The City as a Living Organism"**
> Imagine a city as a **large, complex organism** (like a human body). A healthy organism:
> - **Breathes clean air** (low air pollution, abundant green cover)
> - **Eats efficiently** (resources in, waste out — minimized waste)
> - **Circulates well** (efficient transport = healthy blood flow)
> - **Regulates temperature** (passive cooling, urban trees)
> - **Communicates** (smart sensors = nervous system)
> - **Ages gracefully** (resilient infrastructure, adaptive reuse)
>
> A **sustainable city** is one where this organism lives within its planetary means — just as a healthy body cannot consume 10× its caloric needs without collapsing, a city cannot consume 10× its regional resource share without ecological collapse.

### 1.4 Sustainable City vs Smart City — A Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Smart City | Sustainable City |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Digital connectivity, ICT, data | Environmental balance, equity, longevity |
| **Core Technology** | IoT, sensors, AI | Green infrastructure, renewable energy |
| **Metric of Success** | Efficiency, GDP per capita | Ecological footprint, well-being index |
| **Time Horizon** | Often short-term deployment | Multi-generational planning |
| **Energy Source** | May still rely on fossil fuels | Prioritizes renewables and demand reduction |
| **Governance** | Top-down technocratic | Participatory, inclusive |
| **Outcome** | A digitally optimized city | A regenerative, just city |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A city can be **smart but not sustainable** (e.g., a sensor-rich city powered entirely by coal). Conversely, a city can be **sustainable but not highly digitalized** (e.g., traditional compact townships with passive cooling). The ideal is the **convergence of both** — a *Smart and Sustainable City*.

### 1.5 The Role of Cities in Global Sustainability

Cities occupy a disproportionately large share of global environmental impact despite covering a small fraction of land:

- Cities house **>55% of the world's population** (projected ~70% by 2050).
- They consume **>75% of global primary energy**.
- They generate **>70% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions**.
- They produce **>80% of global GDP**.

> [!NOTE]
> This makes cities the **highest-leverage intervention point** for global sustainability. Decisions made in urban planning today lock in emissions trajectories for 50–100 years (concrete, roads, transit systems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Frameworks for Sustainable Urban Development

#### A. The Three Pillars of Urban Sustainability

$$
\text{Urban Sustainability} = f(\text{Environmental Integrity}, \text{Economic Resilience}, \text{Social Equity})
$$

- **Environmental Integrity:** Carbon neutrality, biodiversity, ecosystem services
- **Economic Resilience:** Diverse livelihood, circular economy, green jobs
- **Social Equity:** Affordable housing, accessibility, gender inclusivity, justice

#### B. The Doughnut Economics Model (Kate Raworth) Applied to Cities

A sustainable city operates **between a social foundation (inner ring) and an ecological ceiling (outer ring)**:

| Social Foundation (Don't Fall Below) | Ecological Ceiling (Don't Exceed) |
|---|---|
| Housing, food, water, sanitation | Climate change |
| Education, healthcare, income | Ocean acidification |
| Gender equality, social equity | Land-use change |
| Political voice, peace | Biodiversity loss |
| Energy, connectivity | Air, water, soil pollution |

#### C. Compact City Theory

A compact city achieves high density, mixed land use, and transit-oriented development (TOD). The mathematical justification rests on the inverse relationship between urban density and per-capita resource consumption.

#### D. The 15-Minute City Concept

Popularized by Carlos Moreno (Paris), this concept ensures residents can access **six essential functions within a 15-minute walk or bike ride**:
1. Living (housing)
2. Working (employment)
3. Commerce (shopping)
4. Healthcare
5. Education
6. Recreation/entertainment

### 2.2 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Sustainable Cities

| KPI Category | Metric | Unit | Target Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon** | Per-capita CO₂ emissions | t CO₂/capita/yr | <2.0 (Paris Agreement aligned) |
| **Air Quality** | PM2.5 annual mean | µg/m³ | <10 (WHO guideline) |
| **Green Cover** | Green space per capita | m²/person | >9 (WHO minimum) |
| **Transit** | Public transit modal share | % of trips | >40% |
| **Water** | Per-capita water consumption | L/person/day | <110 (sustainable benchmark) |
| **Waste** | Waste diverted from landfill | % | >75% |
| **Energy** | Renewable energy share | % | 100% (net-zero cities) |
| **Housing** | Affordable housing ratio | % | >30% |
| **Walkability** | Walk Score | 0–100 | >70 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any quantitative question on Sustainable Cities.**

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-Capita Carbon Footprint | $C_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{total} \times EF}{P}$ | $C_{pc}$ = t CO₂/capita; $E_{total}$ = total energy (MJ); $EF$ = emission factor (t CO₂/MJ); $P$ = population | Benchmarking city emissions |
| 2 | Urban Heat Island (UHI) Intensity | $\Delta T_{UHI} = T_{urban} - T_{rural}$ | $T$ in °C or K | Quantifies heat-island effect |
| 3 | Energy Use Intensity (EUI) | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | $E_{annual}$ in kWh/yr; $A_{floor}$ in m² | Building-level efficiency |
| 4 | Population Density | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{A_{urban}}$ | $P$ = population; $A_{urban}$ = urban area (km²) | Compactness indicator |
| 5 | Modal Share | $MS_i = \dfrac{Trips_i}{Trips_{total}} \times 100$ | $i$ = mode (walk, bike, bus, car) | Transit planning |
| 6 | Waste Diversion Rate | $WDR = \dfrac{W_{recycled} + W_{composted}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | $W$ = waste mass (kg) | Circular economy metric |
| 7 | Green Space per Capita | $GS_{pc} = \dfrac{A_{green}}{P}$ | $A_{green}$ in m²; $P$ = population | Liveability indicator |
| 8 | Carbon Sequestration (Urban Forest) | $C_{seq} = A_{canopy} \times B_i \times CF$ | $A$ = canopy area (ha); $B_i$ = biomass increment (t/ha/yr); $CF$ = carbon fraction (~0.5) | Urban forestry accounting |
| 9 | Stormwater Runoff Coefficient | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | $Q$ = runoff (m³/s); $C$ = runoff coeff.; $I$ = rainfall intensity (m/s); $A$ = area (m²) | Sponge city design |
| 10 | Building Energy Savings | $\%_{savings} = \left(1 - \dfrac{EUI_{green}}{EUI_{baseline}}\right) \times 100$ | Both in kWh/m²/yr | Green building certification |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineers** use compact city and sponge city equations for site planning, drainage, and transit corridor design.
- **Electrical Engineers** apply renewable share and EUI formulas for microgrid sizing and building electrification.
- **Mechanical Engineers** use UHI and passive cooling equations for HVAC retrofits in dense urban contexts.
- **All Branches:** Per-capita footprint and waste diversion metrics appear in ESG reporting, GRIHA/LEED certifications, and Smart City Mission (India) project evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Per-Capita Carbon Footprint of a City

**Statement:** Given a city's annual electricity and fuel consumption, derive the per-capita CO₂ emission.

**Given:**
- Annual grid electricity consumption: $E_{elec} = 5{,}000$ GWh
- Grid emission factor (India average): $EF_{elec} = 0.72$ t CO₂/MWh
- Annual transport fuel (diesel + petrol): $V_{fuel} = 200$ million litres
- Emission factor for mixed fuel: $EF_{fuel} = 2.68$ t CO₂/kL (kilolitre)
- City population: $P = 3{,}000{,}000$

**Step 1 — Compute electricity-sector emissions:**

$$
E_{CO_2,elec} = E_{elec} \times EF_{elec}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2,elec} = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ MWh} \times 0.72 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{MWh}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2,elec} = 3{,}600{,}000 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert fuel volume from litres to kilolitres:**

$$
V_{fuel,kL} = \dfrac{200{,}000{,}000 \text{ L}}{1000 \text{ L/kL}} = 200{,}000 \text{ kL}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute transport-sector emissions:**

$$
E_{CO_2,fuel} = V_{fuel,kL} \times EF_{fuel}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2,fuel} = 200{,}000 \text{ kL} \times 2.68 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{kL}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2,fuel} = 536{,}000 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 4 — Total city emissions:**

$$
E_{CO_2,total} = E_{CO_2,elec} + E_{CO_2,fuel}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2,total} = 3{,}600{,}000 + 536{,}000 = 4{,}136{,}000 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 5 — Per-capita emissions:**

$$
C_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{CO_2,total}}{P} = \dfrac{4{,}136{,}000}{3{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
C_{pc} \approx 1.38 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{capita/yr}
$$

**Interpretation:** This value is well below the global urban average (~4.5 t CO₂/capita) but above the Paris Agreement 1.5°C-aligned benchmark (~2.0 t CO₂/capita). The city must reduce emissions by ~31% to be Paris-aligned.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Urban Stormwater Runoff for a Sponge City

**Statement:** Estimate the peak stormwater runoff from a 50-hectare mixed-use catchment before and after green infrastructure intervention.

**Given:**
- Catchment area: $A = 50$ ha $= 500{,}000$ m²
- Design rainfall intensity (10-year storm, 1-hr duration): $I = 50$ mm/hr $= 1.39 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- Pre-development (impervious) runoff coefficient: $C_{pre} = 0.85$
- Post-development (with green roofs, permeable pavements, bioswales): $C_{post} = 0.45$

**Step 1 — Rational Method formula:**

$$
Q = C \times I \times A
$$

**Step 2 — Pre-development peak runoff:**

$$
Q_{pre} = 0.85 \times 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \times 500{,}000
$$

$$
Q_{pre} = 5.91 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Post-intervention peak runoff:**

$$
Q_{post} = 0.45 \times 1.39 \times 10^{-5} \times 500{,}000
$$

$$
Q_{post} = 3.13 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Runoff reduction:**

$$
\%_{reduction} = \left(\dfrac{Q_{pre} - Q_{post}}{Q_{pre}}\right) \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{5.91 - 3.13}{5.91}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{reduction} \approx 47.0\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A nearly 47% reduction in peak stormwater flow — reducing flood risk, replenishing groundwater, and lowering drainage infrastructure load. This is the foundational calculation behind **China's Sponge City Program**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sustainability Strategy Mapping

| Urban Challenge | Root Cause | Sustainable City Intervention | Quantified Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air pollution** | Vehicular emissions, coal-based power | EV adoption, BRT systems, rooftop solar | PM2.5 ↓ 30–60% |
| **Urban heat island** | Concrete density, low albedo, sparse canopy | Cool roofs, green walls, street trees | ΔT ↓ 2–4°C |
| **Flooding** | Impervious surfaces, encroached drains | Sponge city, bioswales, permeable pavement | Runoff ↓ 40–60% |
| **Traffic congestion** | Car-centric design, sprawl | TOD, metro, cycling infrastructure | Modal share shift 25–50% |
| **Housing crisis** | Speculation, exclusionary zoning | Inclusionary zoning, social housing | Affordability ratio ↑ to 30% |
| **Food deserts** | Retail consolidation, sprawl | Urban agriculture, vertical farms | Food miles ↓ 70–90% |
| **Energy poverty** | Fossil dependency, inefficient buildings | District heating, retrofits, solar | EUI ↓ 30–50% |
| **Waste crisis** | Linear "take-make-dispose" | Circular economy, composting, MRFs | Diversion rate ↑ to 75%+ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture: Pillars of a Sustainable City

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Sustainable City Master System"]
    B1["Pillar 1: Land Use and Mobility"]
    B2["Pillar 2: Energy and Buildings"]
    B3["Pillar 3: Water and Waste"]
    B4["Pillar 4: Green Infrastructure"]
    B5["Pillar 5: Social Equity"]
    B6["Pillar 6: Governance and Economy"]
    C1["Compact mixed-use TOD 15-min city"]
    C2["Net-zero buildings Microgrids Renewables"]
    C3["Sponge city Closed-loop water Circular waste"]
    C4["Urban forests Biodiversity Corridors"]
    C5["Affordable housing Inclusive design Participatory planning"]
    C6["Green jobs Circular economy Smart governance"]
    D["Regenerative, Net-Zero, Just Urban Ecosystem"]
    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    A --> B3
    A --> B4
    A --> B5
    A --> B6
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    B5 --> C5
    B6 --> C6
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Urban Sustainability Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["Inputs: Energy, Water, Materials, Food"]
    N2["Urban Metabolism: Buildings, Transport, Industry"]
    N3["Outputs: Emissions, Effluents, Solid Waste, Heat"]
    N4["Impact Assessment: Air Quality, Carbon, Ecology, Equity"]
    N5["Policy Levers: Zoning, Pricing, Codes, Investment"]
    N6["Sustainable City Outcomes: Net-Zero, Resilient, Inclusive"]
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N2
    N4 --> N6
    N5 --> N6
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Sustainable Urban Mobility Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0["Mobility Priority Pyramid"]
    M1["Tier 1: Walking and Wheelchairs - Universal, Zero-Emission"]
    M2["Tier 2: Cycling and E-Bikes - Active, Low-Cost"]
    M3["Tier 3: Public Transit - Buses, Trams, Metro"]
    M4["Tier 4: Shared Mobility - Carshare, Rideshare"]
    M5["Tier 5: Low-Emission Vehicles - EVs, Hybrids"]
    M6["Tier 6: Single-Occupancy ICE - Last Resort"]
    M0 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> M6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Sustainable city planning inverts traditional transport hierarchy. Instead of designing for cars first, the sustainable city prioritizes pedestrians, cyclists, and transit users — applying the "**inverted pyramid**" or "**avoid-shift-improve**" framework from the Institute for Transportation and Development Policy (ITDP).

### 4.4 Hierarchical Diagram: SDG 11 Targets for Sustainable Cities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["SDG 11: Sustainable Cities and Communities"]
    T1["Target 11.1: Adequate Housing for All"]
    T2["Target 11.2: Safe, Affordable Transport Systems"]
    T3["Target 11.3: Inclusive, Sustainable Urbanization"]
    T4["Target 11.4: Protect Cultural and Natural Heritage"]
    T5["Target 11.5: Reduce Deaths from Disasters"]
    T6["Target 11.6: Reduce Adverse Environmental Impact"]
    T7["Target 11.7: Universal Access to Green and Public Space"]
    T8["Target 11.A: National-Regional Development Planning"]
    T9["Target 11.B: Inclusive, Resilient, Sustainable Buildings"]
    T10["Target 11.C: Support Least Developed Countries"]
    S1 --> T1
    S1 --> T2
    S1 --> T3
    S1 --> T4
    S1 --> T5
    S1 --> T6
    S1 --> T7
    S1 --> T8
    S1 --> T9
    S1 --> T10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define a Sustainable City and list its key characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sustainable city is an urban area designed to minimize its ecological footprint while maximizing economic productivity, social equity, and quality of life for present and future generations.

**Key characteristics (any four):**
1. Compact, mixed-use land development with transit-oriented design
2. Net-zero or low-carbon energy systems (renewables, efficiency)
3. Closed-loop water and waste management (circular economy)
4. Abundant green infrastructure (parks, urban forests, biodiversity corridors)
5. Affordable, inclusive housing and accessible public services
6. Participatory governance and strong social capital

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Defining the term + giving 4 characteristics = full 3 marks. Skipping the definition loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between a Smart City and a Sustainable City.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Smart City | Sustainable City |
|---|---|---|
| **Core focus** | ICT deployment, data, connectivity | Environmental balance, equity, longevity |
| **Driving technology** | Sensors, IoT, AI, big data | Green infrastructure, renewables, compact design |
| **Energy source** | May rely on fossil fuels | Prioritizes renewables and efficiency |
| **Governance** | Often top-down | Participatory and inclusive |
| **Key metric** | Efficiency, GDP growth | Ecological footprint, well-being |

**Conclusion:** Smartness is a *means*; sustainability is the *end*. A truly future-ready city must be **both**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept of a Sustainable City with reference to SDG 11. Discuss the role of compact city design, transit-oriented development (TOD), and the 15-minute city in achieving urban sustainability.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Sustainable cities integrate environmental integrity, economic viability, and social equity. SDG 11 calls for inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable human settlements.

**Compact City Design (2 marks):**
A compact city features high-density, mixed-use development. Benefits include:
- Reduced per-capita land consumption
- Lower energy use for transport and buildings
- Preservation of peri-urban ecosystems and farmland
- Enhanced infrastructure efficiency

**Transit-Oriented Development — TOD (2 marks):**
TOD clusters residential, commercial, and office spaces around high-quality public transit nodes. It:
- Reduces private vehicle dependency
- Cuts urban GHG emissions
- Raises land value around stations (value capture financing)
- Promotes walkability and active mobility

**The 15-Minute City (2 marks):**
Proposed by Carlos Moreno (Paris, 2020), it ensures residents access six essential urban functions (living, working, commerce, healthcare, education, recreation) within 15 minutes by walking or cycling. It enhances:
- Quality of life and reduced commute stress
- Local economic vitality
- Carbon emissions reduction
- Social cohesion

---

**(b) Calculate the per-capita CO₂ emission of a city given the following data. Comment on whether the city meets the Paris Agreement benchmark of 2.0 t CO₂/capita/year.** **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Population: $P = 2{,}500{,}000$
- Annual grid electricity use: $E_{elec} = 6{,}000$ GWh
- Grid emission factor: $EF_{elec} = 0.82$ t CO₂/MWh
- Annual diesel consumption: $V_{diesel} = 150{,}000$ kL
- Diesel emission factor: $EF_{diesel} = 2.68$ t CO₂/kL
- Annual LPG consumption: $V_{LPG} = 20{,}000$ tonnes
- LPG emission factor: $EF_{LPG} = 2.94$ t CO₂/tonne

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Electricity emissions:**
$$
E_{CO_2,elec} = 6{,}000{,}000 \text{ MWh} \times 0.82 = 4{,}920{,}000 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Diesel emissions:**
$$
E_{CO_2,diesel} = 150{,}000 \times 2.68 = 402{,}000 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 3 — LPG emissions:**
$$
E_{CO_2,LPG} = 20{,}000 \times 2.94 = 58{,}800 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 4 — Total emissions:**
$$
E_{total} = 4{,}920{,}000 + 402{,}000 + 58{,}800 = 5{,}380{,}800 \text{ t CO}_2
$$

**Step 5 — Per-capita emissions:**
$$
C_{pc} = \dfrac{5{,}380{,}800}{2{,}500{,}000} = 2.15 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{capita/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with Paris Agreement:**
$$
\%_{excess} = \left(\dfrac{2.15 - 2.00}{2.00}\right) \times 100 \approx 7.5\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The city **exceeds the Paris-aligned benchmark by ~7.5%**. Required mitigation actions include renewable energy expansion, EV adoption, building retrofits, and modal shift to public transit.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Describe the concept of a Sponge City. With a suitable diagram, explain how green infrastructure reduces urban stormwater runoff. Solve: A 30 ha catchment has a pre-development runoff coefficient of 0.80 and a post-intervention coefficient of 0.40. With a design rainfall intensity of 40 mm/hr, compute the percentage reduction in peak runoff.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sponge City Concept (2 marks):**
A sponge city is an urban design paradigm that mimics natural hydrology — absorbing, storing, and reusing rainwater rather than letting it run off impervious surfaces. It deploys green infrastructure (bioswales, permeable pavements, green roofs, wetlands, rain gardens) to manage stormwater at the source.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Captures rainfall where it falls
- Enhances infiltration to recharge groundwater
- Filters pollutants through soil and vegetation
- Reduces load on piped drainage systems
- Buffers urban flooding and mitigates heat island effect

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Convert intensity: $I = 40 \text{ mm/hr} = \dfrac{40}{1000 \times 3600} = 1.11 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
Area: $A = 30 \text{ ha} = 300{,}000$ m²

Pre-development runoff:
$$
Q_{pre} = 0.80 \times 1.11 \times 10^{-5} \times 300{,}000 = 2.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Post-intervention runoff:
$$
Q_{post} = 0.40 \times 1.11 \times 10^{-5} \times 300{,}000 = 1.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Percentage reduction:
$$
\%_{reduction} = \dfrac{2.67 - 1.33}{2.67} \times 100 \approx 50.2\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A **50% reduction** in peak runoff — directly lowering flood risk and drainage infrastructure cost.

---

**(b) Discuss the role of green buildings, urban forests, and renewable energy systems in making a city sustainable. Support your answer with two global case studies.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Green Buildings (1.5 marks):**
- Reduce energy use intensity (EUI) by 30–50% through passive design, insulation, efficient HVAC
- Examples: LEED, GRIHA, BREEAM, Passive House, net-zero buildings
- Generate on-site renewable power (rooftop PV) and harvest rainwater

**Urban Forests and Green Infrastructure (1.5 marks):**
- Sequester CO₂: $C_{seq} = A_{canopy} \times B_i \times 0.5$
- Lower urban heat island intensity by 2–4°C via shading and evapotranspiration
- Improve air quality (PM2.5 capture), storm-water absorption, biodiversity
- WHO benchmark: ≥9 m² green space per capita

**Renewable Energy Systems (1.5 marks):**
- Rooftop PV, building-integrated solar, district heating/cooling, wind microgrids
- Decarbonizes the building and transport sectors
- Supports energy democracy and resilience

**Global Case Studies (2.5 marks):**

**Case 1 — Masdar City, Abu Dhabi (UAE):**
A planned carbon-neutral city powered entirely by renewables, using traditional Arabic passive cooling (wind towers), narrow shaded streets, and a Personal Rapid Transit (PRT) system. It demonstrates that hot-arid cities can be both livable and low-carbon.

**Case 2 — Copenhagen, Denmark:**
Targets **carbon neutrality by 2025**. Strategies: district heating (98% of homes), >50% cycling modal share, wind power supplying >40% of grid, ambitious building retrofit program. It demonstrates that cold-climate, high-density cities can lead the global energy transition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Missing the three-pillar framing:** Students often discuss only environmental aspects. Always mention *social equity* and *economic viability* explicitly — full marks require all three.
> 2. **Calculation unit slips:** Converting GWh → MWh and L → kL incorrectly is the single most common error. Always state the conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing Smart City with Sustainable City:** Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if these are treated as synonyms.
> 4. **Sketching diagrams without labels:** A sponge city or compact city diagram with unlabeled arrows/blocks loses 1 mark. Always label every component.
> 5. **Forgetting SDG 11 reference:** A question on sustainable cities almost always expects an SDG 11 linkage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sustainable Cities**

- **Definition:** Sustainable city = low ecological footprint + high liveability + economic resilience + social equity.
- **SDG 11:** The UN's overarching goal for cities — inclusive, safe, resilient, sustainable.
- **Smart ≠ Sustainable:** Smart = digital tools; Sustainable = long-term ecological balance.
- **Compact city + TOD + 15-minute city:** Three intertwined urban form strategies.
- **Key KPIs:** Per-capita CO₂ < 2.0 t/yr; PM2.5 < 10 µg/m³; green space > 9 m²/capita; modal share > 40% public transit; waste diversion > 75%.
- **Sponge city:** Green infrastructure mimics natural hydrology — runoff reduction 40–60% achievable.
- **Doughnut economics:** Operate between social foundation and ecological ceiling.
- **Case studies to memorize:** Masdar City (UAE), Copenhagen (Denmark), Singapore, Curitiba (Brazil), Freiburg (Germany).
- **Mobility hierarchy:** Walk → Cycle → Transit → Shared → EV → ICE (inverted from car-centric design).
- **Formulae to master:** Per-capita carbon, EUI, modal share, runoff (rational method), waste diversion, green space per capita.
- **Three pillars of urban sustainability:** Environment + Economy + Equity — always mention all three.
- **Time horizon:** Urban infrastructure locks in emissions for 50–100 years — act now.
- **Global leverage:** Cities host 55% of population but emit 70%+ of GHGs — highest-leverage intervention point for climate action.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Transport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainable Transport (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Sustainable Transport refers to the provision of services and infrastructure for the mobility of people and goods that meets present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own mobility needs. It is a low-carbon, energy-efficient, socially inclusive, and economically viable transport system that minimizes environmental degradation, reduces greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and promotes public health and urban livability.*

The **Brundtland Commission's** definition of sustainable development (1987) is the foundational reference for the concept, and in transport, this translates to a multi-dimensional framework comprising:
- **Environmental Sustainability** — Reduction of CO₂, NOₓ, PM (Particulate Matter) emissions.
- **Social Sustainability** — Equity, accessibility, and safety for all users.
- **Economic Sustainability** — Affordability and long-term financial viability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍃 Real-World Analogy: The City's Bloodstream**
> Think of a city as a living organism. Its **transport system is the bloodstream**. Just as arteries clogged with fat lead to heart attacks, cities clogged with **petrol/diesel vehicles** lead to environmental "heart attacks" — smog, respiratory diseases, and climate change. A sustainable transport system is like adopting a **healthy lifestyle** — clean blood (electric vehicles), efficient circulation (mass transit), walking/jogging lanes (pedestrian zones), and yoga (carpooling). The goal is to keep the city **alive, healthy, and fit for the long term**.

## 1.3 Key Sustainability Metrics in Transport

| Metric | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ Emission Factor** | **g CO₂ / km** or **g CO₂ / passenger-km** | Direct climate impact |
| **Energy Intensity** | **MJ / passenger-km** | Energy efficiency |
| **Modal Share** | **%** | Share of trips by each transport mode |
| **PM2.5 / PM10 Emission** | **µg/m³** | Air quality indicator |
| **Fuel Economy** | **km / litre** or **MPGe** | Resource efficiency |
| **Vehicle Kilometers Travelled (VKT)** | **km / day / capita** | Mobility demand indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🌍 Critical Global Fact:** The **transport sector accounts for approximately 24% of global CO₂ emissions** (IPCC AR6 data, 2022), making it the **second-largest emitting sector** after electricity/heat generation. Of this, **road transport contributes ~75%**, aviation ~12%, and shipping ~10%.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Sustainable Transport

1. **Avoid** — Reduce the need to travel (e.g., land-use planning, telepresence, remote work).
2. **Shift** — Move to cleaner, more efficient modes (e.g., public transport, cycling, walking).
3. **Improve** — Enhance vehicle & fuel efficiency (e.g., EVs, hybrid vehicles, alternative fuels).

This **A-S-I (Avoid-Shift-Improve)** framework is the **de-facto global strategy** adopted by the GIZ, UNEP, and World Bank for sustainable mobility planning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The A-S-I Strategy

### 2.1.1 **AVOID** — Reducing Transport Demand
- **Concept:** Decrease the *need* for motorized travel through better urban design.
- **Mechanisms:**
  - **Compact City Planning** (15-minute city model).
  - **Mixed-Use Zoning** — Residential, commercial, and recreational zones in close proximity.
  - **Tele-commuting and Digital Substitution** — E-commerce, video conferencing.
- **Engineering Utility:** Reduces road congestion, lowers VKT, and minimizes energy demand at the source.

### 2.1.2 **SHIFT** — Modal Shift to Sustainable Modes
- **Concept:** Encourage users to migrate from private cars to public transport, walking, and cycling.
- **Hierarchy of Sustainable Modes (from most to least preferred):**
  1. **Walking & Cycling** (zero emissions during operation).
  2. **Mass Rapid Transit (MRT)** — Metro, Light Rail Transit (LRT), Bus Rapid Transit (BRT).
  3. **Public Buses** (especially electric/hybrid).
  4. **Carpooling / Ridesharing.**
  5. **Electric / Hybrid Private Vehicles.**
  6. **Conventional ICE (Internal Combustion Engine) Vehicles** (least preferred).

### 2.1.3 **IMPROVE** — Technology & Fuel Upgradation
- **Concept:** Enhance the efficiency of vehicles and the cleanliness of fuels.
- **Mechanisms:**
  - **Electrification of Fleets** — BEVs (Battery Electric Vehicles), HEVs (Hybrid Electric Vehicles), PHEVs (Plug-in Hybrid EVs).
  - **Alternative Fuels** — Hydrogen (H₂), Compressed Natural Gas (CNG), Biofuels (Ethanol, Biodiesel), Synthetic e-fuels.
  - **Vehicle Light-Weighting** — Use of aluminium, carbon-fibre composites.
  - **Aerodynamic Improvements** — Streamlined vehicle design, low rolling resistance tyres.
  - **Smart Transport Systems (ITS)** — AI-driven traffic management, signal optimization.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **core quantitative toolkit** for KTU 2024 exam questions on sustainable transport. **Memorize the units and emission factors carefully.**

### Table 2.1 — Core Equations for Sustainable Transport Calculations

| # | Quantity | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total CO₂ Emissions (Tailpipe)** | $E_{CO_2} = D \times EF$ | $E_{CO_2}$ = emissions in g CO₂; $D$ = distance (km); $EF$ = emission factor (g/km) | Direct emissions of a single vehicle |
| 2 | **CO₂ per Passenger-Kilometre** | $E_{pax} = \dfrac{E_{CO_2}}{P \times D}$ | $E_{pax}$ in g CO₂/pax-km; $P$ = number of passengers | Compares modes fairly (bus vs car) |
| 3 | **Fuel Consumption** | $FC = \dfrac{D}{FE}$ | $FC$ in litres; $FE$ = fuel economy in km/litre | Operational cost estimation |
| 4 | **Modal Share** | $MS_{i} = \dfrac{T_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_{j}} \times 100$ | $MS_i$ = share of mode $i$ (%); $T_i$ = trips by mode $i$ | Urban transport planning |
| 5 | **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{E_{thermal}}{P \times D}$ | $EI$ in MJ/pax-km; $E_{thermal}$ = energy input in MJ | Energy efficiency ranking |
| 6 | **Equivalent CO₂ from Fuel (Combustion)** | $E_{comb} = m_{fuel} \times NCV \times EF_{fuel}$ | $m_{fuel}$ = fuel mass (kg); $NCV$ = net calorific value (MJ/kg); $EF_{fuel}$ = emission factor (kg CO₂/MJ) | Lifecycle tailpipe emissions |
| 7 | **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency** | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{TTW} \times \eta_{WTT}$ | $\eta_{TTW}$ = Tank-to-Wheel efficiency; $\eta_{WTT}$ = Well-to-Tank efficiency | EV vs ICE comparison |
| 8 | **Total Trip Cost (Per Capita)** | $C_{trip} = \dfrac{C_{op} + C_{cap} + C_{env}}{P}$ | $C_{op}$ = operating cost; $C_{cap}$ = capital cost; $C_{env}$ = environmental cost (externalities) | True cost comparison |
| 9 | **Reduction Potential** | $\Delta E = E_{base} \times \dfrac{MS_{new} - MS_{old}}{100}$ | $\Delta E$ = CO₂ reduction (g); $E_{base}$ = base emissions | Impact assessment of modal shift |
| 10 | **Air Quality Index Contribution** | $AQI_{contrib} = \sum_{i} C_i \times w_i$ | $C_i$ = concentration of pollutant $i$ ($\mu g/m^3$); $w_i$ = weighting factor | Health impact analysis |

### Table 2.2 — Standard Emission Factors (KTU-recommended values)

| Mode | Approximate CO₂ EF (g CO₂/pax-km) | Energy Intensity (MJ/pax-km) |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrol Car (single occupancy)** | **150 – 200** | **2.8 – 3.6** |
| **Diesel Car (single occupancy)** | **120 – 170** | **2.2 – 3.0** |
| **Electric Car (grid-mix average)** | **50 – 90** | **1.0 – 1.8** |
| **Diesel City Bus** | **60 – 100** | **1.0 – 1.6** |
| **Electric Bus (BRT)** | **20 – 40** | **0.4 – 0.8** |
| **Metro / Electric Train** | **30 – 50** | **0.5 – 1.0** |
| **Cycling** | **0 (operational)** | **0.06 – 0.16** |
| **Walking** | **0 (operational)** | **0.10 – 0.20** |
| **Domestic Flight** | **150 – 250** | **2.5 – 4.0** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a numerical question does not provide an emission factor, use the **mid-point** value from Table 2.2 and **state the assumption explicitly** in your solution. Examiners reward clarity of assumptions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planners** use these formulas to design **Transit-Oriented Development (TOD)** corridors.
- **Automotive Engineers** use WTW efficiency to evaluate powertrain designs (BEV vs FCEV vs ICE).
- **Environmental Consultants** apply A-S-I strategies to develop **City Mobility Plans (CMPs)**.
- **Policy Makers** rely on modal share data to set targets under **NDC (Nationally Determined Contributions)** commitments of the Paris Agreement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Model Problem 1 — Comparative CO₂ Analysis (Modal Shift)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A commuter travels **30 km one-way** to work daily (5 days/week, 50 weeks/year). Compare the **annual CO₂ emissions** if he uses: **(a)** a solo petrol car, **(b)** a 4-occupant shared petrol car, **(c)** a city bus, and **(d)** a metro train. Identify the most sustainable option.
> *Use emission factors:* $EF_{car} = 160$ g CO₂/pax-km, $EF_{bus} = 80$ g CO₂/pax-km, $EF_{metro} = 40$ g CO₂/pax-km.

### **Step 1 — Establish Common Parameters**

- One-way distance: $D = 30$ km
- Working days: $5$ days/week × $50$ weeks/year = $250$ days/year
- Round trip distance: $D_{RT} = 2 \times 30 = 60$ km/day
- Annual distance: $D_{annual} = 60 \times 250 = 15{,}000$ km/year

### **Step 2 — Apply the Per-Passenger Emission Formula**

The general formula for each mode is:

$$E_{annual} = D_{annual} \times EF_{mode} \times P_{occupancy\_factor}$$

Where the **occupancy factor** = 1 for solo car, and = (1/n) for shared cars with n passengers.

### **Step 3 — Case (a): Solo Petrol Car (P = 1)**

$$E_{a} = 15{,}000 \times 160 \times 1 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

$$E_{a} = 2{,}400{,}000 \div 1000 = 2{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

### **Step 4 — Case (b): 4-Occupant Shared Car (P = 4)**

Since emissions are shared, the per-passenger emission factor becomes:

$$EF_{per\_pax,\,b} = \frac{EF_{car}}{P} = \frac{160}{4} = 40 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{pax-km}$$

$$E_{b} = 15{,}000 \times 40 = 600{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

$$E_{b} = 600 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

### **Step 5 — Case (c): City Bus (P = 1, EF = 80 g CO₂/pax-km)**

$$E_{c} = 15{,}000 \times 80 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

$$E_{c} = 1{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

### **Step 6 — Case (d): Metro Train (P = 1, EF = 40 g CO₂/pax-km)**

$$E_{d} = 15{,}000 \times 40 = 600{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

$$E_{d} = 600 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

### **Step 7 — Comparative Analysis**

| Mode | Annual CO₂ (kg) | % Reduction vs Solo Car |
|---|---|---|
| Solo Petrol Car (a) | **2,400** | **Baseline (0%)** |
| 4-Occupant Car (b) | **600** | **75%** |
| City Bus (c) | **1,200** | **50%** |
| Metro Train (d) | **600** | **75%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **metro train** and **4-occupant carpool** are **equally the most sustainable options**, each cutting emissions by **75%** compared to solo driving. The bus offers a **50% reduction**. The shift from solo car to public transit (metro/bus) is a **classic "SHIFT" strategy** in the A-S-I framework.

---

## 3.2 Model Problem 2 — Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Compare the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** of a **Petrol ICE Car** vs a **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** charged from the **Indian grid mix** (assume 70% coal-based).
> Given:
> - Petrol ICE: $\eta_{WTT} = 0.88$ (refining efficiency), $\eta_{TTW} = 0.30$ (engine thermal efficiency)
> - BEV: $\eta_{WTT} = 0.40$ (avg. grid generation + transmission), $\eta_{TTW} = 0.85$ (electric motor + battery)

### **Step 1 — State the Well-to-Wheel Formula**

The Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency is the product of:

$$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$$

This represents the **fraction of primary energy** in the fuel source that actually reaches the wheels.

### **Step 2 — Calculate WTW Efficiency for Petrol ICE**

$$\eta_{WTT,\,ICE} = 0.88$$

$$\eta_{TTW,\,ICE} = 0.30$$

$$\eta_{WTW,\,ICE} = 0.88 \times 0.30 = 0.264 \text{ or } 26.4\%$$

### **Step 3 — Calculate WTW Efficiency for BEV**

$$\eta_{WTT,\,BEV} = 0.40$$

$$\eta_{TTW,\,BEV} = 0.85$$

$$\eta_{WTW,\,BEV} = 0.40 \times 0.85 = 0.34 \text{ or } 34\%$$

### **Step 4 — Comparative Inference**

$$\Delta \eta = \eta_{WTW,\,BEV} - \eta_{WTW,\,ICE} = 0.34 - 0.264 = 0.076$$

Percentage improvement:

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{0.076}{0.264} \times 100 \approx 28.8\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The BEV is **~29% more efficient** in WTW terms even with a coal-heavy grid. As India's grid decarbonizes (target 500 GW non-fossil by 2030), BEV efficiency will rise to **>50%**, making electrification a critical **"IMPROVE" strategy**.

---

## 3.3 Model Problem 3 — Modal Share and Emission Reduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A city has a current modal share of **60% private cars** and **40% buses**. The city plans to **shift 20%** of car users to metro rail. Given the emission factors from Model Problem 1, calculate the **total annual CO₂ reduction** if the city has **1 million daily commuters** each travelling **20 km one-way**.

### **Step 1 — Initial State Calculation**

Daily commuters = $N = 1{,}000{,}000$
Round-trip distance per day = $2 \times 20 = 40$ km

Initially:
- Car users = $0.60 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 600{,}000$
- Bus users = $0.40 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 400{,}000$

Daily emissions (initial):

$$E_{initial} = (600{,}000 \times 40 \times 160) + (400{,}000 \times 40 \times 80) \text{ g CO}_2$$

$$E_{initial} = 3{,}840{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}280{,}000{,}000 = 5{,}120{,}000{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{day}$$

### **Step 2 — Post-Shift State Calculation**

Shifted car users = $20\% \times 600{,}000 = 120{,}000$ shift to metro.
- Remaining car users = $480{,}000$
- Bus users = $400{,}000$ (unchanged)
- New metro users = $120{,}000$

Daily emissions (post-shift):

$$E_{new} = (480{,}000 \times 40 \times 160) + (400{,}000 \times 40 \times 80) + (120{,}000 \times 40 \times 40)$$

$$E_{new} = 3{,}072{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}280{,}000{,}000 + 192{,}000{,}000$$

$$E_{new} = 4{,}544{,}000{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{day}$$

### **Step 3 — Net Daily Reduction**

$$\Delta E_{daily} = E_{initial} - E_{new} = 5{,}120{,}000{,}000 - 4{,}544{,}000{,}000 = 576{,}000{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{day}$$

$$\Delta E_{daily} = 576{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{day} = 576 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{day}$$

### **Step 4 — Annual Reduction**

$$\Delta E_{annual} = 576 \times 365 = 210{,}240 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A modest **20% modal shift** saves **over 2 lakh tonnes of CO₂ annually** — equivalent to planting **~3.5 million trees** (using standard factor: 1 tree ≈ 60 kg CO₂/year). This illustrates the **massive leverage** of even small policy interventions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sustainable Transport System — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Sustainable Transport System"]
    nodeB["Pillar 1: AVOID - Reduce Travel Demand"]
    nodeC["Pillar 2: SHIFT - Change Transport Mode"]
    nodeD["Pillar 3: IMPROVE - Enhance Efficiency"]
    nodeE["Compact City Planning"]
    nodeF["Mixed Use Zoning"]
    nodeG["Telepresence and Remote Work"]
    nodeH["Public Transit Metro LRT BRT"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Sustainable Transport. List any four key indicators used to measure its effectiveness.**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
Sustainable Transport is the provision of mobility services and infrastructure that meets current needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs. It balances environmental, social, and economic sustainability.

**Four key indicators:**
1. **CO₂ Emissions per passenger-km** (climate impact).
2. **Modal Share of public transport** (mode distribution).
3. **Air Quality Index (AQI)** — particularly PM2.5 and NO₂ levels.
4. **Energy Intensity** (MJ per passenger-km).

---

### **Question 2: What is the "A-S-I" framework in sustainable transport? Explain with one example each.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The A-S-I framework is a global strategy comprising three pillars:

- **AVOID** — Reduce travel demand. *Example:* Promoting remote work to cut daily commuting trips.
- **SHIFT** — Migrate to sustainable modes. *Example:* Constructing dedicated cycle lanes to shift car users to bicycles.
- **IMPROVE** — Enhance vehicle and fuel efficiency. *Example:* Mandating **BS-VI emission norms** for all new vehicles in India.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**Q.A (a)** Define Sustainable Transport and discuss the **environmental, social, and economic pillars** of sustainability in the context of urban mobility. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Calculate the **annual CO₂ emissions** of a person who drives **40 km/day** in a solo petrol car (EF = 170 g CO₂/km). If he shifts to an **electric car** (EF = 70 g CO₂/km), what is the **percentage reduction**? Assume 300 working days. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A (a)

**Definition:** Sustainable Transport is a holistic approach to mobility that delivers safe, affordable, and accessible transport services while minimizing environmental harm, ensuring social equity, and supporting economic growth.

**The Three Pillars:**

**1. Environmental Sustainability**
- Reduction of **greenhouse gas (GHG)** emissions — particularly CO₂, CH₄, N₂O.
- Improvement of **air quality** — lowering PM2.5, PM10, NOₓ, SOₓ, and ground-level ozone.
- Reduction of **noise pollution** (electric motors are inherently quieter).
- Conservation of **non-renewable resources** (fossil fuels).
- Mitigation of **land-use change** — dense, transit-oriented development.

**2. Social Sustainability**
- **Accessibility** — Transport for all, including the disabled, elderly, and economically weaker sections.
- **Safety** — Reduction of road accidents (Vision Zero initiative).
- **Equity** — Affordable fares and universal access to mobility.
- **Public Health** — Reduced respiratory diseases from cleaner air; increased physical activity through walking/cycling.

**3. Economic Sustainability**
- **Cost Efficiency** — Public transport is far cheaper per passenger-km than private vehicles.
- **Job Creation** — Mass transit projects generate large-scale employment.
- **Reduced Externalities** — Healthcare costs from pollution-related illnesses are avoided.
- **Energy Security** — Diversification away from imported crude oil.
- **Real Estate Value** — Properties near metro stations command **20–30% premium**.

**Conclusion:** A truly sustainable transport system must integrate all three pillars; prioritizing only one (e.g., electric vehicles for the environment) without addressing social equity or economic viability will fail long-term.

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A (b)

**Given:**
- Daily distance (one-way): Not specified → assume **40 km one-way** as per problem = 40 km
- Working days/year: $N = 300$
- Round-trip daily distance: $D_{RT} = 2 \times 40 = 80$ km
- Annual distance: $D_{annual} = 80 \times 300 = 24{,}000$ km
- Petrol car EF: $EF_{p} = 170$ g CO₂/km
- Electric car EF: $EF_{e} = 70$ g CO₂/km

**Step 1: Annual emissions (Petrol Car)**

$$E_{p} = D_{annual} \times EF_{p} = 24{,}000 \times 170 = 4{,}080{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2$$

$$E_{p} = 4{,}080 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year} = 4.08 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

**Step 2: Annual emissions (Electric Car)**

$$E_{e} = 24{,}000 \times 70 = 1{,}680{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2$$

$$E_{e} = 1{,}680 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year} = 1.68 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

**Step 3: Absolute Reduction**

$$\Delta E = E_{p} - E_{e} = 4{,}080 - 1{,}680 = 2{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{year}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Reduction**

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{p}} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}400}{4{,}080} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Reduction} = 58.82\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The shift from petrol to electric car yields a **~58.8% reduction** in tailpipe-equivalent CO₂ emissions, saving **2,400 kg CO₂ annually**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** concept. Compare the WTW efficiency of a **Petrol ICE Vehicle** and a **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** with a coal-heavy grid, using suitable numerical data. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the role of **Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)** and **shared mobility** (Uber/Ola/Rapido) in achieving sustainable urban transport. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B (a)

**Concept of Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency:**

The **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** is the **overall efficiency** of a vehicle, measured from the **primary energy source ("well")** to the **energy delivered at the wheels ("wheel")**. It has two components:

- **Well-to-Tank (WTT)**: From primary energy extraction to the vehicle's energy storage (fuel tank or battery).
- **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)**: From stored energy to motion at the wheels.

$$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$$

**Numerical Comparison:**

**Petrol ICE Vehicle:**
- Refining efficiency (WTT): $\eta_{WTT} = 0.88$
- Engine thermal efficiency (TTW): $\eta_{TTW} = 0.30$

$$\eta_{WTW,\,ICE} = 0.88 \times 0.30 = 0.264 \text{ or } 26.4\%$$

**Battery Electric Vehicle (Coal-heavy grid):**
- Grid generation + transmission (WTT): $\eta_{WTT} = 0.40$
- Electric motor + battery (TTW): $\eta_{TTW} = 0.85$

$$\eta_{WTW,\,BEV} = 0.40 \times 0.85 = 0.34 \text{ or } 34\%$$

**Improvement:**

$$\Delta \eta = 0.34 - 0.264 = 0.076 \text{ (i.e., } 7.6 \text{ percentage points)}$$

$$\text{Percentage Improvement} = \frac{0.076}{0.264} \times 100 \approx 28.8\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The BEV is **~29% more efficient** even with a coal-heavy grid. As India's grid shifts to **50% non-fossil by 2030** (Government of India target), $\eta_{WTT}$ will rise to **~0.65**, pushing BEV WTW efficiency to **>55%**, making electrification a cornerstone of sustainable transport policy.

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B (b)

**Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS):**

ITS integrates **Information Technology, Electronics, and Telecommunications** with transport infrastructure to improve safety, efficiency, and environmental performance.

**Key ITS Components:**
- **Adaptive Traffic Signal Control (ATSC)** — AI-driven signals that adjust to real-time traffic.
- **Advanced Traveller Information Systems (ATIS)** — Google Maps, real-time bus tracking.
- **Electronic Toll Collection (ETC)** — FASTag reduces idling emissions.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Self-driving cars optimize acceleration/braking for fuel efficiency.
- **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication** — Cars communicate with infrastructure to prevent accidents and optimize routing.

**Sustainability Impact of ITS:**
- Reduces congestion-induced emissions by **15–25%**.
- Cuts fuel waste from idling at signals.
- Optimizes routing to reduce **VKT (Vehicle Kilometers Travelled)**.
- Enables **Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS)** — single platform for all transport modes.

**Shared Mobility (Uber, Ola, Rapido, Bounce):**

Shared mobility is the **shared use of a vehicle, journey, or transportation service**. It includes:
- **Ride-hailing** (Uber, Ola) — Single vehicle, multiple sequential passengers.
- **Carpooling** (BlaBlaCar) — Multi-occupant trips.
- **Bike-sharing** (Bounce, Yulu) — Last-mile connectivity.
- **Micro-mobility** (e-scooters, e-bikes) — Short urban trips.

**Sustainability Impact of Shared Mobility:**
- Each shared ride replaces **3–4 private vehicles**, reducing congestion.
- Studies show ride-pooling can reduce urban **CO₂ emissions by 15–20%**.
- Reduces parking demand — freeing urban land for green/public spaces.
- Lowers per-capita cost of mobility, improving accessibility.

**Challenges:**
- **Deadheading** — Empty rides between passengers add to emissions.
- **Driver behaviour** — Aggressive driving increases fuel use.
- **Equity concerns** — May under-serve low-income or peripheral areas.
- **Modal shift risk** — Could attract users from public transit, increasing VKT.

**Integration Strategy:** The most sustainable approach is **MaaS (Mobility-as-a-Service)** — combining metro, bus, shared mobility, and micro-mobility into a **single subscription-based digital platform**, with public transport as the backbone and shared modes as feeders.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write only the **definition** of sustainable transport without addressing the **three pillars** (environment, society, economy). Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for the pillars. **Always include them.**
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In WTW efficiency questions, students **swap the ICE and BEV values** or use $\eta_{WTT}$ and $\eta_{TTW}$ interchangeably. **Memorize the correct assignment**: ICE has high WTT (oil refining is efficient) but low TTW (engine losses); BEV has lower WTT (grid losses) but high TTW (electric motor).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In modal share calculations, students forget that **emission factors for shared modes are divided by passenger count**. Writing $E = D \times EF$ without the **occupancy factor** is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** When asked for percentage reduction, always compute: $(\Delta E / E_{base}) \times 100$. Confusing the **base case** (solo car) with the **new case** (electric car) is a frequent error.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Avoid **generic "advantages of EVs"** essays in 14-mark questions. Examiners expect **specific quantitative analysis** (numbers, emission factors, comparison tables). Always **tabulate** your answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Sustainable Transport**

- ✅ **Sustainable Transport** = mobility meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
- ✅ **A-S-I Framework**: **AVOID** (reduce demand) → **SHIFT** (to public/active modes) → **IMPROVE** (tech/fuel efficiency).
- ✅ **Transport contributes ~24% of global CO₂ emissions**; road transport = ~75% of that.
- ✅ **Modal Hierarchy (Best to Worst)**: Walking/Cycling > Metro/LRT/BRT > Electric Bus > Carpool > EV > ICE.
- ✅ **Core Formula**: $E_{CO_2} = D \times EF$ (Tailpipe) and $E_{pax} = E_{CO_2} / (P \times D)$ (Per passenger).
- ✅ **Modal Share Formula**: $MS_i = (T_i / \sum T_j) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Well-to-Wheel**: $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$. ICE ≈ 26–30%; BEV (coal grid) ≈ 30–40%; BEV (renewable grid) ≈ 55–70%.
- ✅ **Emission Factor Ranges (memorize midpoints)**: Car = 160 g CO₂/km; Bus = 80 g; Metro = 40 g; Walking/Cycling = 0.
- ✅ **Standard Vehicle Occupancy**: Car = 1.5 (avg), Carpool = 4, Bus = 30–50, Metro = 1000+ (per train set).
- ✅ **Sustainability Pillars**: Environmental + Social + Economic. **Never** answer without addressing all three.
- ✅ **Key Technologies**: BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV (Hydrogen), ITS, MaaS, TOD (Transit-Oriented Development).
- ✅ **Policy Frameworks**: Paris Agreement NDCs, India's FAME-II scheme, BS-VI norms, National Electric Mobility Mission Plan (NEMMP), Smart Cities Mission.
- ✅ **One Tree Equivalent**: 1 mature tree absorbs **~20–25 kg CO₂/year** (varies by species and region). Use this for equivalent comparisons in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Conversion Units**: 1 tonne = 1000 kg = 10⁶ g. Always convert before presenting final answers.
