# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Define poverty and differentiate between absolute and relative poverty.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Poverty is the condition in which an individual or household lacks the financial resources and access to basic services required to maintain a minimum acceptable standard of living. It is broadly classified into two categories:

**Absolute Poverty** refers to a fixed, universal threshold of deprivation — typically measured by an income level insufficient to afford a minimum basket of goods (e.g., World Bank's **$2.15/day** or India's Tendulkar line of **₹32 / ₹37**). A person below this line is considered absolutely poor regardless of where they live.

**Relative Poverty** is contextual and compares a person's income to the median income of their society. A household earning less than **50%** of the national median is considered in relative poverty. This captures inequality rather than just subsistence.

(3 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q]`
**What is the Headcount Ratio? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Headcount Ratio (H)** is the most widely used measure of the **incidence** of poverty. It represents the proportion of the total population whose income or consumption falls below the official poverty line.

The formula is:

$$H = \frac{q}{n}$$

where $q$ is the number of individuals (or households) below the poverty line and $n$ is the total population (or total number of households). The value of H lies between **0 and 1**, and it can be expressed as a percentage. For example, if $H = 0.20$, then **20%** of the population is poor. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students describe poverty reduction strategies in **generic, non-engineering terms** (e.g., "give money to the poor"). KTU board examiners specifically award marks for **engineering specificity** — name the **discipline, the technology, and the measurable outcome**. Always link your answer to an SDG indicator or a quantitative metric (H, PGI, MPI). Generic answers typically fetch only **4–5 out of 14 marks**.

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Q]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Millennium Development Goals (MDGs)** and **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** in the context of poverty reduction. Compare their effectiveness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable case study, describe how engineering interventions in **energy access** have contributed to poverty reduction.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — MDGs vs SDGs in Poverty Reduction

The **Millennium Development Goals (MDGs)** were adopted by 189 UN member states in the year **2000**, with a target completion year of **2015**. They consisted of **8 goals** focused primarily on developing countries. The first goal, **MDG 1**, was to *"Eradicate extreme poverty and hunger."* It had three measurable targets:
1. Halve the proportion of people living on less than **$1.25/day**.
2. Achieve full and productive employment for all, including women and young people.
3. Halve the proportion of people who suffer from hunger.

**Achievements of MDG 1:**
- The global extreme poverty rate fell from **36%** in 1990 to **12%** in 2015 — the target of halving was met **5 years early**.
- However, **sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia** lagged significantly behind.

**Limitations of MDGs:**
- They were **top-down**, donor-driven, and focused narrowly on income poverty.
- They ignored **environmental sustainability**, **inequality within nations**, and **multidimensional deprivation**.

The **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**, adopted in **2015**, consist of **17 goals** with **169 targets** to be achieved by **2030**. **SDG 1: "End poverty in all its forms everywhere"** explicitly broadens the definition of poverty beyond income to include **social, economic, and environmental dimensions**.

**Key Differences (Table for 7-mark depth):**

| Aspect | MDGs (2000–2015) | SDGs (2015–2030) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | 8 goals, developing-world focus | 17 goals, universal (all nations) |
| Poverty definition | Income-based ($1.25/day) | Multidimensional (income + services + rights) |
| Sustainability | Ignored | Central (SDG 13, 14, 15 integrated) |
| Approach | Top-down, donor-led | Bottom-up, country-owned |
| Accountability | Weak monitoring | Annual Voluntary National Reviews |

**Engineering relevance:** The SDGs make engineering indispensable. SDG 1 cannot be achieved without SDG 6 (water), SDG 7 (energy), SDG 9 (infrastructure), and SDG 11 (sustainable cities) — all of which are engineering-driven. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Engineering Case Study in Energy Access for Poverty Reduction

**Case Study: Solar Microgrid Implementation in Sagar Island, West Bengal (and similar models in sub-Saharan Africa).**

**Background:**
Sagar Island, a remote delta region, had **no grid connectivity** until 2017. Approximately **18,000 households** relied on kerosene lamps, spending **~₹300/month** per household — a significant fraction of their daily wage of **₹150–₹200**.

**Engineering Intervention:**
A team of electrical and renewable energy engineers designed a **hybrid solar microgrid** consisting of:
- **120 kWp** of photovoltaic panels mounted on elevated platforms to withstand flooding.
- **Lead-acid battery bank** (400 kWh storage capacity).
- **Smart metering** infrastructure for pay-as-you-use billing.
- **DC microgrid architecture** to minimize conversion losses.

**Quantifiable Outcomes (linked to poverty indices):**

| Indicator | Before Microgrid | After Microgrid |
|---|---|---|
| Energy expenditure per household | ₹300/month (kerosene) | ₹150/month (solar) |
| Household income retained | 100% (reference) | **+12–15% increase** |
| Hours of productive evening work | ~2 hours | ~6 hours |
| School study hours for children | ~1.5 hours/day | ~4 hours/day |
| Headcount Ratio reduction (energy poverty) | Baseline | Reduced by **~40%** in surveyed households |
| CO₂ emissions avoided | — | ~150 tonnes/year |

**Broader Engineering Role:**
The project also created **local employment** — **30 technicians** were trained from the village itself, exemplifying the **decentralized job-creation model**. The microgrid reduced the **Poverty Gap Index (PGI)** by lowering the energy cost burden, freeing household income for nutrition and education.

**Conclusion:** Energy access engineering is a **force multiplier** for poverty reduction because it simultaneously addresses income (lower energy bills), education (study hours), health (no indoor kerosene pollution), and gender equity (women no longer need to collect fuel). (7 marks)

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI)**. Explain its indicators and compute the MPI for a hypothetical region.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **sustainable agriculture and irrigation engineering** in reducing rural poverty.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) Explained

The **Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI)** was developed by the **Oxford Poverty and Human Development Initiative (OPHI)** and **UNDP** in **2010**. It captures the **multiple overlapping deprivations** that poor people face simultaneously, going beyond income alone.

**Structure of MPI:**

The MPI measures deprivation across **10 indicators** organized into **3 dimensions**:

**1. Health (2 indicators):**
- Child Mortality — Any child in the household has died in the past 5 years.
- Nutrition — Any adult or child is malnourished.

**2. Education (2 indicators):**
- Years of Schooling — No household member has completed **6 years** of schooling.
- School Attendance — Any school-aged child is not attending school up to Class 8.

**3. Standard of Living (6 indicators):**
- Electricity
- Improved Sanitation
- Safe Drinking Water
- Clean Cooking Fuel
- Housing (floor not made of mud/dung)
- Asset Ownership (owning more than one of: radio, TV, telephone, bike, etc.)

A household is classified as **multidimensionally poor** if it is deprived in **at least 33.3% (one-third)** of the weighted indicators. The maximum weight per dimension is **1/3**, split equally among its indicators.

**Numerical Computation:**

Let the hypothetical region have:
- Total households $n = 5000$
- Multidimensionally poor households $q = 1500$
- Average intensity of deprivation $A = 45\% = 0.45$

Step 1 — Compute Headcount ratio:

$$H = \frac{q}{n} = \frac{1500}{5000} = 0.30$$

Step 2 — Multiply by Intensity:

$$MPI = H \times A = 0.30 \times 0.45 = 0.135$$

Step 3 — Interpretation:

Since $MPI = 0.135 > 0.10$, the region is classified under **"Very High Multidimensional Poverty"** as per global benchmarks. An engineering planner here must therefore coordinate **multi-sectoral** projects (water + sanitation + electricity) rather than single-domain fixes. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Sustainable Agriculture and Irrigation Engineering in Rural Poverty Reduction

**The Rural Poverty Problem:**
Approximately **80%** of India's poor live in rural areas and depend on agriculture. Key challenges include:
- Rain-fed farming dependency (**55%** of Indian farmland is rain-fed).
- Post-harvest losses of **6–16%** due to inadequate storage and cold-chain infrastructure.
- Groundwater depletion from inefficient flood irrigation.

**Engineering Interventions:**

**(i) Drip and Sprinkler Irrigation Systems:**
- Drip irrigation delivers water directly to the root zone, achieving **~40–60% water savings** compared to flood irrigation.
- **Yield increase of 30–50%** in vegetables and horticultural crops.
- Example: **Jain Irrigation** has installed drip systems covering over **5 million hectares** globally.

**(ii) Precision Agriculture and IoT Sensors:**
- Soil moisture sensors, drones, and satellite imagery (e.g., **ISRO's Bhuvan** platform) optimize fertilizer and water use.
- Reduces input costs by **15–25%**, directly raising net farmer income.

**(iii) Solar-Powered Cold Storage:**
- Decentralized cold rooms powered by solar PV extend shelf life of produce from **2 days to 14 days**.
- Eliminates distress sales, allowing farmers to sell at **30–50%** higher prices.

**(iv) Watershed Management Engineering:**
- Construction of check dams, percolation tanks, and contour bunds recharges groundwater.
- Example: **Sukhomajri** and **Jhabua** watershed projects transformed tribal villages from net importers of food to surplus producers.

**Quantifiable Impact on Poverty Indices:**

| Intervention | Headcount Ratio (H) Reduction | PGI Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| Drip Irrigation | 8–15% | 10–20% |
| Cold Storage | 5–12% | 8–15% |
| Watershed Mgmt. | 12–25% | 15–30% |

**Conclusion:** Agricultural engineering is the most direct lever for rural poverty reduction because it simultaneously raises incomes, reduces vulnerability, and conserves water — embodying the trinity of **economic, social, and environmental sustainability**. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Poverty Reduction** = engineering-enabled multidimensional transformation, not just income transfer.
- ✅ **SDG 1** is the umbrella goal; it is **interdependent** with SDGs 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, and 11.
- ✅ **Absolute Poverty** = fixed threshold (e.g., $2.15/day). **Relative Poverty** = compared to national median (**50%** benchmark).
- ✅ **Headcount Ratio** $H = q / n$ — measures **incidence** of poverty.
- ✅ **Poverty Gap Index (PGI)** — measures **depth** (how far below the line).
- ✅ **Squared Poverty Gap (SPGI)** — measures **severity** (inequality among the poor).
- ✅ Always: $SPGI \leq PGI \leq H$.
- ✅ **MPI** uses **10 indicators** in **3 dimensions** (Health, Education, Living Standard).
- ✅ **MPI = H × A**; MPI > 0.10 = "very high multidimensional poverty."
- ✅ **Gini Coefficient** ranges **0 to 1**; higher means more inequality.
- ✅ Five engineering pillars: **Infrastructure, Energy, WASH, Agriculture, Digital Inclusion.**
- ✅ **JAM Trinity** (Jan Dhan + Aadhaar + Mobile) is India's flagship digital poverty alleviation system.
- ✅ Always link engineering interventions to **quantifiable outcomes** (H, PGI, MPI reduction percentages) — this is what examiners reward.
- ✅ Avoid generic answers — KTU specifically wants **engineering specificity** (technology + metric + impact).
- ✅ Remember key Indian schemes: **PMGSY** (rural roads), **PMAY** (housing), **MGNREGA** (employment), **PM-KUSUM** (solar agriculture), **Jal Jeevan Mission** (rural piped water).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Business
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sustainable Business ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Sustainable Business** (also termed a *Green Business* or *Responsible Business*) is an enterprise that operates in a manner which **balances economic profitability, environmental stewardship, and social equity** across its entire value chain, while ensuring that the natural capital and social capital it depends upon are preserved or regenerated for future generations. It integrates the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** philosophy proposed by John Elkington (1994), aligning core strategy, operations, and stakeholder engagement with the United Nations **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** and the **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)** disclosure framework. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Sustainable business refers to the management and coordination of organisational resources to not only satisfy stakeholders but also to **create value for the broader community and the natural environment**, ensuring long-term resilience rather than short-term extraction. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a farmer who owns an apple orchard. An **unsustainable business** would harvest every apple, sell them, and never replant trees or care for the soil — within a few years the orchard dies and so does the income. A **sustainable business** is the farmer who **harvests only what the orchard produces each season (economic gain)**, **plants new saplings and uses compost instead of harsh chemicals (environmental care)**, and **pays fair wages to workers and supplies healthy fruit to the local school (social responsibility)**. The orchard survives for centuries because it is treated as a *living system*, not a resource mine. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Sustainability in business is NOT about charity. It is a **strategic operating model** that converts environmental and social performance into long-term competitive advantage, risk mitigation, and brand equity. ### 1.3 Foundational Pillars of a Sustainable Business | Pillar | Description | Standard Metric | |--------|-------------|-----------------| | **Economic (Profit)** | Long-term financial viability & shared value creation | **ROI, EVA, Net Present Value (NPV)** | | **Environmental (Planet)** | Minimising ecological footprint, circular resource use | **Carbon Footprint (tCO₂e), Eco-Efficiency Index** | | **Social (People)** | Fair labour, community welfare, human rights | **GRI 400 Indicators, S-LCA Score** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The **United Nations 17 SDGs** are the universally accepted blueprint for embedding sustainability into business strategy. Businesses typically map their core activities to specific SDGs (e.g., SDG 7 - Affordable & Clean Energy, SDG 12 - Responsible Consumption & Production, SDG 13 - Climate Action). ### 1.4 Sustainability Standard Frameworks Used by Businesses - **GRI (Global Reporting Initiative):** Most widely used sustainability reporting standard globally. - **SASB (Sustainability Accounting Standards Board):** Industry-specific material sustainability accounting. - **TCFD (Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures):** Climate risk disclosure framework. - **ISO 14001:** International standard for Environmental Management Systems (EMS). - **ISO 26000:** Guidance on Social Responsibility. - **B Corp Certification:** Rigorous certification for verified social and environmental performance. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Framework The TBL framework measures organisational performance across three interconnected dimensions. Elkington's core premise: *"In the long run, a company that does not manage its social and environmental impact will not survive financially."* **Step-by-Step Logic of TBL Adoption:** 1. **Identify Stakeholders:** Map all internal (employees, shareholders) and external (communities, regulators, environment) stakeholders. 2. **Materiality Assessment:** Determine which sustainability issues are most material (significant) to the business. 3. **Baseline Measurement:** Quantify current economic, environmental, and social performance. 4. **Strategy Integration:** Embed TBL objectives into core business strategy, KPIs, and incentive structures. 5. **Reporting & Disclosure:** Publish integrated reports (e.g., following ** Framework** by IIRC). 6. **Continuous Improvement:** Use audit cycles (PDCA: Plan-Do-Check-Act) for iterative enhancement. ### 2.2 The Sustainable Business Value Chain Sustainable thinking must permeate every link of Porter's traditional value chain: - **Procurement →** Sustainable sourcing (e.g., conflict-free minerals, FSC-certified wood). - **Production →** Clean technology, waste minimisation, renewable energy adoption. - **Logistics →** Optimised routing, electric fleets, low-carbon packaging. - **Marketing →** Green marketing with verifiable claims (avoid greenwashing). - **End-of-Life →** Take-back programmes, remanufacturing, recycling design. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Framework / Metric | Formula / Expression | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Triple Bottom Line Value** | $\text{TBL}_{\text{Score}} = w_E \cdot V_{\text{Econ}} + w_N \cdot V_{\text{Env}} + w_S \cdot V_{\text{Soc}}$ | Dimensionless Index | Holistic business valuation | | 2 | **Carbon Footprint** | $\text{CF} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i \times \text{EF}_i)$ | tCO₂e (tonnes CO₂ equivalent) | GHG inventory | | 3 | **Eco-Efficiency** | $\text{EcoEff} = \dfrac{\text{Product/Service Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}}$ | Value/Impact ratio | Resource productivity | | 4 | **Carbon Intensity** | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\text{Total CO}_2 \text{ Emissions}}{\text{Unit of Output (Revenue/Product)}}$ | tCO₂e / ₹ or tCO₂e / unit | Decarbonisation tracking | | 5 | **ROI on Sustainability Investment** | $\text{SROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Present Value of Benefits}}{\text{Net Present Value of Investment}}$ | Ratio (often 1:3 to 1:7) | Justifying green capex | | 6 | **Discounted Payback for Green Project** | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$ | Years | Green investment appraisal | | 7 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency (₹, $) | Project selection | | 8 | **Materiality Score** | $M_{ij} = I_i \times S_j$ | 1–25 scale | ESG issue prioritisation | | 9 | **Circular Materiality Rate** | $\text{CMR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{recycled}} + M_{\text{reused}} + M_{\text{remanufactured}}}{M_{\text{total input}}}$ | Percentage (%) | Circular economy tracking | | 10 | **ESG Composite Score** | $\text{ESG}_{\text{score}} = \alpha E + \beta S + \gamma G$ | 0–100 | Investor ratings (MSCI, Sustainalytics) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Symbols:** $A_i$ = Activity Data (e.g., kWh, litres, km); $\text{EF}_i$ = Emission Factor (e.g., kg CO₂/kWh); $I_i$ = Impact magnitude; $S_j$ = Stakeholder concern; $w_E, w_N, w_S$ = normalised weights summing to 1; $B_t$ = Benefit at time $t$; $C_t$ = Cost at time $t$; $r$ = discount rate; $E, S, G$ = Environmental, Social, Governance sub-scores. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business - **Manufacturing Firms:** Apply eco-efficiency formulas to reduce material wastage per unit produced. - **Construction Industry:** Use SROI to justify investments in green buildings (LEED, GRIHA). - **IT & Data Centres:** Apply Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) under the same eco-efficiency umbrella. - **Automotive Sector:** Carbon intensity metric guides transition from ICE to EV powertrains. - **Investor Decision-Making:** ESG composite scores determine capital allocation and cost of equity. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Takeaway:** Sustainable business is no longer a "compliance cost" — empirical studies show companies with high ESG ratings exhibit **lower cost of capital (typically 50–100 basis points lower)** and **lower volatility** than their low-ESG peers. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Carbon Footprint Calculation (Scope-Based GHG Inventory) The **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Protocol** classifies emissions into three scopes. The total carbon footprint is the summation across all scopes and all greenhouse gases converted to CO₂-equivalent. **Starting Principle:** Each GHG has a different global warming potential (GWP). The IPCC AR5 standard provides GWP₁₀₀ values (100-year time horizon). $$ \text{CF}_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{\text{scope}=1}^{3} \sum_{g=1}^{G} (A_{g,\text{scope}} \times \text{EF}_{g,\text{scope}} \times \text{GWP}_g) $$ **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Identify emission sources and classify into Scopes.} \quad & \text{Scope 1 = Direct (e.g., company fleet, boilers);} \\ & \text{Scope 2 = Indirect from purchased electricity;} \\ & \text{Scope 3 = Value-chain indirect (suppliers, use-phase, end-of-life).} \\[6pt] \text{Step 2: Collect Activity Data } A_{g,\text{scope}}. \quad & \text{e.g., 500,000 kWh of grid electricity, 12,000 litres of diesel.} \\[6pt] \text{Step 3: Apply Emission Factor } \text{EF}_{g,\text{scope}}. \quad & \text{e.g., grid EF in India = 0.82 kg CO}_2\text{/kWh (CEA 2023).} \\[6pt] \text{Step 4: Convert to CO}_2\text{-equivalent using GWP}. \quad & \text{For CH}_4\text{: GWP} = 28; \text{ For N}_2\text{O: GWP} = 265. \\[6pt] \text{Step 5: Aggregate over all sources.} \quad & \text{Sum all } (A \times \text{EF} \times \text{GWP}) \text{ terms.} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example — Sample Manufacturing Unit:** | Source | Scope | Activity (A) | Emission Factor (EF) | GWP | Emission (tCO₂e) | |--------|-------|--------------|----------------------|------|-------------------| | Diesel in forklifts | 1 | 12,000 L | 2.68 kg CO₂/L | 1 | 32.16 | | Grid electricity | 2 | 500,000 kWh | 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh | 1 | 410.00 | | Methane leakage | 1 | 50 kg | 28 kg CO₂/kg | 28 | 1.40 | | Upstream transport | 3 | 100,000 tonne-km | 0.062 kg CO₂/(t·km) | 1 | 6.20 | $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CF}_{\text{Total}} &= 32.16 + 410.00 + 1.40 + 6.20 \\ &= 449.76 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e per year} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Social Return on Investment (SROI) for a Sustainability Project SROI monetises social and environmental outcomes to compare with investment cost. **Fundamental Relationship:** $$ \text{SROI Ratio} = \frac{\text{Present Value of Total Benefits (PVB)}}{\text{Present Value of Total Inputs (PVC)}} $$ **Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Identify stakeholders and outcomes.} \quad & \text{e.g., workers trained, CO}_2\text{ avoided, water saved.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Quantify outputs in physical units.} \quad & \text{e.g., 200 workers trained, 1,500 tCO}_2\text{e avoided/year.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Apply financial proxies (monetisation rates).} \quad & \text{Proxy}_1\text{ = wage uplift ₹30,000/worker/year;} \\ & \text{Proxy}_2\text{ = carbon price ₹1,500/tCO}_2\text{e.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Compute gross present value of benefits.} \quad & \text{PVB} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\sum (\text{Outcome}_i \times \text{Proxy}_i)}{(1+r)^t} \\[4pt] \text{Step 5: Compute present value of investment.} \quad & \text{PVC} = I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t} \\[4pt] \text{Step 6: Calculate SROI ratio.} \quad & \text{SROI} = \frac{\text{PVB}}{\text{PVC}} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Example — Solar PV Installation in a College:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Investment: } & I_0 = ₹50,00,000; \text{ Annual O\&M } C_t = ₹2,00,000; \\ & r = 8\%; \text{ Project life } T = 10 \text{ years.} \\ \text{Annual benefits:} & \\ & B_{\text{electricity saved}} = ₹8,00,000 \\ & B_{\text{carbon credit}} = 80 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e} \times ₹1,500 = ₹1,20,000 \\ & B_{\text{education value}} = ₹2,00,000 \\ & B_{\text{total}} = ₹11,20,000 \text{ per year} \\[4pt] \text{PVB} & = 11,20,000 \times \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} \\ & = 11,20,000 \times 6.7101 \\ & = ₹75,15,312 \\[4pt] \text{PVC} & = 50,00,000 + 2,00,000 \times 6.7101 \\ & = 50,00,000 + 13,42,020 \\ & = ₹63,42,020 \\[4pt] \text{SROI} & = \frac{75,15,312}{63,42,020} = 1.185 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** An SROI of **1.185** means for every ₹1 invested, **₹1.185 of social, environmental, and economic value** is created — a positive, justified sustainability investment. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sustainable Business Models | Business Model | Core Principle | Revenue Logic | Example Company | Primary SDG | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Circular Economy** | Eliminate waste, circulate materials | Product-as-a-service, refurbishment | Philips (lighting-as-service) | SDG 12 | | **Sharing Economy** | Utilisation over ownership | Platform fees, subscription | Uber, Airbnb | SDG 11 | | **Renewable Energy** | Decarbonise energy supply | Power Purchase Agreements (PPA) | Tata Power Solar, Adani Green | SDG 7 | | **Green Chemistry** | Design safer chemicals | Premium pricing for non-toxic products | BASF | SDG 9, 12 | | **Fair Trade** | Ethical supply chains | Premium for certified products | Fair Trade India | SDG 1, 8 | | **Sustainable Agriculture** | Regenerative farming | Premium organic pricing | ITC e-Choupal | SDG 2, 15 | | **Impact Investing** | Invest with dual returns | Capital appreciation + social dividend | Acumen Fund | SDG 17 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SUSTAINABLE BUSINESS] --> B[ECONOMIC Pillar] A --> C[ENVIRONMENTAL Pillar] A --> D[SOCIAL Pillar] A --> E[GOVERNANCE Layer Cross-Cutting] B --> B1[Profitability and ROI] B --> B2[Long-term Value Creation] B --> B3[Risk-adjusted Returns] C --> C1[Carbon Footprint Reduction] C --> C2[Water and Waste Stewardship] C --> C3[Biodiversity Protection] D --> D1[Employee Welfare and Safety] D --> D2[Community Engagement] D --> D3[Human Rights Compliance] E --> E1[Board Oversight] E --> E2[Ethics and Transparency] E --> E3[Stakeholder Reporting] A --> F[INTEGRATED VALUE CREATION] F --> F1[Resilient Business Model] F --> F2[Enhanced Brand Equity] F --> F3[Lower Cost of Capital] ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Sustainable Business Value Chain Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Raw Material Extraction] --> P2[Sustainable Sourcing] P2 --> P3[Clean Production] P3 --> P4[Green Logistics] P4 --> P5[Responsible Marketing] P5 --> P6[Consumer Use Phase] P6 --> P7[End-of-Life Recovery] P7 --> P8[Remanufacturing and Recycling] P8 --> P2 P2 -.Material Loop.-> P2 P3 -.Energy Loop.-> P3 style P2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1b5e20 style P3 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1b5e20 style P7 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a16207 style P8 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a16207 ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: ESG Decision-Making and Reporting Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Identify ESG Material Topics] --> S2[Set KPIs and Targets] S2 --> S3[Data Collection and Measurement] S3 --> S4[Third-Party Assurance] S4 --> S5[Disclose via GRI / SASB / TCFD] S5 --> S6[Stakeholder Feedback] S6 --> S7[Strategic Review] S7 --> S1 S5 --> S8[Integrated Annual Report] S5 --> S9[BRSR for SEBI Listed Companies] S5 --> S10[CDP and MSCI Submissions] ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4: Mapping Business Activities to UN SDGs ```mermaid mindmap root((Sustainable Business
SDG Alignment)) SDG 7 Renewable Energy Adoption Energy Efficiency SDG 8 Decent Work Conditions Fair Wages SDG 9 Sustainable Infrastructure Clean Technology SDG 12 Circular Production Reduced Material Footprint SDG 13 Net-Zero Pathway Climate Risk Disclosure ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] Define the Triple Bottom Line (TBL) concept. List its three pillars.** **Model Answer:** The **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)**, coined by **John Elkington in 1994**, is a sustainability framework that expands the traditional reporting focus from financial profit alone to include environmental and social performance. The three pillars are: 1. **Economic (Profit):** Financial performance, value creation, and long-term economic viability. 2. **Environmental (Planet):** Ecological impact, resource efficiency, and pollution prevention. 3. **Social (People):** Stakeholder welfare, labour rights, and community development. The TBL asserts that a business is only truly successful when it simultaneously performs well across all three dimensions, ensuring intergenerational equity. *(3 marks)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] What is ESG reporting? Name any two global ESG disclosure frameworks.** **Model Answer:** **ESG Reporting** is the disclosure of a company's performance and risks related to **Environmental, Social, and Governance** factors to investors, regulators, and stakeholders. It enables transparency, comparability, and informed capital allocation decisions. Two global ESG disclosure frameworks: 1. **GRI (Global Reporting Initiative)** – Universal standards for sustainability reporting. 2. **SASB (Sustainability Accounting Standards Board)** – Industry-specific materiality-based standards. *(Honourable mentions: TCFD for climate risk, BRSR for Indian SEBI-listed firms, CDP for carbon disclosure.)* *(3 marks)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **Q1. (a)** Explain the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework in detail. Discuss how a manufacturing firm can integrate TBL into its operations, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **Circular Economy**? Explain the key business models used to operationalise circularity in industries. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q1 (a):** **Introduction:** The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework, introduced by John Elkington in 1994, asserts that businesses must measure their success not only by financial returns but also by their **environmental** and **social** impact. It shifts the lens from shareholder primacy to **stakeholder value**. **Three Pillars:** 1. **Economic Profit:** Long-term financial sustainability, ROI, shareholder value. 2. **Environmental Planet:** Minimising carbon, water, and waste footprint; preserving biodiversity. 3. **Social People:** Employee welfare, fair labour, community engagement. **Integration in a Manufacturing Firm — Practical Examples:** | Stage | Traditional Practice | TBL-Integrated Practice | |---|---|---| | Sourcing | Cheapest raw materials | FSC-certified, fair-trade, low-mile suppliers | | Production | High energy consumption | Solar rooftops, ISO 14001 EMS, lean manufacturing | | Workforce | Low wages, unsafe conditions | Living wages, OSHA-compliant safety, skilling | | Product End-of-Life | Landfill disposal | Take-back, refurbish, recycling program | | Reporting | Financial statements only | Integrated Report following GRI Standards | **Example:** A textile manufacturer in Tirupur adopting TBL would install a Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) plant (Environmental), ensure fair wages and women's safety (Social), and reduce operational cost through water reuse, increasing EBITDA (Economic) — a virtuous cycle. **Conclusion:** TBL is not an additional cost; it is a strategic redesign that converts sustainability into competitive advantage. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q1 (b):** **Definition:** A **Circular Economy** is a regenerative industrial model where resources are kept in use for as long as possible, maximum value is extracted during use, then products are recovered and regenerated at end-of-life, contrasting with the linear "take-make-dispose" model. **Key Business Models for Circularity:** 1. **Circular Supplies:** Replacing virgin materials with bio-based, renewable, or fully recycled inputs (e.g., Coca-Cola's PlantBottle). 2. **Resource Recovery:** Turning waste streams into valuable inputs (e.g., *Used Coffee Grounds → Biofuel*). 3. **Product Life Extension:** Refurbishing, repairing, reselling (e.g., Apple Renew, Refurbished iPhones). 4. **Sharing Platforms:** Utilisation over ownership (e.g., Zipcar, OLX). 5. **Product-as-a-Service (PaaS):** Customers pay for use, not ownership (e.g., Philips "Pay-per-Lux," Rolls-Royce "Power-by-the-Hour" jet engines). 6. **Industrial Symbiosis:** Waste from one industry becomes input for another (e.g., Kalundborg Symbiosis in Denmark). **Indian Context:** The **Plastic Waste Management Rules (2022)** and the **Vehicle Scrappage Policy (2021)** are strong policy drivers pushing firms toward circular models. **Conclusion:** Circular economy business models decouple economic growth from resource consumption, making sustainability a structural feature of the business model itself. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q2. (a)** Discuss the **Sustainable Supply Chain Management (SSCM)** framework. Explain its key drivers, barriers, and best practices. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Green Marketing**? Explain the **4Ps of Green Marketing** and discuss the ethical concern of *greenwashing*. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q2 (a):** **Definition:** **Sustainable Supply Chain Management (SSCM)** is the integration of environmental, social, and economic considerations into the end-to-end supply chain — from raw material extraction to end-of-life management — to enhance long-term resilience and stakeholder value. **Key Drivers of SSCM:** 1. **Regulatory Pressure:** SEBI BRSR, EU CSRD, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM). 2. **Consumer Demand:** Growing preference for ethical and eco-labelled products. 3. **Investor Expectations:** ESG-integrated capital allocation. 4. **Risk Management:** Climate, geopolitical, and reputational risks in supply chains. 5. **Cost Optimisation:** Energy efficiency, waste reduction, logistics optimisation. **Major Barriers:** - Lack of supplier transparency (especially in Tier 2/3 suppliers). - Higher upfront costs of sustainable sourcing. - Inconsistent global standards. - Resistance to change within procurement teams. **Best Practices:** - **Supplier Code of Conduct** aligned with UN Global Compact. - **Supplier Audits & Certifications** (ISO 14001, SA8000). - **Blockchain for traceability** (e.g., De Beers for conflict-free diamonds). - **Carbon Disclosure in Scope 3 emissions.** - **Local sourcing** to reduce logistics footprint. **Conclusion:** SSCM is no longer optional; it is a strategic imperative for resilience, compliance, and brand trust. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q2 (b):** **Definition:** **Green Marketing** is the promotion of products and services based on their environmental benefits — such as reduced carbon footprint, recyclability, biodegradability, or energy efficiency. It involves production, packaging, pricing, and promotion decisions that align with ecological responsibility. **The 4Ps of Green Marketing:** | Element | Green Approach | Example | |---|---|---| | **Product** | Eco-design, minimal packaging, durable, recyclable | Toyota Prius (hybrid vehicle) | | **Price** | Premium pricing for verified green attributes; long-term cost savings framing | Energy-efficient 5-star ACs | | **Place** | Local sourcing, low-carbon logistics, e-commerce with consolidated shipping | Bigbasket's eco-deliveries | | **Promotion** | Truthful green claims, eco-labels (FSSAI Green Dot, EU Ecolabel) | Tata Steel Pravesh's "Eco-Friendly Doors" | **Ethical Concern — Greenwashing:** **Greenwashing** is the practice of making misleading or unsubstantiated environmental claims to appear more sustainable than the company actually is. Common greenwashing tactics include: - Vague claims like *"eco-friendly"* without proof. - Selective disclosure of positives while hiding negatives. - Imagery overload (green leaves, earth imagery) without substantive action. - Irrelevant certifications. **Regulatory Response:** The **Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI)** issued Guidelines for Environmental Marketing Claims (2022). The **Competition Commission of India (CCI)** and **SEBI** monitor greenwashing in securities markets. **Conclusion:** Green marketing must be backed by verifiable life-cycle evidence; otherwise it destroys consumer trust and attracts legal penalties. *(7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Common Errors:** > 1. **Confusing TBL with CSR:** CSR is a *subset activity* (often philanthropic), while TBL is an *integrated operating philosophy*. Students often lose 2 marks by conflating the two. > 2. **No Formulae for "Management" Topics:** Even though this is a management topic, the KTU examiner expects **at least one quantitative metric** (e.g., Carbon Footprint, SROI, Eco-Efficiency). Writing only qualitative answers caps your score at ~10/14. > 3. **Skipping Indian Context:** Always include **Indian references** — BRSR (SEBI), Swachh Bharat, Unnat Bharat Abhiyan, Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme, FAME India. Purely Western examples lose marks. > 4. **No Mention of SDGs:** Any 14-mark question on sustainable business **must** explicitly reference the relevant UN SDG number(s) for full credit. > 5. **Avoid One-Sided View:** Sustainability has a *cost-benefit trade-off*. Acknowledge the tensions (e.g., higher upfront cost, greenwashing risks) — examiners reward critical thinking. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ Sustainable Business = **Triple Bottom Line** (Profit + Planet + People) — coined by **John Elkington, 1994**. - ✅ Core frameworks to remember: **TBL, ESG, GRI, SASB, TCFD, ISO 14001, ISO 26000, BRSR (India)**. - ✅ **United Nations 17 SDGs (2015)** — businesses must map activities to at least 2–3 material SDGs. - ✅ **Carbon Footprint Formula:** $CF = \sum (A_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$ — measured in **tCO₂e**. - ✅ **Eco-Efficiency** = Value ÷ Environmental Impact (higher is better). - ✅ **SROI Ratio** = PVB ÷ PVC; values >1 indicate net positive sustainability investment. - ✅ **Circular Economy** models: Circular Supplies, Resource Recovery, Life Extension, Sharing Platforms, PaaS, Industrial Symbiosis. - ✅ **SSCM** is end-to-end: raw material → production → logistics → consumer → end-of-life. - ✅ **Green Marketing** = 4Ps + ethics; **Greenwashing** = false/unverified environmental claims — punishable under ASCI guidelines. - ✅ Indian policy drivers: **BRSR (SEBI 2023), PAT Scheme, FAME India, GRIHA, Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022**. - ✅ ESG leaders have **lower cost of capital** and **better long-term returns** — empirical and now mainstream finance fact. - ✅ Always link answers to **specific SDG numbers** — this is a high-scoring examiner technique. ---

topics.title: Green Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Green Technology (Green Tech / Clean Technology / Environmental Technology)** is a discipline of engineering and applied science that focuses on designing, developing, and implementing products, processes, and systems that **reduce the negative environmental impact** of human activities, **conserve natural resources**, and promote **sustainable development** across industrial, commercial, and domestic sectors.

According to the KTU 2024 OESFT721 syllabus, Green Technology is positioned as the practical engineering arm of sustainability — the application arm where theory, policy, and innovation converge to deliver measurable ecological and economic outcomes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Exam Ready):**
> *"Green Technology refers to the use of science, engineering, and innovative processes to create products and services that are environmentally benign throughout their life cycle — from raw material extraction to disposal or recycling — while delivering economic and social value."*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Green Technology

Green Technology is anchored on the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework, which is also the foundation of sustainable engineering:

| Pillar | Focus | Practical Engineering Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental** | Reduce ecological footprint | Carbon capture, zero-emission vehicles, biodegradable materials |
| **Economic** | Ensure cost-effectiveness & profit | Life-cycle cost reduction, energy-efficient manufacturing |
| **Social** | Improve quality of life & equity | Clean water access, green jobs, health improvements |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Houseplant" View of Green Tech:**
> Imagine your home as the planet Earth. For years, you have left the windows shut, used chemical cleaners, burned trash indoors, and ignored the leaking roof. The air is bad, the walls are stained, and you are getting sick. **Green Technology is like opening the windows, switching to natural cleaners, composting, and fixing the roof with sustainable materials.** You are not abandoning the house — you are *living in it more wisely* so it lasts longer.
>
> In engineering terms: we do not stop building factories, cars, or cities — we build them **smarter, cleaner, and renewable-powered** so that the Earth (the house) can keep supporting us for generations.

### 1.4 Why Green Technology Matters — Key Drivers

- **Climate emergency** — global average temperature has risen by approximately **1.1 °C** above pre-industrial levels (IPCC 2021 baseline).
- **Resource depletion** — many critical minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Rare Earths) face supply risk within 30–50 years.
- **Regulatory pressure** — UN SDGs, Paris Agreement, EU Green Deal, India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission.
- **Market opportunity** — the global green technology and sustainability market is valued in the **multi-trillion USD** range, growing double-digit annually.

### 1.5 Scope and Domains of Green Technology

Green Technology is a vast umbrella covering multiple engineering and scientific domains:

1. **Renewable & Clean Energy** — solar, wind, hydro, tidal, geothermal, green hydrogen, nuclear (advanced).
2. **Green Building & Sustainable Construction** — LEED-certified buildings, rammed earth, green roofs, low-carbon cement.
3. **Sustainable Transportation** — EVs, hydrogen fuel-cell vehicles, biofuels, public transit redesign.
4. **Waste Management & Circular Economy** — recycling, upcycling, e-waste handling, waste-to-energy.
5. **Green Chemistry & Materials** — biodegradable plastics, bio-based polymers, non-toxic solvents.
6. **Sustainable Agriculture & Food Tech** — precision farming, vertical farming, lab-grown meat.
7. **Water Purification & Conservation** — desalination, rainwater harvesting, smart irrigation.
8. **Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage (CCUS)** — direct air capture, biochar, mineral sequestration.
9. **Green IT & Computing** — energy-efficient data centers, green coding, e-waste management.
10. **Bioremediation & Ecosystem Restoration** — microbial cleanup of oil spills, reforestation tech.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principles of Green Technology

Green Technology is not a single invention — it is a **design philosophy guided by 7 universally accepted principles**. These principles are extremely high-yield for KTU ESE short-answer questions.

**Principle 1 — Life-Cycle Thinking (Cradle-to-Cradle)**

Every product has a *birth* (raw material extraction), *life* (manufacture, use), and *death* (disposal). Green tech engineers must consider environmental impact at **every stage**, not just the use phase.

**Principle 2 — Pollution Prevention at Source**

It is cheaper and more effective to prevent waste than to treat it after creation. Example: replacing a toxic solvent in paint *before* it enters the waste stream, rather than installing a costly effluent treatment plant later.

**Principle 3 — Resource Efficiency & Conservation**

Doing **more with less**. The standard engineering metric is captured in the formula:

$$\text{Resource Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Useful Output (Value)}}{\text{Resource Input (Material + Energy + Water)}}$$

A higher ratio = greener process.

**Principle 4 — Renewable Feedstock & Energy**

Replace finite resources (coal, petroleum, mined minerals) with replenishable ones (solar radiation, wind, biomass, tides). The renewable share in the global energy mix is targeted to rise from ~30% (current) toward 60–80% by mid-century.

**Principle 5 — Design for Disassembly & Recycling (DfD/DfR)**

Products should be designed so components can be **easily separated, recovered, and reprocessed** at end-of-life. This is the engineering backbone of the **circular economy**.

**Principle 6 — Mimic Natural Systems (Biomimicry)**

Nature has spent ~3.8 billion years optimizing. Engineers study biological systems (e.g., lotus leaf self-cleaning → hydrophobic coatings, termite mound ventilation → passive cooling buildings).

**Principle 7 — Precautionary & Systems Thinking**

If an action or technology has a suspected risk of harm, precautionary measures should be taken *before* conclusive scientific proof. Also, every technology is part of a larger socio-ecological system — assess ripple effects, not just direct impacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Board Exam:** **"LIFE-PRM"** → Life-cycle, In-source prevention, Feedstock renewable, Efficiency, Precaution, Recycling, Mimicry.

### 2.2 The 5R / 6R / 7R Waste Hierarchy (Extended Green Tech Framework)

This is one of the most frequently tested topics in OESFT721. Green Technology is fundamentally about *moving up the hierarchy*.

```
Level 7 (Best)  →  REFUSE      — do not generate the waste in the first place
Level 6         →  RETHINK     — redesign systems (e.g., product-as-a-service)
Level 5         →  REDUCE      — minimize use of materials/energy
Level 4         →  REUSE       — use items again for same or different purpose
Level 3         →  REPAIR      — fix instead of replacing
Level 2         →  RECYCLE     — reprocess into new raw materials
Level 1 (Worst) →  RECOVERY    — recover energy only (e.g., incineration w/ energy)
                  LANDFILL     — last resort
```

### 2.3 Green Technology Evaluation Metrics (KTU Formula Sheet)

The following formulas and units are **must-know** for numerical and short-answer questions:

**A. Carbon Footprint**

Total greenhouse gases (expressed as CO₂-equivalent) emitted directly or indirectly over a product's life cycle.

$$CF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( A_i \times GWP_i \right)$$

Where:
- $CF$ = Carbon Footprint in **kg CO₂e**
- $A_i$ = mass (kg) of greenhouse gas $i$
- $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential of gas $i$ (CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265 — IPCC AR5 100-year values)
- $n$ = number of distinct GHGs

**B. Energy Payback Time (EPBT)**

Time required for a renewable energy system to generate the **equivalent amount of energy** consumed during its manufacture, installation, and decommissioning.

$$EPBT \; [\text{years}] = \frac{E_{\text{embodied}} \; [\text{kWh}]}{E_{\text{annual generated}} \; [\text{kWh/year}]}$$

Typical values: rooftop solar PV ≈ 1.5–3 years; onshore wind ≈ 6–12 months.

**C. Energy Returned on Energy Invested (EROEI / EROI)**

$$EROEI = \frac{E_{\text{useful output}}}{E_{\text{input}}}$$

A sustainable energy source should have **EROEI > 5** ideally. Wind: ~18–25; Solar PV: ~10–15; Coal: ~30–50 (declining); Tar sands: ~3–5.

**D. Water Footprint**

$$WF = WF_{\text{blue}} + WF_{\text{green}} + WF_{\text{grey}}$$

Where blue = surface/ground water, green = rainwater, grey = polluted water (volume needed to dilute pollutants to acceptable standards).

**E. Eco-Efficiency (World Business Council for Sustainable Development)**

$$\text{Eco-Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Product/Service Value}}{\text{Environmental Impact}}$$

**F. Material Circularity Indicator (MCI) — Ellen MacArthur Foundation**

$$MCI = 1 - \left( \frac{F_{\text{linear}} + F_{\text{waste}}}{2} \right) \times \left( \frac{0.9}{L_{\text{avg}}/L_{\text{max}}} \right)$$

Higher MCI → more circular (range 0 to 1).

**G. Net Present Value (NPV) for Green Investments**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$$

Where $B_t$ = benefits (energy savings + carbon credits), $C_t$ = costs, $r$ = discount rate.

**H. Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** — most important for comparing green tech

$$LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{I_t + M_t + F_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

Where $I_t$ = investment, $M_t$ = O&M, $F_t$ = fuel, $E_t$ = energy generated in year $t$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Summary Table

| Formula | Name | Unit | Board Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $CF = \sum A_i \cdot GWP_i$ | Carbon Footprint | **kg CO₂e** | High (numericals) |
| $EPBT = E_{\text{emb}} / E_{\text{annual}}$ | Energy Payback Time | **years** | High |
| $EROEI = E_{\text{out}} / E_{\text{in}}$ | Energy Return on Investment | dimensionless | Medium |
| $LCOE = \sum \frac{I_t+M_t+F_t}{(1+r)^t} \big/ \sum \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Levelized Cost of Energy | **$/kWh** or **₹/kWh** | Very High |
| $WF = WF_{\text{blue}} + WF_{\text{green}} + WF_{\text{grey}}$ | Water Footprint | **L/kg or m³/tonne** | Medium |
| $MCI \in [0,1]$ | Material Circularity Indicator | dimensionless | Medium |
| $\eta_{\text{eco}} = \text{Value}/\text{Impact}$ | Eco-Efficiency | varies | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" renewable technologies, the **LCOE** and **EROEI** are the two gold-standard metrics. Always cite them with units.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Sector | Green Technology Used | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy** | Solar PV, Onshore/Offshore wind, Green H₂ | ~30% global electricity from renewables (2024) |
| **Transport** | Lithium-ion EVs, hydrogen FCVs | EVs reduce lifecycle GHG by ~60% vs ICE |
| **Buildings** | Passive design, green roofs, BIPV | LEED buildings use ~25% less energy |
| **Industry** | Carbon capture, electrified furnaces | Hard-to-abate sectors (steel, cement) |
| **Agriculture** | Drip irrigation, precision farming | Cuts water use by up to 50% |
| **IT** | Liquid-cooled data centers, PUE < 1.2 | Hyperscale clouds targeting carbon-neutral by 2030 |
| **Materials** | Bio-plastics, mycelium packaging | PLA, PHA replacing single-use PET |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating the Carbon Footprint of a Coal Power Plant

> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
> A 100 MW coal-fired power plant operates at 60% plant load factor (PLF) for 8000 hours/year. The coal has a calorific value of 5000 kcal/kg and carbon content of 60%. Combustion is complete, but with a CH₄ emission factor of 0.05 kg CH₄ per MWh of electricity. Calculate the **annual carbon footprint in tonnes CO₂-equivalent**. (Use GWP values: CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28.)

**Step 1 — Calculate annual electrical energy generated**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{rated}} \times PLF \times t$$

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 100 \;\text{MW} \times 0.60 \times 8000 \;\text{h}$$

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 480{,}000 \;\text{MWh/year} = 4.8 \times 10^{5} \;\text{MWh/year}$$

*Logic: Plant generates electricity only at 60% of its capacity for 8000 hours in a year.*

**Step 2 — Calculate mass of CH₄ emitted per year**

$$m_{CH_4} = EF_{CH_4} \times E_{\text{annual}}$$

$$m_{CH_4} = 0.05 \;\text{kg/MWh} \times 4.8 \times 10^{5} \;\text{MWh}$$

$$m_{CH_4} = 24{,}000 \;\text{kg/yr} = 24 \;\text{tonnes/yr}$$

*Logic: Multiply emission factor (kg CH₄ per MWh) by total electricity generated.*

**Step 3 — Convert mass of CH₄ to CO₂-equivalent**

$$CF_{CH_4} = m_{CH_4} \times GWP_{CH_4}$$

$$CF_{CH_4} = 24 \;\text{tonnes} \times 28$$

$$CF_{CH_4} = 672 \;\text{tonnes CO}_2\text{e/yr}$$

*Logic: Each kg of CH₄ traps 28× the heat of one kg of CO₂ over 100 years (IPCC AR5).*

**Step 4 — Calculate mass of coal consumed per year**

$$m_{\text{coal}} = \frac{E_{\text{annual}} \times 1000}{\text{CV} \times \eta_{\text{thermal}}}$$

We assume thermal efficiency $\eta_{\text{thermal}} \approx 0.35$ (typical sub-critical). Convert CV to kWh/kg:

$$CV = 5000 \;\text{kcal/kg} \times \frac{1.163 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kWh}}{1 \;\text{kcal}} = 5.815 \;\text{kWh/kg}$$

*Logic: 1 kcal = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kWh (mechanical equivalent of heat).*

$$m_{\text{coal}} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{5} \;\text{MWh} \times 1000 \;\text{kWh/MWh}}{5.815 \;\text{kWh/kg} \times 0.35}$$

$$m_{\text{coal}} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{8}}{2.035} \;\text{kg/yr}$$

$$m_{\text{coal}} \approx 2.36 \times 10^{8} \;\text{kg/yr} = 2.36 \times 10^{5} \;\text{tonnes/yr}$$

*Logic: Divide total energy output by energy density of fuel × plant efficiency.*

**Step 5 — Calculate mass of CO₂ from coal combustion**

$$m_{CO_2} = m_{\text{coal}} \times C_{\text{fraction}} \times \frac{44}{12}$$

*Logic: Of the 60% carbon in coal, each 12 kg of C becomes 44 kg of CO₂ on combustion (mass ratio = 44/12).*

$$m_{CO_2} = 2.36 \times 10^{5} \times 0.60 \times \frac{44}{12}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 2.36 \times 10^{5} \times 0.60 \times 3.667$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 5.19 \times 10^{5} \;\text{tonnes CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

**Step 6 — Total annual carbon footprint**

$$CF_{\text{total}} = m_{CO_2} + CF_{CH_4}$$

$$CF_{\text{total}} = 5.19 \times 10^{5} + 672 \approx 5.20 \times 10^{5} \;\text{tonnes CO}_2\text{e/yr}$$

*Logic: Add the CO₂ direct emissions and the CH₄ emissions (already converted to CO₂e).*

**Final Answer:** The coal plant emits approximately **5.2 × 10⁵ tonnes CO₂-equivalent per year** (~520,000 t CO₂e/yr).

**Sanity Check:** Typical benchmark is ~0.9–1.0 t CO₂ per MWh for coal.  
$0.95 \;\text{t/MWh} \times 4.8 \times 10^{5}\;\text{MWh} = 4.56 \times 10^{5}\;\text{tonnes}$.  
Our calculated 5.2 × 10⁵ is in the same order — ✓ correct.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Solar PV: LCOE and EPBT Comparison

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 1 MW rooftop solar PV system in Kerala has the following data:
> - Total installed cost: ₹4 crore
> - Annual O&M cost: ₹2 lakh (constant for 25 years)
> - Annual energy generation: 14 lakh kWh (year-1) with 0.5% annual degradation
> - Discount rate: 8%
> - Embodied energy: 14,00,000 kWh
> - Lifetime: 25 years
>
> Calculate (a) Energy Payback Time, (b) Levelized Cost of Energy.

**Part (a) — Energy Payback Time**

$$EPBT = \frac{E_{\text{embodied}}}{E_{\text{annual, avg}}}$$

Approximate average annual generation accounting for degradation:

$$E_{\text{avg}} \approx E_1 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.005 \times 24}{2}\right) = 14 \times 10^{5} \times (1 - 0.06)$$

$$E_{\text{avg}} = 14 \times 10^{5} \times 0.94 \approx 13.16 \times 10^{5} \;\text{kWh/yr}$$

*Logic: Average degradation over 25-year life ≈ (0.5% × 24)/2 = 6%.*

$$EPBT = \frac{14 \times 10^{5}}{13.16 \times 10^{5}} \approx 1.06 \;\text{years}$$

**Final Answer:** Energy Payback Time ≈ **1.06 years** (very fast — system pays back its energy debt in just over 1 year).

**Part (b) — Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**

$$LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{I_t + M_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$$

Numerator (present value of costs):

$$PV_{\text{cost}} = I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{25} \frac{M}{(1.08)^t} = 4 \times 10^{7} + 2 \times 10^{5} \times \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-25}}{0.08}$$

Compute annuity factor:

$$AF = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-25}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - 0.1460}{0.08} = \frac{0.8540}{0.08} \approx 10.675$$

$$PV_{\text{cost}} = 4 \times 10^{7} + 2 \times 10^{5} \times 10.675$$

$$PV_{\text{cost}} = 4 \times 10^{7} + 2.135 \times 10^{6} = 4.2135 \times 10^{7}\;\text{₹}$$

Denominator (present value of energy):

Energy in year $t$: $E_t = 14 \times 10^{5} \times (1 - 0.005)^{t-1}$

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{25} \frac{14 \times 10^{5} \times (0.995)^{t-1}}{(1.08)^t}$$

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = 14 \times 10^{5} \times \frac{1}{1.08} \times \sum_{t=1}^{25} \left(\frac{0.995}{1.08}\right)^{t-1}$$

Effective discount rate on energy: $d = (1.08/0.995) - 1 \approx 0.0854$ → use:

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = \frac{14 \times 10^{5}}{1.08} \times \frac{1 - (0.995/1.08)^{25}}{1 - (0.995/1.08)}$$

$$0.995/1.08 = 0.9213$$

$$(0.9213)^{25} \approx 0.1307$$

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = \frac{14 \times 10^{5}}{1.08} \times \frac{1 - 0.1307}{1 - 0.9213} = 12.96 \times 10^{5} \times \frac{0.8693}{0.0787}$$

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = 12.96 \times 10^{5} \times 11.05 \approx 14.32 \times 10^{6} \;\text{kWh}$$

**LCOE:**

$$LCOE = \frac{4.2135 \times 10^{7}}{14.32 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2.94 \;\text{₹/kWh}$$

**Final Answer:** LCOE ≈ **₹2.94/kWh** (competitive with grid tariffs in Kerala in 2024).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Comparing Green Technologies

This matrix is **frequently asked in KTU ESE 14-mark questions** as a comparison.

| Parameter | Solar PV | Onshore Wind | Biomass | Geothermal | Green Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LCOE (USD/MWh, 2024)** | 40–60 | 30–50 | 60–120 | 50–100 | 100–200 |
| **EROEI** | 10–15 | 18–25 | 3–8 | 10–20 | <2 (current) |
| **Capacity Factor** | 15–25% | 25–40% | 60–85% | 80–95% | n/a (storage) |
| **Intermittency** | High (day/night) | Medium (wind variability) | Low | None | None (with storage) |
| **Land Footprint** | Medium | Low–Medium | High | Very Low | Low |
| **Water Use** | Very Low | Very Low | High | Medium | High (electrolysis) |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (commercial) | 9 (commercial) | 8 | 8–9 | 6–7 (pilot) |
| **Best Use Case** | Distributed & utility | Utility scale | Baseload | Baseload | Industry, transport, storage |

*TRL = Technology Readiness Level (NASA scale, 1 = basic research, 9 = fully commercial).*

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Green Technology. List any four of its guiding principles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Green Technology is the application of science, engineering, and innovative processes to develop products, processes, and systems that **reduce environmental impact**, **conserve natural resources**, and support **sustainable development** across the entire life cycle.

**Any four principles (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Life-Cycle Thinking** — assess impact from cradle to grave.
2. **Pollution Prevention at Source** — eliminate waste before creation.
3. **Use of Renewable Feedstock & Energy** — replace finite resources.
4. **Design for Disassembly & Recycling** — enable circular material flows.

> *(Other acceptable: Resource Efficiency, Biomimicry, Precautionary Principle.)*

---

#### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between **Carbon Footprint** and **Ecological Footprint**.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Carbon Footprint | Ecological Footprint |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Total GHG emissions expressed as kg CO₂-equivalent | Biologically productive land/water area required to sustain a population's consumption |
| **Unit** | **kg CO₂e** or **t CO₂e** | **global hectares (gha)** |
| **Scope** | Climate change impact only | Multiple impact categories (carbon, food, housing, goods, services) |
| **Introduced by** | Global Carbon Project / Carbon Trust | Mathis Wackernagel & William Rees (1990s) |

> Carbon footprint is essentially a **subset** of the broader ecological footprint.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **seven principles of Green Technology**. Illustrate with suitable engineering examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 50 MW solar PV farm in Rajasthan has the following specifications:
- Total installed cost: ₹200 crore
- Annual O&M: ₹1.2 crore (constant for 25 years)
- Annual degradation: 0.7%/year
- Year-1 generation: 9 crore kWh
- Discount rate: 9%
- Lifespan: 25 years

Calculate (i) **Energy Payback Time** (assume embodied energy = 1.0 × 10⁸ kWh), and (ii) **LCOE**. Compare with grid tariff of ₹4.5/kWh.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) The Seven Principles of Green Technology [7 marks]**

Each principle is to be explained in **2–3 lines with a clear engineering example** (≈ 1 mark per principle).

1. **Life-Cycle Thinking** [1 mark]
   The engineer considers environmental impact at every stage of a product's life — raw material extraction, manufacturing, transport, use, and end-of-life. *Example:* Life-Cycle Assessment (LCA) of an electric vehicle showing that 40% of its lifetime emissions occur during battery manufacturing, justifying the push for renewable-powered gigafactories.

2. **Pollution Prevention at Source** [1 mark]
   Eliminate pollutants before they are generated, rather than treating them afterward. *Example:* Replacing hexavalent chromium electroplating with trivalent chromium or with physical vapor deposition in the automotive industry, eliminating toxic wastewater at source.

3. **Resource Efficiency & Conservation** [1 mark]
   Achieve more output per unit of material and energy. *Example:* Using CNC-optimized nesting in sheet-metal cutting to reduce scrap from ~25% to <8%, or switching to induction furnaces that reduce specific energy consumption in steel from 7.0 to 5.5 GJ/tonne.

4. **Use of Renewable Feedstock & Energy** [1 mark]
   Substitute finite resources with replenishable ones. *Example:* Replacing petroleum feedstock with sugarcane bagasse for bio-ethanol production; powering data centers with solar PPAs (Power Purchase Agreements) instead of grid coal.

5. **Design for Disassembly & Recycling (DfD/DfR)** [1 mark]
   Products are designed so that components can be easily separated, recovered, and reprocessed. *Example:* Fairphone modular smartphone with 7 replaceable modules; Apple's iPhone 16 with screw-mounted (not glued) battery.

6. **Biomimicry** [1 mark]
   Innovation inspired by nature's strategies. *Example:* Shark-skin-inspired riblet surfaces on aircraft fuselages reduce drag and fuel burn by ~1.5%; lotus-leaf hydrophobic coatings create self-cleaning windows.

7. **Precautionary & Systems Thinking** [1 mark]
   Take preventive action against suspected risk; evaluate the whole system context, not just isolated effects. *Example:* EU's precautionary ban on certain phthalates in toys even before conclusive toxicity data; assessing the full land-use and water impact of bio-fuels, not just their tailpipe emissions.

---

**(b) LCOE and EPBT Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Average annual energy generation [2 marks]**

$$E_{\text{avg}} = E_1 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.007 \times 24}{2}\right) = 9 \times 10^{7} \times (1 - 0.084)$$

$$E_{\text{avg}} = 9 \times 10^{7} \times 0.916 \approx 8.244 \times 10^{7} \;\text{kWh/yr}$$

*Logic: Average degradation over 25 years ≈ (0.7% × 24)/2 = 8.4%.*

**Step 2 — Energy Payback Time [1 mark]**

$$EPBT = \frac{E_{\text{embodied}}}{E_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{8}}{8.244 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.21 \;\text{years}$$

*Final:* **EPBT ≈ 1.21 years.**

**Step 3 — Present value of costs [2 marks]**

$$PV_{\text{cost}} = I_0 + M \times AF_{25,9\%}$$

$$AF = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-25}}{0.09}$$

Compute: $(1.09)^{25} \approx 8.623 \Rightarrow (1.09)^{-25} \approx 0.1160$

$$AF = \frac{1 - 0.1160}{0.09} = \frac{0.8840}{0.09} \approx 9.823$$

$$PV_{\text{cost}} = 2.0 \times 10^{9} + 1.2 \times 10^{7} \times 9.823$$

$$PV_{\text{cost}} = 2.0 \times 10^{9} + 1.179 \times 10^{8} \approx 2.118 \times 10^{9}\;\text{₹}$$

**Step 4 — Present value of energy [1.5 marks]**

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = 9 \times 10^{7} \times \frac{1}{1.09} \times \frac{1 - (0.993/1.09)^{25}}{1 - (0.993/1.09)}$$

$0.993/1.09 \approx 0.9110$ → $(0.9110)^{25} \approx 0.0995$

$$PV_{\text{energy}} = \frac{9 \times 10^{7}}{1.09} \times \frac{1 - 0.0995}{1 - 0.9110} = 8.257 \times 10^{7} \times \frac{0.9005}{0.0890}$$

$$PV_{\text{energy}} \approx 8.257 \times 10^{7} \times 10.118 \approx 8.354 \times 10^{8} \;\text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 — LCOE [0.5 mark]**

$$LCOE = \frac{2.118 \times 10^{9}}{8.354 \times 10^{8}} \approx 2.54 \;\text{₹/kWh}$$

*Final:* **LCOE ≈ ₹2.54/kWh.**

**Comparison with grid tariff (₹4.5/kWh) [bonus interpretation]:** Since LCOE < grid tariff, the project is **economically viable** and contributes to **decarbonization** — a textbook win-win of green technology.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of Green Technology**. Discuss how each block contributes to sustainable engineering practice.

**(b) [7 marks]** A textile dyeing unit in Tirupur consumes 2,00,000 m³ of water/month. As a green technology intervention, a **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** system is proposed that recovers 95% water and produces 50 tonnes of dry salt (NaCl) per month as a saleable by-product.
- Cost of ZLD plant: ₹15 crore; annual O&M: ₹50 lakh; lifetime: 15 years
- Water cost saved: ₹40/m³; Salt revenue: ₹2000/tonne
- Discount rate: 10%

Calculate the **annual net cash flow** and the **simple payback period**. Comment on the sustainability impact.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Functional Architecture of Green Technology [7 marks]**

The four-layer architecture of Green Technology (refer Section 4.2 diagram) is:

**Layer 1 — Input Layer (1.5 marks)**
Provides the raw materials, energy, regulatory constraints (e.g., PCBs norms, ISO 14001, Paris Agreement NDC targets), and market/SDG-aligned demand signals to the green tech core. *Engineering example:* India targets 500 GW of non-fossil installed capacity by 2030 — this policy signal flows as the *regulatory* input.

**Layer 2 — Green Tech Core (2.5 marks — 0.625 each)**
Four sub-processes convert inputs to outputs sustainably:
- **Clean Energy Generation:** solar PV, wind, green hydrogen, geothermal.
- **Material Substitution & Efficiency:** bio-plastics, fly-ash bricks, HPC concrete.
- **Pollution Prevention & Treatment:** effluent treatment, carbon capture, biofilters.
- **Resource Recovery & Circular Loops:** e-waste recycling, composting, urban mining.

*Each sub-process reduces at least one environmental impact category.*

**Layer 3 — Output Layer (1.5 marks)**
The engineered outputs are:
- **Green products and services** — LEED-certified buildings, EVs, biodegradable packaging.
- **Reduced emissions and waste** — measured as drop in CF, NOx, SOx, BOD, COD.
- **Economic and social value** — green jobs, cost savings, improved public health.

**Layer 4 — Feedback Layer (1.5 marks)**
Continuous monitoring via **LCA**, **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, and **Social Life-Cycle Assessment (S-LCA)** feeds back to the input layer, closing the loop and enabling adaptive improvement (analogous to a control system's feedback path).

> The architecture is **iterative**, not linear — green tech is a continuously self-improving system.

---

**(b) ZLD Plant — Cash Flow and Payback Period [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Annual water savings [2 marks]**

$$\text{Volume recovered} = 2{,}00{,}000 \;\text{m}^3/\text{mo} \times 12 \times 0.95 = 22{,}80{,}000 \;\text{m}^3/\text{yr}$$

*Logic: 95% of monthly consumption is recovered and reused per year.*

$$\text{Annual water saving} = 22{,}80{,}000 \times ₹40 = ₹9.12 \;\text{crore/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Annual salt revenue [1.5 marks]**

$$\text{Salt revenue} = 50 \;\text{tonnes/mo} \times 12 \times ₹2000 = ₹12{,}00{,}000 = ₹0.12 \;\text{crore/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Net annual cash inflow [1.5 marks]**

$$\text{Net CF} = (9.12 + 0.12) - 0.50 = ₹8.74 \;\text{crore/yr}$$

*Logic: Gross revenue − annual O&M = net cash inflow.*

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period [2 marks]**

$$P_{\text{simple}} = \frac{\text{Capital Cost}}{\text{Net Annual CF}} = \frac{15}{8.74} \approx 1.72 \;\text{years}$$

*Logic: Time to recover the ₹15 crore initial investment from ₹8.74 crore/year net savings.*

**Sustainability Impact (concluding remark) [bonus]**:
- Water consumption drops by 95% → aligns with **SDG 6 (Clean Water)**.
- Salt recovery creates a **circular economy revenue stream** → supports **SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption)**.
- Reduced effluent discharge improves river water quality (Noyyal, Tirupur) → **SDG 14/15**.
- 1.72-year payback shows **green tech is also good business**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing LCOE and EPBT** — EPBT is in *years*, LCOE is in *₹/kWh* or *$/MWh*. They measure **energy time** and **cost**, not the same thing.
> 2. **Forgetting the mass ratio 44/12 in coal CF problems** — students often multiply carbon content by 1 directly, getting a value ~3.67× too low.
> 3. **Wrong GWP values** — always specify *which IPCC report* (AR4, AR5, AR6) you are using; AR5 GWP for CH₄ = 28, AR6 = 27.9 (with climate-carbon feedback 29.8).
> 4. **Treating "carbon footprint" and "ecological footprint" as synonyms** — board examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the 7 Principles in ESE answers** — never write just 3 or 4; list **all 7 with one example each** for full marks.
> 6. **Not converting units** in numericals — 1 kcal = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kWh. This is the most common numerical mistake.
> 7. **In pay-back calculations, ignoring O&M** — capital cost recovery ≠ simple payback unless O&M is subtracted from benefits.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Green Technology = environmentally benign products/processes across the **entire life cycle**.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Environmental + Economic + Social (Triple Bottom Line).
- ✅ **7 Principles Mnemonic — "LIFE-PRM":** Life-cycle, In-source prevention, Feedstock renewable, Efficiency, Precaution, Recycling, Mimicry.
- ✅ **Waste Hierarchy — "RRRRRR-L":** Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Recycle → (Landfill last).
- ✅ **Carbon Footprint Formula:** $CF = \sum A_i \cdot GWP_i$ (units: **kg CO₂e**).
- ✅ **LCOE Formula:** Total discounted cost / Total discounted energy (units: **₹/kWh**).
- ✅ **EROEI:** Should be **> 5** for a sustainable energy source.
- ✅ **GWP values (AR5):** CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265.
- ✅ **Mass conversion for carbon → CO₂:** Multiply by **44/12 = 3.667**.
- ✅ **Energy conversion:** 1 kcal = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kWh.
- ✅ **TRL scale:** 1–9; renewable energy technologies mostly at **TRL 8–9**; green H₂ at **TRL 6–7**.
- ✅ **SDG linkages:** Green Tech directly enables **SDG 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13**.
- ✅ **Carbon Footprint ⊂ Ecological Footprint** (sub-set relationship).
- ✅ **Circular economy ≠ Recycling alone** — it begins with **Refuse** and **Rethink** at design stage.
- ✅ **Biomimicry examples:** Lotus (self-cleaning), Shark skin (drag reduction), Termite mound (passive cooling).
- ✅ **ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge)** = a flagship green tech intervention in textile, pharma, and power sectors.
- ✅ **Green Hydrogen = H₂ produced by electrolysis using renewable electricity** (not grey/blue H₂).
- ✅ **Life-Cycle Assessment (LCA)** stages: Extraction → Manufacturing → Transport → Use → End-of-Life → Recovery.
- ✅ **Eco-Efficiency** = Value ÷ Environmental Impact (higher = greener).
- ✅ **Net Present Value (NPV)** must be **> 0** for a green investment to be financially viable.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure as **(i) Theory + Example (7 marks)** and **(ii) Numerical with units + interpretation (7 marks)**.

---
*End of Notes — OESFT721 Module 4: Green Technology*
