topics.title: Green Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Green Energy

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Green Energy** is defined as energy derived from natural, replenishable sources that have a minimal environmental impact, producing little to no greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions during generation and operation. According to the KTU 2024 OBE framework, Green Energy encompasses energy systems that satisfy the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** of sustainability: environmental protection, economic viability, and social equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard)**
> *"Green Energy refers to renewable energy sources such as solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, and tidal energy that are naturally replenished on a human timescale, produce zero or near-zero net carbon emissions over their lifecycle, and are aligned with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs 7, 13, and 15)."*

**Key Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU Exam):**
- **1 kWh (kilowatt-hour) = 3.6 × 10⁶ Joules = 3.6 MJ**
- **1 MW (Megawatt) = 1000 kW = 10⁶ W**
- **Standard solar constant (Iₛc) = 1367 W/m²** (at top of atmosphere, AM 0)
- **Air density at sea level (ρ_air) = 1.225 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g) = 9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 OBE):**
> Green Energy is directly mapped to **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Identify and analyze various green energy technologies and their role in sustainable development."* This topic is consistently asked for **14 marks** in End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy — "The Earth's Natural Savings Account"**

Imagine Earth as a giant financial savings account:
- **Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas)** are like *withdrawing money that was deposited millions of years ago*. You are spending the planet's ancient capital, and once it is exhausted, the account is empty.
- **Green Energy (solar, wind, hydro)** is like *living off the daily interest that the sun, wind, and water continuously deposit into Earth's account*. As long as the sun shines and wind blows, the "interest" keeps arriving every single day — for free, with no environmental debt.

### Why Does This Matter for an Engineer?

As a B.Tech engineer, you will design systems that:
1. **Capture** this natural "interest" (solar panels, wind turbines, hydro dams).
2. **Convert** it into usable electricity or thermal energy.
3. **Integrate** it into the existing grid without violating the laws of thermodynamics.
4. **Evaluate** whether the technology is truly sustainable using metrics like **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**, **Capacity Factor (CF)**, and **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 6 Pillars of Green Energy:** **"S.W.H.B.G.T."**
> **S**olar, **W**ind, **H**ydro, **B**iomass, **G**eothermal, **T**idal/Wave.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Green Energy Sources

Green Energy is broadly classified into two engineering categories:

### A. Intermittent / Variable Renewable Energy Sources (VRES)
- **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) & Solar Thermal**
- **Wind Energy (Onshore & Offshore)**
- **Tidal & Wave Energy**

> These depend on **weather and time-of-day**, requiring energy storage or grid balancing.

### B. Baseload / Dispatchable Renewable Sources
- **Hydroelectric Power (Large & Small)**
- **Geothermal Energy**
- **Biomass & Biogas**

> These can provide **continuous, controllable power** similar to conventional plants.

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of Each Pillar

### 1. Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Energy

A solar cell is essentially a large-area **p-n junction diode** made of doped silicon. When photons strike the cell with energy greater than the silicon bandgap ($E_g \approx 1.12$ eV for crystalline Si), they create electron-hole pairs that are separated by the junction's electric field, generating DC current.

- **Single-crystalline Si efficiency:** 18%–24%
- **Polycrystalline Si efficiency:** 15%–20%
- **Thin-film (CdTe, CIGS):** 10%–15%

### 2. Wind Energy

Wind turbines convert the **kinetic energy of moving air** into mechanical shaft work, which drives a generator. The available power follows the famous **Betz's Law**, which limits extraction to 59.3% of the wind's kinetic energy.

### 3. Hydroelectric Energy

Gravitational potential energy of stored water is converted to kinetic energy, then to mechanical rotation. Governed by:
$$P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$
where $\eta$ is turbine efficiency, $\rho$ is water density, $Q$ is discharge, $H$ is net head.

### 4. Biomass Energy

Stored solar energy (in the form of chemical bonds of organic matter) is released through **combustion, gasification, or anaerobic digestion**. Biofuels include ethanol, biodiesel, and biogas (CH₄ + CO₂).

### 5. Geothermal Energy

Heat from Earth's core (radioactive decay of U, Th, K) is used directly for space heating, or flashed to steam to drive turbines. **Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS)** allow access even in non-volcanic regions.

### 6. Tidal & Wave Energy

Harnesses the gravitational pull of the moon and sun on ocean waters. Tidal barrage uses potential energy; tidal stream uses kinetic energy similar to wind.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Print This for Revision**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Power from Solar PV | $P = \eta_{pv} \cdot A \cdot G \cdot \cos\theta$ | $\eta_{pv}$=cell efficiency, $A$=area, $G$=irradiance, $\theta$=incidence angle | W (Watts) |
| 2 | Wind Power (Available) | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | $\rho$=air density (1.225 kg/m³), $A$=swept area, $v$=wind speed | W |
| 3 | Betz's Max Coefficient | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical maximum extraction | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Hydroelectric Power | $P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | $\eta$=turbine eff., $\rho$=1000 kg/m³, $Q$=discharge, $H$=head | W |
| 5 | Capacity Factor (CF) | $CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760 \text{ h}}$ | Ratio of actual to max possible energy | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | Levelized Cost of Energy | $LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{I_t + O_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | $I_t$=investment, $O_t$=O&M, $E_t$=energy, $r$=discount rate | ₹/kWh or \$/kWh |
| 7 | Energy Payback Time | $EPBT = \frac{E_{lifecycle}}{E_{annual,gen}}$ | Lifecycle energy input ÷ annual output | Years |
| 8 | Carbon Payback Time | $CPBT = \frac{CO_{2,emit}}{CO_{2,avoided}}$ | Emissions in manufacturing ÷ annual CO₂ saved | Years |
| 9 | Biomass Energy | $E = m \cdot HHV$ | $m$=mass, $HHV$=Higher Heating Value (e.g., wood ≈ 20 MJ/kg) | MJ |
| 10 | Geothermal Gradient | $\frac{dT}{dz} \approx 25\text{–}30 °C/km$ | Average Earth's crust temperature rise | °C/km |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar PV** powers satellites (where $G \approx 1361$ W/m² in space), rooftop grids, and solar farms like Bhadla Solar Park (Rajasthan, 2.25 GW).
- **Wind energy** now powers 8% of global electricity, with offshore farms reaching 15 MW per turbine.
- **Biomass cogeneration** in sugar mills (India) produces both electricity and process heat.
- **Geothermal** provides baseload power in Iceland (≈25% of electricity) and the Philippines.
- **Hydroelectric** remains the largest renewable source globally (~15% of world electricity).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Application Note:** When KTU asks *"Discuss the role of green energy in sustainable development"*, you must connect technology → carbon reduction → UN SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy) and SDG 13 (Climate Action). This linkage carries 3–4 marks by itself.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Extraction from Wind (Betz's Law)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a wind turbine with rotor swept area $A$ exposed to a free-stream wind velocity $v_1$. After passing through the rotor, the air slows to $v_2$, and far downstream to $v_3$. The rotor disc extracts kinetic energy, so by conservation: $v_1 > v_2 > v_3$.

### Step 1: Mass Flow Rate of Air Through the Rotor

The mass of air flowing per unit time through the disc:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_{avg}$$
where $v_{avg} = \frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}$ (average of upstream and downstream velocities).

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law (Force on the Rotor)

The force exerted by the rotor on the air equals the rate of change of momentum:
$$F = \dot{m} \cdot (v_1 - v_3) = \rho A v_{avg} (v_1 - v_3)$$

### Step 3: Power Extracted by the Turbine

The mechanical power extracted equals force × velocity at the rotor:
$$P_{extracted} = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A v_{avg} (v_1 - v_3) \cdot v_2$$

### Step 4: Compare with Available Wind Power

The available kinetic power in the undisturbed wind is:
$$P_{available} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3$$

### Step 5: Define the Power Coefficient

$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{available}} = \frac{\rho A v_{avg} (v_1 - v_3) v_2}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3} = \frac{2 v_{avg} v_2 (v_1 - v_3)}{v_1^3}$$

### Step 6: Introduce the Axial Induction Factor

Let $a$ be the axial induction factor, defined such that:
$$v_2 = v_1 (1 - a) \quad \text{and} \quad v_{avg} = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{a}{2}\right)$$

Substituting:
$$C_p = 4 a (1-a)^2$$

### Step 7: Maximize $C_p$ with Respect to $a$

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4 \left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

This gives two solutions: $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or $a = 1/3$.

### Step 8: Compute Maximum $C_p$

At $a = 1/3$:
$$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

### Step 9: Final Result — Betz's Law

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 59.3\%}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** No wind turbine — regardless of design — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in the wind. This is the **fundamental physical limit**, not a technological limitation. Real turbines achieve 35%–45% due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Example: Solar PV System Sizing (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A rooftop solar PV system in Kerala has:
- Panel area: $A = 20$ m²
- Efficiency: $\eta_{pv} = 0.18$ (18%)
- Average daily solar irradiance: $G = 5.5$ kWh/m²/day
- Performance ratio (PR): $k = 0.75$ (accounts for temperature, wiring, dust losses)

**Find:** Annual electrical energy output.

### Step 1: Daily Energy Output
$$E_{day} = \eta_{pv} \cdot A \cdot G \cdot k$$
$$E_{day} = 0.18 \times 20 \times 5.5 \times 0.75 = 14.85 \text{ kWh/day}$$

### Step 2: Annual Energy Output
$$E_{annual} = E_{day} \times 365 = 14.85 \times 365$$
$$E_{annual} = 5420.25 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 5.42 \text{ MWh/year}$$

### Step 3: CO₂ Avoided Annually

Assuming Kerala's grid emission factor = 0.65 kg CO₂/kWh:
$$CO_2_{avoided} = 5420.25 \times 0.65 = 3523.16 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} \approx 3.52 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** Always end numerical problems with a **sustainability impact statement** (e.g., "This system offsets ~3.52 tonnes of CO₂ annually, equivalent to planting 58 trees per year"). Examiners reward this linkage to COs.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Green Energy Technologies

| Parameter | Solar PV | Wind | Hydro | Biomass | Geothermal | Tidal |
|-----------|----------|------|-------|---------|------------|-------|
| **Capacity Factor** | 15%–25% | 25%–45% | 40%–60% | 60%–90% | 70%–95% | 20%–35% |
| **LCOE (USD/kWh)** | 0.04–0.10 | 0.03–0.08 | 0.05–0.10 | 0.05–0.15 | 0.05–0.12 | 0.10–0.30 |
| **GHG Emissions (gCO₂/kWh)** | 40–50 | 10–20 | 4–10 | 30–60 | 30–50 | 5–15 |
| **Land Use (m²/MWh/yr)** | 8–12 | 2–5 | 1–3 (large dams affect land) | High | Low | 0 (offshore) |
| **EPBT (years)** | 1.5–3 | 0.5–1.5 | 2–5 | 1–3 | 1–2 | 5–10 |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (Mature) | 9 (Mature) | 9 (Mature) | 8 (Commercial) | 7 (Developing) | 6 (Pilot) |
| **Storage Needed** | Yes (battery) | Yes (battery) | No (reservoir) | No (fuel) | No | Yes |

> **TRL** = Technology Readiness Level (1=basic research, 9=fully commercial).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Green Energy Ecosystem

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional flow** of how a green energy system integrates with the grid, society, and the environment, in compliance with KTU 2024 OBE mapping to SDGs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Energy Sources] --> B[Conversion Technology]
    A1[Solar Radiation] --> A
    A2[Wind Kinetic Energy] --> A
    A3[Hydro Potential] --> A
    A4[Biomass Chemical] --> A
    A5[Geothermal Heat] --> A
    A6[Tidal Kinetic] --> A

    B --> C[Power Conditioning Unit]
    C --> D[Inverter DC to AC]
    D --> E[Smart Grid Interface]
    E --> F[End User Load]

    E --> G[Energy Storage Battery]
    E --> H[Net Metering]
    H --> I[Utility Grid]

    F --> J[CO2 Emission Reduction]
    F --> K[UN SDG 7 Clean Energy]
    F --> L[UN SDG 13 Climate Action]
    F --> M[Economic Savings]

    G --> E
    I --> E

    classDef source fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tech fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef impact fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6 source
    class B,C,D,G tech
    class E,F,H,I grid
    class J,K,L,M impact
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Solar PV System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Sunlight Photons] --> PV[Solar PV Array]
    PV --> CC[Charge Controller MPPT]
    CC --> BATT[Battery Bank DC Storage]
    CC --> INV[Inverter DC to AC]
    BATT --> INV
    INV --> LOAD[AC Load Home Industry]
    INV --> METER[Net Meter]
    METER --> GRID[Utility Grid]

    classDef light fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class SRC,PV light
    class CC,INV,METER elec
    class BATT storage
    class LOAD,GRID load
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Selecting the Right Green Energy Source

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Choose Technology]) --> Q1{Geographic Location?}
    Q1 -->|Tropical Sunny| Q2{Space Available?}
    Q1 -->|Coastal High Wind| WIND[Wind Turbine]
    Q1 -->|River / Dam Site| HYDRO[Hydroelectric]
    Q1 -->|Volcanic Region| GEO[Geothermal]
    Q1 -->|Agricultural Area| BIO[Biomass]

    Q2 -->|Yes Large Roof| SOLAR[Solar PV System]
    Q2 -->|Limited| SHARED[Solar Microgrid]

    SOLAR --> EVAL{Annual Energy Need?}
    WIND --> EVAL
    HYDRO --> EVAL
    GEO --> EVAL
    BIO --> EVAL
    SHARED --> EVAL

    EVAL -->|Less than 10 MWh| SMALL[Off grid System]
    EVAL -->|Greater than 10 MWh| LARGE[Grid Tied System]
    EVAL -->|Variable 24x7| HYBRID[Hybrid Storage System]

    SMALL --> DONE([Deploy and Monitor])
    LARGE --> DONE
    HYBRID --> DONE

    classDef start fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:3px
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tech fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef final fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:3px

    class START,DONE final
    class Q1,Q2,EVAL decision
    class SOLAR,WIND,HYDRO,GEO,BIO,SHARED,SMALL,LARGE,HYBRID tech
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Green Energy. List any four types of renewable energy sources with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Green Energy is energy generated from natural, replenishable resources such as sunlight, wind, water, biomass, and geothermal heat, characterized by low carbon emissions and minimal environmental degradation.

**Four Types with Examples (2 marks):**
1. **Solar Energy** – Photovoltaic cells converting sunlight into electricity (e.g., rooftop solar panels).
2. **Wind Energy** – Wind turbines converting kinetic energy of air into mechanical power (e.g., Muppandal wind farm, Tamil Nadu).
3. **Hydroelectric Energy** – Dams converting gravitational potential of water into electricity (e.g., Idukki Dam, Kerala).
4. **Biomass Energy** – Combustion or digestion of organic matter to release stored chemical energy (e.g., biogas plants using agricultural waste).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State and explain Betz's Law. What is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a wind turbine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** Betz's Law states that no wind turbine can convert more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3%)** of the kinetic energy of wind into useful mechanical work.

**Explanation (1.5 marks):** This limit arises from the conservation of mass and momentum. As wind passes through the rotor disc, it slows down but cannot be brought to a complete stop (which would block further airflow). The optimum axial induction factor is $a = 1/3$, giving $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$. Real-world turbines achieve 35%–45% due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern (Strict):** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student must answer **either** the whole of Choice A **or** Choice B.

---

### ✅ CHOICE A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024]`

#### Question A (a): Solar Photovoltaic System Analysis (7 marks)

**"With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a grid-connected solar PV system. A 5 kW rooftop solar system has 20 panels, each rated 250 W, area 1.6 m², and efficiency 16%. Calculate the annual energy generation in Kerala (assume 5.0 kWh/m²/day average irradiance and 0.78 performance ratio)."**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Sunlight → PV Array → MPPT Charge Controller → Inverter (DC to AC) → Net Meter → Grid/Load. (Refer to Section 4.2 diagram.)

**Working Explanation (2 marks):** Solar PV cells, made of doped silicon, generate DC electricity via the photovoltaic effect when photons strike the p-n junction. The charge controller with MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) optimizes voltage-current output. The inverter converts DC to AC at grid frequency (50 Hz in India), which is fed to the household load and excess exported to the grid via net metering.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $N = 20$ panels, $P_{rated} = 250$ W, $A_{panel} = 1.6$ m², $\eta = 0.16$, $G = 5.0$ kWh/m²/day, $PR = 0.78$.

$$E_{daily} = N \cdot P_{rated} \cdot \frac{PR \cdot G}{1000 \cdot \text{(per kW standard)}}$$

Alternatively:
$$E_{daily} = \eta \cdot A_{total} \cdot G \cdot PR$$
$$A_{total} = 20 \times 1.6 = 32 \text{ m}^2$$
$$E_{daily} = 0.16 \times 32 \times 5.0 \times 0.78 = 19.97 \text{ kWh/day}$$
$$E_{annual} = 19.97 \times 365 = 7289.6 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 7.29 \text{ MWh/year}$$

**Sustainability Impact Statement:** This system avoids approximately **4.74 tonnes of CO₂/year** (using Kerala's grid factor of 0.65 kg CO₂/kWh), equivalent to planting 78 trees annually.

---

#### Question A (b): Wind Energy & Betz's Law Application (7 marks)

**"Derive Betz's Law for a wind turbine. A wind farm site has an average wind speed of 10 m/s, air density 1.225 kg/m³, and rotor diameter 80 m. Calculate (i) the available wind power, and (ii) the maximum extractable power as per Betz's limit."**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to the step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key result: $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $v = 10$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $D = 80$ m.

**(i) Available Wind Power:**

Swept area: $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = 5026.55$ m²

$$P_{available} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (10)^3$$
$$P_{available} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1000$$
$$P_{available} = 3,078,760 \text{ W} \approx 3.08 \text{ MW}$$

**(ii) Maximum Extractable Power (Betz's Limit):**

$$P_{max} = C_{p,max} \times P_{available} = 0.593 \times 3,078,760$$
$$P_{max} \approx 1,825,704 \text{ W} \approx 1.83 \text{ MW}$$

**Conclusion:** Even with perfect design, this turbine can extract at most **1.83 MW** out of 3.08 MW available.

---

### ✅ CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question

#### Question B (a): Biomass & Bioenergy Technologies (7 marks)

**"Explain the different biomass conversion technologies. Discuss the role of biomass in achieving India's renewable energy targets."**

**Model Solution:**

**Biomass Conversion Technologies (4 marks):**
1. **Direct Combustion:** Burning biomass (wood, agricultural residue) to produce heat/steam for electricity. Simple, but emits particulates.
2. **Gasification:** Partial oxidation at 800–1200°C produces **syngas** (CO + H₂), which can fuel engines or be processed to biofuels.
3. **Anaerobic Digestion:** Microbial breakdown of organic waste in absence of oxygen produces **biogas** (60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂). Digestate is used as fertilizer.
4. **Fermentation:** Sugars/starches converted by yeast into **ethanol** (C₂H₅OH), used as transport fuel blended with petrol.
5. **Transesterification:** Vegetable oils react with methanol to produce **biodiesel** (FAME – Fatty Acid Methyl Esters).

**Role in India's RE Targets (3 marks):**
- India targets **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030** (announced at COP26, Glasgow).
- Biomass contributes to the **"Other Renewables"** category (~10% share).
- National Biogas Programme (1981–) and SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) scheme promote CBG (Compressed Biogas).
- India produces ~500 million tonnes of agricultural residue annually — a vast untapped resource.
- Biomass is **dispatchable** (unlike solar/wind), making it crucial for grid stability.
- Aligns with **Swachh Bharat Mission** by converting waste to energy.

---

#### Question B (b): Geothermal & Tidal Energy (7 marks)

**"Discuss the principles of geothermal and tidal energy. Compare their potential in the Indian context."**

**Model Solution:**

**Geothermal Energy (3.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** Heat from Earth's interior (radioactive decay of U²³⁸, Th²³², K⁴⁰) raises subsurface temperatures. The geothermal gradient is ~25–30°C/km in normal crustal regions; in volcanic zones, it can exceed 100°C/km.
- **Types of Power Plants:**
  - *Dry Steam:* Direct use of natural steam (e.g., Larderello, Italy).
  - *Flash Steam:* High-pressure hot water (>180°C) is flashed to steam (e.g., The Geysers, USA).
  - *Binary Cycle:* Hot water (100–180°C) vaporizes an organic fluid (e.g., isobutane) with low boiling point.
- **Indian Potential:** Puga Valley (Ladakh), Manikaran (Himachal Pradesh), Surajkund (Haryana), and the Andaman–Nicobar volcanic arc. Estimated potential: ~10 GW.

**Tidal Energy (3.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** Gravitational pull of the moon and sun creates predictable tidal cycles (~12 h 25 min period). Tidal range in India: Gulf of Cambay (8–10 m) and Gulf of Kutch (5–8 m) are the most promising.
- **Methods:**
  - *Tidal Barrage:* A dam with sluice gates and turbines across an estuary; water flows through turbines during high and low tide cycles.
  - *Tidal Stream:* Underwater turbines in fast-flowing tidal channels (similar to wind turbines).
  - *Dynamic Tidal Power:* Long dam perpendicular to coast (conceptual).
- **Indian Potential:** Gulf of Kutch (estimated 7 GW), Gulf of Cambay (estimated 7 GW), Sundarbans (~1 GW). Total potential: ~15 GW, but commercial deployment is limited.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Geothermal | Tidal |
|--------|------------|-------|
| **Predictability** | High (24×7) | High (twice daily) |
| **Carbon Emissions** | ~30 g CO₂/kWh | ~5 g CO₂/kWh |
| **Capacity Factor** | 70%–95% | 20%–35% |
| **Indian Potential** | ~10 GW | ~15 GW |
| **Current Status** | Pilot projects | Pilot at Vizhinjam (Kerala) |
| **Challenges** | Site-specific, drilling cost | Marine environment, corrosion |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Green Energy Questions**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Green Energy with Renewable Energy:** Green Energy ⊂ Renewable Energy. All green is renewable, but *all* renewable is **not** green (e.g., large hydropower with deforestation impacts is debated).
> 
> 2. **Wrong Formulas for LCOE and CF:** Students often forget to multiply by **8760 hours/year** in CF formula, or skip the discount factor in LCOE.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to write units:** $P$ in MW vs. kW, $E$ in MWh vs. kWh — losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Betz's Law derivation incomplete:** Examiners check for **$C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$** step. Skipping it loses 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **No sustainability linkage:** KTU 2024 OBE requires connecting every technology to **CO₂ reduction + SDGs**. Missing this loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark answers.
> 
> 6. **Mere listing without explanation:** Just naming solar, wind, hydro etc. without describing working principle = **0 marks** for that type.
> 
> 7. **Incorrect grid frequency:** Always remember **India = 50 Hz, USA/Japan = 60 Hz.**

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Core Definitions to Memorize
- [ ] Green Energy = Renewable + Low-Carbon + Sustainable
- [ ] Capacity Factor (CF) = Actual Energy ÷ Max Possible Energy
- [ ] Betz Coefficient = 16/27 ≈ 0.593 (59.3%)
- [ ] Solar Constant = **1367 W/m²** (AM 0)
- [ ] Air Density at Sea Level = **1.225 kg/m³**
- [ ] Water Density = **1000 kg/m³**
- [ ] LCOE unit = ₹/kWh or $/kWh
- [ ] 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ

### ✅ Must-Remember Equations
- [ ] Solar Power: $P = \eta \cdot A \cdot G$
- [ ] Wind Power: $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$
- [ ] Hydro Power: $P = \eta \rho g Q H$
- [ ] CF: $CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$
- [ ] LCOE: ratio of total discounted cost to total discounted energy
- [ ] EPBT: $E_{input} \div E_{annual}$

### ✅ Six Pillars of Green Energy (Mnemonic: **S.W.H.B.G.T.**)
- [ ] **S**olar – Photovoltaic + Solar Thermal
- [ ] **W**ind – Onshore + Offshore
- [ ] **H**ydro – Large + Small + Micro
- [ ] **B**iomass – Combustion + Biogas + Biofuels
- [ ] **G**eothermal – Dry Steam + Flash + Binary
- [ ] **T**idal/Wave – Barrage + Stream

### ✅ Numerical Problem Templates (Practice These 3 Patterns)
1. **Solar PV Energy Output** = $\eta \times A \times G \times PR \times 365$
2. **Wind Power (Available & Betz-Limited)** = $\frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \times C_p$
3. **Hydro Power** = $\eta \rho g Q H$

### ✅ SDG Linkages (Always Mention in Long Answers)
- [ ] **SDG 7** — Affordable and Clean Energy
- [ ] **SDG 13** — Climate Action
- [ ] **SDG 11** — Sustainable Cities and Communities
- [ ] **SDG 9** — Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure

### ✅ Quick Facts for Bonus Marks
- [ ] Largest Solar Park in India: **Bhadla, Rajasthan (2.25 GW)**
- [ ] First Geothermal Pilot in India: **Puga Valley, Ladakh (1980s)**
- [ ] India's RE Target: **500 GW by 2030**
- [ ] Global Renewables Share (2024): ≈ **30%** of electricity
- [ ] Kerala has pilot tidal project at **Vizhinjam** (near Trivandrum)

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always structure your 14-mark answer as:
> **(i) Introduction & Definition (2 marks)**
> **(ii) Working Principle with Diagram (3–4 marks)**
> **(iii) Technical Analysis / Numerical (4–5 marks)**
> **(iv) Sustainability Impact & SDG Linkage (2–3 marks)**
> This format guarantees full marks under KTU OBE assessment.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Green Construction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Green Construction (Green Building / Sustainable Construction):** A holistic, life-cycle-oriented approach to the planning, design, construction, operation, maintenance, renovation, and deconstruction of buildings and infrastructure that delivers high performance while minimizing environmental impact, conserving natural resources, enhancing occupant health and well-being, and ensuring long-term economic viability.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Green Construction is a **closed-loop, systems-engineering paradigm** where the built environment is treated as an integral subsystem of the natural ecosystem. The objective is to **decouple economic growth from resource depletion** within the Architecture-Engineering-Construction (AEC) sector.

### The Three Pillars of Green Construction (Triple Bottom Line)

1. **Environmental Stewardship** — Reduction of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, conservation of water, protection of biodiversity, and minimization of waste.
2. **Social Responsibility** — Provision of healthy indoor environments, thermal comfort, daylighting, and improved occupant productivity.
3. **Economic Viability** — Reduction of life-cycle costs, increased asset value, and lower operational expenditure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OESFT721 Module 4):** Students must internalize the difference between *Conventional Construction* (linear: extract → build → demolish → waste) and *Green Construction* (circular: design → build → use → reuse → recycle).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a building as a living organism, not a finished product.**
>
> - A **conventional building** is like a **plastic toy** bought from a shop — cheap, fast to make, but when it breaks, it goes straight to a landfill and never returns to the system. It has no relationship with nature.
>
> - A **green building** is like a **tree**:
>   - It is grown from **local, natural materials** (like a tree uses soil, sunlight, and water).
>   - During its life, it **produces oxygen, shades the ground, and provides shelter** (operational benefits: energy generation, thermal comfort, healthy indoor air).
>   - At the end of its life, every part of it **decomposes and feeds back into the earth** (deconstruction, recycling, composting).
>   - Even its *seeds* (recycled materials) are used to grow new "trees" (new buildings).

### Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Benchmark Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Embodied Energy** | $EE$ | **MJ/kg** or **GJ/m²** |
| **Embodied Carbon** | $EC$ | **kgCO₂e/kg** or **kgCO₂e/m²** |
| **Energy Performance Index** | $EPI$ | **kWh/m²/year** |
| **Potable Water Consumption** | $W_p$ | **litres/person/day** (Standard: **135 LPCD**) |
| **Indoor CO₂ Concentration** | $C_{CO_2}$ | **< 800 ppm** (ASHRAE 62.1) |
| **Daylight Factor (DF)** | $DF$ | **2 % – 5 %** for offices |
| **Construction Waste Diverted** | $\eta_{div}$ | **> 75 %** from landfill |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Core Principles of Green Construction (UNEP / TERI-GRIHA Framework)

1. **Site Selection & Sustainable Land Use**
   - Avoid ecologically sensitive zones (wetlands, forests, prime farmland).
   - Prefer **brownfield** (previously developed) sites over **greenfield** (virgin) sites.
   - Optimize orientation to harness passive solar design.

2. **Water Efficiency & Conservation**
   - **Reduce** — low-flow fixtures (target: **30 %–50 %** reduction over baseline).
   - **Reuse** — greywater recycling for landscape and flushing.
   - **Recycle** — rainwater harvesting with **≥ 50 %** annual runoff capture.

3. **Energy Efficiency & Renewable Integration**
   - **Passive design** first: orientation, insulation, shading, natural ventilation.
   - **Active systems** next: high-efficiency HVAC, LED lighting (target ≥ **50 %** energy savings).
   - **Renewables** last: rooftop solar PV, solar water heaters, wind integration.

4. **Material Sustainability**
   - Use **locally sourced** materials (transportation footprint ↓).
   - Prefer **recycled-content** materials (e.g., fly-ash bricks, recycled steel).
   - Use **rapidly renewable** materials (bamboo, cork, straw-bale).
   - Eliminate materials with high **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound)** emissions.

5. **Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ)**
   - Adequate **daylighting** (DF ≥ 2 %).
   - **Natural ventilation** (ACH ≥ 4 to 6).
   - Low-VOC paints, adhesives, sealants.
   - Acoustic comfort (NC ≤ 40 for offices).

6. **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste Management**
   - The 5R Hierarchy: **Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover**.
   - Target diversion rate from landfill: **≥ 75 %** (GRIHA benchmark).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are standard KTU 2024 syllabus deliverables. Master these before attempting numerical problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Embodied Energy of a Building** | $EE_{building} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \times EE_i$ | MJ or GJ | $M_i$ = mass of material *i*; $EE_i$ = EE per unit mass |
| 2 | **Embodied Carbon** | $EC_{building} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \times EC_i$ | kgCO₂e | $EC_i$ = carbon factor per unit mass |
| 3 | **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{built-up}}$ | kWh/m²/year | ECBC benchmark for office: **90–165 kWh/m²/yr** |
| 4 | **Operational Energy (HVAC)** | $E_{HVAC} = \dfrac{Q_{load}}{\eta_{system} \times 3600}$ | kWh | $Q_{load}$ in kJ; $\eta_{system}$ = COP |
| 5 | **Payback Period (Green Investment)** | $PB = \dfrac{\Delta C_{capex}}{\Delta S_{annual}}$ | years | $\Delta C$ = extra cost; $\Delta S$ = annual savings |
| 6 | **Net Present Value (Life-Cycle Cost)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{C_t - B_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ or \$ | $r$ = discount rate; $T$ = life in years |
| 7 | **Water Savings** | $S_w = W_{baseline} - W_{actual}$ | litres/day | Baseline per IS 1172: **135 LPCD** |
| 8 | **Daylight Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$ | % | ANSI/IES RP-30-13 |
| 9 | **C&D Waste Diversion Rate** | $\eta_{div} = \dfrac{W_{recycled} + W_{reused}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Target: **≥ 75 %** |
| 10 | **Carbon Payback of Solar PV** | $CPB = \dfrac{EC_{PV}}{A \times I \times 8760 \times \eta \times EF_{grid}}$ | years | $EF_{grid}$ = grid emission factor (~**0.82 kgCO₂e/kWh** in India) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Memorization for KTU ESE:** Embodied Energy of cement ≈ **4.5–5.5 MJ/kg**; Steel ≈ **25–30 MJ/kg**; Fly-ash brick ≈ **2.0 MJ/kg**; Aluminium ≈ **200 MJ/kg**; Bamboo ≈ **0.5 MJ/kg**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Green Construction is not academic. It is the **operational backbone** of:
- **Smart Cities Mission** (Government of India, 100 cities).
- **Net-Zero Energy Buildings (NZEB)** targets by **2030** (EU EPBD recast).
- **India's Panchamrit climate commitments** at COP-26 (50 % emissions intensity reduction by 2030).
- **RERA compliance** with sustainable disclosures for premium real estate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Embodied Energy & Embodied Carbon of a Residential Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A 1-storey, 100 m² residential building uses the following materials. Compute (a) Total Embodied Energy (EE) and (b) Total Embodied Carbon (EC), and (c) recommend the most sustainable substitution.

**Given Data:**

| Material | Quantity ($M_i$) | Unit | EE per Unit ($EE_i$) | EC per Unit ($EC_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) | 5,000 | kg | 4.8 MJ/kg | 0.93 kgCO₂e/kg |
| Steel (reinforcement) | 1,200 | kg | 28.0 MJ/kg | 1.99 kgCO₂e/kg |
| Sand (river) | 12,000 | kg | 0.10 MJ/kg | 0.005 kgCO₂e/kg |
| Burnt clay brick (traditional) | 18,000 | kg | 3.0 MJ/kg | 0.42 kgCO₂e/kg |
| Aluminium (windows) | 80 | kg | 210.0 MJ/kg | 11.5 kgCO₂e/kg |

#### Part (a): Total Embodied Energy — Exhaustive Derivation

The governing equation is:

$$
EE_{building} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \times EE_i
$$

We substitute each material term by term.

**Step 1 — Embodied Energy of Cement:**

$$
EE_{cement} = M_{cement} \times EE_{cement} = 5{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 4.8 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

$$
EE_{cement} = 24{,}000 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Embodied Energy of Steel:**

$$
EE_{steel} = 1{,}200 \text{ kg} \times 28.0 \text{ MJ/kg} = 33{,}600 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Embodied Energy of Sand:**

$$
EE_{sand} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.10 \text{ MJ/kg} = 1{,}200 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Embodied Energy of Bricks:**

$$
EE_{bricks} = 18{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 3.0 \text{ MJ/kg} = 54{,}000 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Embodied Energy of Aluminium:**

$$
EE_{aluminium} = 80 \text{ kg} \times 210.0 \text{ MJ/kg} = 16{,}800 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 6 — Summation (Total Embodied Energy):**

$$
EE_{total} = 24{,}000 + 33{,}600 + 1{,}200 + 54{,}000 + 16{,}800
$$

$$
\boxed{EE_{total} = 129{,}600 \text{ MJ} = 129.6 \text{ GJ}}
$$

**Step 7 — Normalize to Floor Area (engineering metric):**

$$
EE_{specific} = \dfrac{EE_{total}}{A_{built-up}} = \dfrac{129{,}600 \text{ MJ}}{100 \text{ m}^2} = 1{,}296 \text{ MJ/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{EE_{specific} \approx 1.30 \text{ GJ/m}^2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Indian Green Benchmark (GRIHA):** Typical residential building = **1.5–2.0 GJ/m²**. Our baseline building is at the lower edge, but we can do far better.

#### Part (b): Total Embodied Carbon — Exhaustive Derivation

Governing equation:

$$
EC_{building} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \times EC_i
$$

**Step 1 — Embodied Carbon of Cement:**

$$
EC_{cement} = 5{,}000 \times 0.93 = 4{,}650 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 2 — Embodied Carbon of Steel:**

$$
EC_{steel} = 1{,}200 \times 1.99 = 2{,}388 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 3 — Embodied Carbon of Sand:**

$$
EC_{sand} = 12{,}000 \times 0.005 = 60 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — Embodied Carbon of Bricks:**

$$
EC_{bricks} = 18{,}000 \times 0.42 = 7{,}560 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 5 — Embodied Carbon of Aluminium:**

$$
EC_{aluminium} = 80 \times 11.5 = 920 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 6 — Summation:**

$$
EC_{total} = 4{,}650 + 2{,}388 + 60 + 7{,}560 + 920
$$

$$
\boxed{EC_{total} = 15{,}578 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e} \approx 15.6 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}}
$$

**Step 7 — Carbon intensity per m²:**

$$
EC_{specific} = \dfrac{15{,}578}{100} = 155.78 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e/m}^2
$$

#### Part (c): Recommendation — Sustainable Substitution Matrix

| Original Material | Substitute | Expected EE Reduction | Expected EC Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| OPC Cement (5,000 kg) | 30 % Fly-ash blended (PPC) | **~22 %** | **~25 %** |
| Burnt clay bricks (18,000 kg) | Fly-ash bricks (EE ≈ 2.0 MJ/kg) | **~33 %** | **~50 %** |
| Steel (1,200 kg) | Use 20 % recycled steel | **~18 %** | **~22 %** |
| Aluminium windows (80 kg) | UPVC / FSC-certified wood | **~95 %** | **~90 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Substituting bricks and aluminium alone can reduce **Embodied Carbon by 35–40 %** without compromising structural integrity — a typical KTU-style recommendation expected in 14-mark answers.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Material EE/EC Snapshot

| Material | EE (MJ/kg) | EC (kgCO₂e/kg) | Renewability | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OPC Cement | 4.8 | 0.93 | Non-renewable | Reduce |
| Fly-ash Cement (PPC) | 3.6 | 0.65 | Industrial waste | **Preferred** |
| Virgin Steel | 28.0 | 1.99 | Non-renewable | Reduce |
| Recycled Steel | 22.0 | 1.45 | Recycled | **Preferred** |
| Burnt Clay Brick | 3.0 | 0.42 | Soil-depleting | Reduce |
| Fly-ash Brick | 2.0 | 0.20 | Industrial waste | **Preferred** |
| Aluminium (virgin) | 210.0 | 11.5 | High-impact | Avoid |
| UPVC | 65.0 | 2.5 | Petroleum-based | Use cautiously |
| Bamboo | 0.5 | 0.05 | Rapidly renewable | **Highly preferred** |
| Rammed Earth | 0.5 | 0.02 | Site-sourced | **Highly preferred** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Six Pillars of Green Construction — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    GC[GREEN CONSTRUCTION] --> P1[SITE SUSTAINABILITY]
    GC --> P2[WATER EFFICIENCY]
    GC --> P3[ENERGY EFFICIENCY]
    GC --> P4[MATERIAL SUSTAINABILITY]
    GC --> P5[INDOOR ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY]
    GC --> P6[WASTE MANAGEMENT]

    P1 --> P1A[Brownfield preference]
    P1 --> P1B[Passive orientation]
    P1 --> P1C[Biodiversity protection]

    P2 --> P2A[Low-flow fixtures]
    P2 --> P2B[Rainwater harvesting]
    P2 --> P2C[Greywater recycling]

    P3 --> P3A[Passive design]
    P3 --> P3B[High efficiency HVAC]
    P3 --> P3C[Renewable integration]

    P4 --> P4A[Local materials]
    P4 --> P4B[Recycled content]
    P4 --> P4C[Low VOC products]

    P5 --> P5A[Daylighting]
    P5 --> P5B[Natural ventilation]
    P5 --> P5C[Thermal comfort]

    P6 --> P6A[5R hierarchy]
    P6 --> P6B[Segregation at source]
    P6 --> P6C[Composting organics]
```

### 4.2 Life-Cycle Assessment (LCA) Stages of a Green Building

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STAGE A1 RAW MATERIAL SUPPLY] --> B[STAGE A2 TRANSPORT]
    B --> C[STAGE A3 MANUFACTURING]
    C --> D[STAGE A4 TRANSPORT TO SITE]
    D --> E[STAGE A5 CONSTRUCTION]
    E --> F[STAGE B1 USE PHASE]
    F --> G[STAGE B2 MAINTENANCE]
    G --> H[STAGE B3 REPAIR]
    H --> I[STAGE B4 REPLACEMENT]
    I --> J[STAGE B5 REFURBISHMENT]
    J --> K[STAGE C1 DECONSTRUCTION]
    K --> L[STAGE C2 TRANSPORT TO EOL]
    L --> M[STAGE C3 WASTE PROCESSING]
    M --> N[STAGE C4 FINAL DISPOSAL]
    N --> O[STAGE D RECOVERY RECYCLING BENEFITS]
```

### 4.3 Green Construction Decision Flowchart (Design to Commissioning)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Project Inception] --> Site[Site Selection and Analysis]
    Site --> Orient[Climate Responsive Orientation]
    Orient --> Passive[Passive Design Strategy]
    Passive --> Active[Active System Selection]
    Active --> Mat[Material Selection EE and EC Optimization]
    Mat --> Water[Water Conservation Systems]
    Water --> Waste[Construction Phase Waste Plan]
    Waste --> IEQ[IEQ Optimization Daylight Ventilation]
    IEQ --> Rate[Rating System Registration LEED GRIHA IGBC]
    Rate --> Monitor[Post Occupancy Monitoring]
    Monitor --> End[Net Zero and Circular Closure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flow:** Notice how the entire green construction process is **front-loaded**. Approximately **80 %** of the sustainability decisions are made in the **first 20 %** of the design phase. This is why KTU examiners emphasize *integrated design*.

### 4.4 Green Building Rating Systems — Comparative Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[GLOBAL RATING SYSTEMS] --> B[LEED USA]
    A --> C[BREEAM UK]
    A --> D[DGNB GERMANY]
    A --> E[CASBEE JAPAN]
    F[INDIAN RATING SYSTEMS] --> G[GRIHA INDIA]
    F --> H[IGBC INDIA]
    F --> I[BUILDING ENERGY EFFICIENCY BEE ECBC]
```

| Rating | Country | Levels | Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LEED v4.1** | USA / Global | Certified → Silver → Gold → Platinum | Energy, water, materials, IEQ |
| **BREEAM** | UK | Pass → Good → Very Good → Excellent → Outstanding | Whole-life environmental impact |
| **GRIHA** | India (TERI) | 1 star → 5 star | National context, climate, materials |
| **IGBC** | India (CII) | Certified → Silver → Gold → Platinum | Indian construction practices |
| **ECBC+** | India (BEE) | Compliant → Plus → Super | Net-positive energy ready |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Green Construction. List any four principles of green building design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Green Construction is a holistic, life-cycle-based approach to design, construction, and operation that minimizes environmental impact, conserves resources, and ensures occupant well-being.

**Four Principles (0.5 mark each):**
1. **Sustainable site selection** — avoiding ecologically sensitive zones; preferring brownfield.
2. **Water efficiency** — low-flow fixtures, rainwater harvesting, greywater recycling.
3. **Energy efficiency** — passive design, high-efficiency HVAC, renewable integration.
4. **Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ)** — adequate daylight, natural ventilation, low-VOC materials.
5. *(Any other valid principle: Material Sustainability / C&D Waste Management / Life-Cycle Thinking)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**What is Embodied Energy? How does it differ from Operational Energy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Embodied Energy (EE) (1.5 marks):** The total primary energy consumed during the **extraction, manufacture, transportation, and assembly** of all materials used in a building. It is measured in **MJ/kg** or **GJ/m²** and is a *one-time, "front-loaded" cost* of the structure.

- **Operational Energy (1.5 marks):** The energy consumed during the **use phase** of the building — HVAC, lighting, appliances, and other running services. It is measured **annually (kWh/year)** and accumulates over the building's life.

- **Key Difference:** EE is *locked in* at construction; operational energy is *recurring*. For an NZEB, operational energy is offset by renewables, but EE must still be minimized at the design stage.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six major categories of materials used in green construction**, with suitable examples for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 120 m² residential building uses **8,000 kg of OPC cement, 1,500 kg of steel, 20,000 kg of burnt clay bricks, and 100 kg of aluminium windows**. Calculate the **Total Embodied Carbon** and identify the **single highest-impact material** for replacement. Use the following data: $EC_{OPC}$ = 0.93, $EC_{steel}$ = 1.99, $EC_{brick}$ = 0.42, $EC_{aluminium}$ = 11.5 (all in kgCO₂e/kg). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Material Categories in Green Construction (1 mark per category):**

1. **Locally-Sourced Materials (1 mark):** Reduce transportation energy. *Examples:* Regionally quarried stone, locally manufactured fly-ash bricks, soil-based blocks (in Kerala: laterite stone, compressed earth blocks).

2. **Recycled-Content Materials (1 mark):** Reduce virgin resource extraction. *Examples:* Recycled aggregate concrete (RAC), recycled steel (rebar with ≥ 25 % scrap), reclaimed wood, recycled glass tiles.

3. **Rapidly Renewable Materials (1 mark):** Short harvest cycle (≤ 10 years). *Examples:* Bamboo (3–5 year cycle), cork, straw-bale, jute fibre, coir.

4. **Low-Embodied-Energy Materials (1 mark):** Materials with low process energy. *Examples:* Rammed earth (≈ 0.5 MJ/kg), lime mortar, unfired clay bricks, wood.

5. **Industrial Waste-Based Materials (1 mark):** Convert waste streams to building products. *Examples:* Fly-ash bricks, blast-furnace slag cement, phosphogypsum wall panels, silica-fume concrete.

6. **Low-VOC / Health-Positive Materials (1 mark):** Protect indoor air quality. *Examples:* Zero-VOC paints, water-based adhesives, natural linoleum flooring, formaldehyde-free MDF.

7. **Durable / Long-Life Materials (1 mark):** Extended service life reduces replacement energy. *Examples:* High-performance concrete (HPC), weathering steel, fibre-reinforced polymers.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Governing Equation:**

$$
EC_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \times EC_i
$$

**Step 1 — EC of Cement (2 marks):**

$$
EC_{cement} = 8{,}000 \times 0.93 = 7{,}440 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 2 — EC of Steel:**

$$
EC_{steel} = 1{,}500 \times 1.99 = 2{,}985 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 3 — EC of Bricks:**

$$
EC_{bricks} = 20{,}000 \times 0.42 = 8{,}400 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — EC of Aluminium:**

$$
EC_{aluminium} = 100 \times 11.5 = 1{,}150 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 5 — Total EC (1 mark):**

$$
EC_{total} = 7{,}440 + 2{,}985 + 8{,}400 + 1{,}150 = 19{,}975 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
\boxed{EC_{total} \approx 19.98 \text{ tCO}_2\text{e}}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify Highest-Impact Material (2 marks):**

Although aluminium has the highest *per-kg* EC, the **highest absolute contributor is burnt clay bricks (8,400 kgCO₂e)**, closely followed by cement (7,440 kgCO₂e).

**Recommended Replacement:** Substitute burnt clay bricks with **fly-ash bricks (EC ≈ 0.20 kgCO₂e/kg)**:

$$
EC_{new\_bricks} = 20{,}000 \times 0.20 = 4{,}000 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
\Delta EC = 8{,}400 - 4{,}000 = 4{,}400 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e} \text{ avoided}
$$

**Per-kg Concern:** Aluminium has the **highest embodied carbon per unit mass (11.5 kgCO₂e/kg)** and warrants **UPVC or FSC-wood replacement** for windows.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **Life-Cycle Assessment (LCA) stages** of a building as per **EN 15978 / ISO 14040** framework. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 2 kWp rooftop solar PV system has an **embodied energy of 4,500 kWh per kWp installed**. The system generates **1,500 kWh/kWp/yr**. The **grid emission factor of Kerala is 0.74 kgCO₂e/kWh**. Calculate (i) **Energy Payback Period (EPBT)** and (ii) **Carbon Payback Period (CPBT)** for the system. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**LCA Stages (5 stages × 1 mark each + integration = 6 marks, sketch = 1 mark):**

The ISO 14040 / EN 15978 framework divides a building's life into **5 modules**:

1. **Product Stage (A1–A3):** Raw material extraction, transport to factory, manufacturing. *Example: mining silica, smelting silicon, producing PV cells.*
2. **Construction Stage (A4–A5):** Transport to site + installation/construction process. *Example: truck delivery of panels, mounting on rooftop.*
3. **Use Stage (B1–B7):** Includes use, maintenance, repair, replacement, refurbishment. *Example: cleaning panels, replacing inverters.*
4. **End-of-Life Stage (C1–C4):** Deconstruction, transport to waste processing, waste processing, final disposal. *Example: dismantling panels, landfilling.*
5. **Benefits Beyond System (Module D):** Credits for recycling, energy recovery, and avoided burden in future systems. *Example: aluminium frame recycled into new ingots.*

**Neat Sketch (Block Diagram in SECTION 4.2 — reference):**
Use a **linear flow diagram** from `Raw Material → Construction → Use → End-of-Life → Recovery`. Mention **feedback loops** from Module D back to Module A.

**Valuation Tip:** A diagram is **compulsory** for 7-mark questions; minimum 5 blocks, arrows showing flow, and at least one **feedback loop** (Module D) earns full credit.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- System size: $P = 2 \text{ kWp}$
- EE per kWp: $EE_{per\_kWp} = 4{,}500 \text{ kWh/kWp}$
- Annual generation per kWp: $G = 1{,}500 \text{ kWh/kWp/yr}$
- Grid emission factor (Kerala): $EF_{grid} = 0.74 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e/kWh}$

**Step 1 — Total Embodied Energy of the System (1 mark):**

$$
EE_{system} = P \times EE_{per\_kWp} = 2 \times 4{,}500 = 9{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Annual Generation (1 mark):**

$$
E_{annual} = P \times G = 2 \times 1{,}500 = 3{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Payback Period (EPBT) (2 marks):**

$$
EPBT = \dfrac{EE_{system}}{E_{annual}} = \dfrac{9{,}000}{3{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{EPBT = 3.0 \text{ years}}
$$

**Step 4 — Embodied Carbon of the System (1 mark):**

Assume embodied energy → embodied carbon via Indian grid factor:
$EE_{system} = 9{,}000 \text{ kWh} \Rightarrow EC_{system} = 9{,}000 \times 0.74 = 6{,}660 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e}$

*(Alternatively, take $EC_{PV} \approx 1{,}500$ kgCO₂e/kWp)*

**Step 5 — Annual Carbon Offset (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = E_{annual} \times EF_{grid} = 3{,}000 \times 0.74 = 2{,}220 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Carbon Payback Period (CPBT) (1 mark):**

$$
CPBT = \dfrac{EC_{system}}{\Delta CO_2} = \dfrac{6{,}660}{2{,}220} = 3.0 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{CPBT = 3.0 \text{ years}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The PV system recovers both its energy and carbon cost in **3 years**, while it has a service life of **25+ years** — a textbook KTU example of net-positive green investment.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) and Embodied Carbon (kgCO₂e/kg).** They are NOT the same quantity. EE is energy; EC is greenhouse gas. Always write units.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize** to per-m² when asked. Examiners explicitly look for $EE/m^2$ and $EC/m^2$ metrics in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Omitting the sketch in LCA questions** in 7-mark sub-parts. This alone costs **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing rating systems**: GRIHA is Indian (TERI), IGBC is Indian (CII), LEED is American (USGBC), BREEAM is British. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Skipping the "why"**: Don't just list principles — explain the *engineering rationale* (e.g., *why* fly-ash bricks reduce EC). Examiners reward reasoning, not bullet points.
> 6. **Wrong unit on emission factor**: Indian grid = ~0.74–0.82 kgCO₂e/kWh. Using 0.5 (older value) or 0.45 (global average) loses marks.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Recall):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Green Construction = life-cycle, resource-efficient, occupant-healthy, economically viable building paradigm.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Environment + Society + Economy (Triple Bottom Line).
- ✅ **Six Principles:** Site, Water, Energy, Materials, IEQ, Waste.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $EE = \sum M_i \times EE_i$; $EC = \sum M_i \times EC_i$.
- ✅ **Key Indian Metrics:** GRIHA ratings (1–5 ★), IGBC levels, ECBC / ECBC+ compliance.
- ✅ **Threshold Values to Memorize:**
   - LPCD: **135 litres**
   - Indoor CO₂: **< 800 ppm**
   - Daylight Factor: **2 %–5 %**
   - C&D diversion: **≥ 75 %**
   - Embodied energy: cement ≈ **4.8 MJ/kg**, aluminium ≈ **210 MJ/kg**, bamboo ≈ **0.5 MJ/kg**
- ✅ **LCA Stages:** A (Product + Construction) → B (Use) → C (End-of-Life) → D (Recovery benefits).
- ✅ **EPBT/CPBT typical for Indian solar PV:** **2–4 years**.
- ✅ **5R Hierarchy:** Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover.
- ✅ **Key Indian Context:** Kerala grid emission factor = **0.74 kgCO₂e/kWh**; National ambient air quality standards under CPCB; Smart Cities Mission, ECBC 2017, Energy Conservation Act 2001.
- ✅ **Net-Zero** = Operational Energy offset by on-site renewables. **Net-Positive** = building produces more energy than it consumes.
- ✅ **Front-loading rule:** ~80 % of sustainability decisions happen in the first ~20 % of design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Green Transportation** (also termed *Sustainable Transportation* or *Eco-Mobility*) refers to the integrated system of mobility solutions, vehicle technologies, fuels, and infrastructure planning that collectively minimize the environmental footprint of passenger and freight movement across its entire **life cycle** — from raw material extraction, manufacturing, and operation to end-of-life recycling.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, Green Transportation is defined as a **holistic engineering paradigm** that satisfies three simultaneous objectives:
1. **Environmental Sustainability** – Reduction of **Greenhouse Gas (GHG)** emissions, particulate matter (PM2.5/PM10), **NOx**, **CO**, and **SOx**.
2. **Economic Viability** – Acceptable **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for users and operators.
3. **Social Equity** – Universal accessibility, safety, and reduced urban congestion.

The standard benchmark metric used globally (and referenced by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, India) is:

$$\text{Well-to-Wheel (WTW) CO}_2\text{ Emissions} = \text{Well-to-Tank (WTT)} + \text{Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Green Transportation is **not just electric vehicles**. It is a multi-pillar strategy encompassing vehicle electrification, alternative fuels, modal shift to public/non-motorized transport, intelligent transport systems (ITS), and circular economy practices in automotive manufacturing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "City as a Living Organism"

Imagine a city is a **human body**. Traditional transportation — gasoline cars, diesel trucks — is like a bloodstream carrying a **slow poison**. Every kilometre travelled injects **CO₂, NOx, and particulate matter** into the "lungs" of the city, causing respiratory diseases, climate fever, and noise pollution (a constant headache).

**Green Transportation is the medicine for this body**:
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** are like a clean, oxygenated blood transfusion — they run on electricity that can be generated from renewable sources (solar, wind, hydro).
- **Public Transport (Metro, BRT)** is the efficient, shared circulatory system — fewer vehicles, less congestion.
- **Non-Motorized Transport (cycling, walking)** is the natural immune system — zero emissions, healthy for the user.
- **Biofuels & Hydrogen** are the herbal supplements — renewable, biodegradable, lower toxicity.
- **Smart Traffic Management (ITS)** is the brain's neural network — optimising flow so the body wastes less energy.

Just as you would not treat a sick body with one pill, you cannot make transport green with EVs alone. It requires a **systems-level prescription**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — The "3 Pillars" of Green Transport (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Avoid** – Reduce the need to travel (urban planning, work-from-home).
> 2. **Shift** – Move from private to public/shared modes.
> 3. **Improve** – Make the vehicles themselves cleaner and more efficient.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Unit | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Tailpipe CO₂ (Petrol car) | **~120–200 g CO₂/km** | Average Indian passenger car |
| Tailpipe CO₂ (Diesel car) | **~110–170 g CO₂/km** | Slightly lower CO₂ but higher NOx/PM |
| Tailpipe CO₂ (Battery EV, India grid) | **~80–120 g CO₂/km** | Depends on grid carbon intensity |
| Tailpipe CO₂ (BEV, 100% renewable grid) | **~0 g CO₂/km** (operational) | Theoretical ideal |
| Energy Density — Gasoline | **~34 MJ/litre** (≈ 8.9 kWh/L) | Reference fuel |
| Energy Density — Diesel | **~38 MJ/litre** (≈ 10.6 kWh/L) | Reference fuel |
| Energy Density — Lithium-ion battery | **~0.5–0.9 MJ/kg** (≈ 150–250 Wh/kg) | Key EV limitation |
| 1 Litre of petrol ≈ | **2.36 kg CO₂ emitted** | WTT+TTW combustion |
| 1 kWh electricity (India avg grid) | **~0.7–0.82 kg CO₂** | CEA 2023 grid factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often frame questions asking you to **compare** the carbon footprint of an EV vs ICE vehicle. You must state **both operational emissions AND embodied (manufacturing) emissions**, because the lithium-ion battery itself has a high manufacturing carbon debt of **~150–200 kg CO₂ per kWh** of battery capacity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Strategic Pillars of Green Transportation

Green Transportation is structured around five engineering and policy pillars. Each pillar represents a distinct **intervention point** in the mobility system.

### Pillar 1 — Vehicle Electrification (EVs, HEVs, FCEVs)
- **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV):** Zero tailpipe emissions. Energy stored in **lithium-ion / solid-state / LFP batteries**.
- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV):** Combine ICE + electric motor. Cannot plug in. Examples: Toyota Prius.
- **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEV):** Larger battery, can charge externally. Range ~30–80 km electric.
- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV):** Use **hydrogen** to generate electricity onboard via PEM fuel cell. Only emission is water vapour.

### Pillar 2 — Alternative & Low-Carbon Fuels
- **First-generation biofuels:** Bioethanol from sugarcane/corn, biodiesel from soybean/palm oil (food-vs-fuel conflict).
- **Second-generation biofuels:** From lignocellulosic biomass, agricultural waste (no food conflict).
- **Third-generation biofuels:** From algae — high yield, no arable land.
- **Synthetic e-fuels (e-diesel, e-methane):** Produced by combining **green H₂** with captured **CO₂** (Power-to-Liquid).

### Pillar 3 — Public & Shared Mobility
- **Mass Rapid Transit Systems (MRTS):** Metro, monorail, suburban rail.
- **Bus Rapid Transit (BRT):** Dedicated bus corridors with high-frequency service.
- **Shared mobility:** Carpooling, ride-sharing (Ola/Uber pool), bike-sharing, scooter-sharing.

### Pillar 4 — Non-Motorized Transport (NMT)
- Walking infrastructure (sidewalks, pedestrian plazas).
- Cycling networks (cycle tracks, public bike-share, e-bikes).
- **Why:** A bicycle produces **~5 g CO₂/km** (embodied) vs **~150 g CO₂/km** for a petrol scooter.

### Pillar 5 — Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS) & Smart Logistics
- **AI-based traffic signal optimization** reduces idle time and fuel waste.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC),** platooning, eco-driving assistance.
- **Smart freight:** Load consolidation, route optimization, modal shift to rail/waterways.
- **MaaS (Mobility as a Service):** Single digital platform integrating all modes.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — Engineering Logic

| Pillar | Core "Why" | Core "How" |
|---|---|---|
| Electrification | Internal combustion is **<40% efficient**; electric motors are **>85% efficient** | Replace mechanical drivetrain with electric powertrain |
| Alternative fuels | Liquid hydrocarbons have **inherent carbon**; need renewable carbon | Use biomass-sourced or synthetic carbon |
| Public transport | Occupancy efficiency: 1 bus = **40 cars off road** | Increase passenger-km per litre of fuel |
| NMT | Zero energy conversion, zero emissions | Replace motorised trips with human-powered ones |
| ITS | **20–30% of urban fuel** is wasted in congestion | Use data + AI to smooth traffic flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cognitive Tip:** When asked "Discuss green transportation strategies," structure your answer using these 5 pillars. Examiners award marks for **structured classification**, not just listing random points.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FOR EXAM HALL:** Memorise these equations. They appear frequently in Part B 14-mark questions on carbon accounting, fuel economy, and EV energy efficiency.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{TTW CO}_2 = m_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{fuel}}$ | Tank-to-Wheel emissions = fuel mass × emission factor | g CO₂ |
| 2 | $\text{WTT CO}_2 = E_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{grid/source}}$ | Well-to-Tank emissions (extraction, refining, transport of fuel) | g CO₂ |
| 3 | $\text{WTW} = \text{WTT} + \text{TTW}$ | Total life-cycle fuel-phase emissions | g CO₂/km |
| 4 | $\text{BEV equivalent CO}_2 = \dfrac{E_{\text{consumption}} \times \text{EF}_{\text{grid}}}{\text{Distance}}$ | Grid-intensity based emissions of an EV | g CO₂/km |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{engine (ICE)}} \approx 25\text{–}40\%$ | Indicated thermal efficiency of gasoline/diesel engine | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{electric motor}} \approx 85\text{–}95\%$ | Efficiency of traction electric motor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Energy density (Wh/kg)} = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{battery}}}$ | Battery gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg |
| 8 | $\text{Range (km)} = \dfrac{E_{\text{battery}} \times 1000}{\text{Wh/km consumption}}$ | Practical EV driving range | km |
| 9 | $\text{FC}_{\text{biof}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{biofuel}} \times LHV_{\text{biofuel}}}{V_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Carbon intensity of biofuel (lower heating value) | MJ/L |
| 10 | $\text{TCO} = C_{\text{buy}} + C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} - S_{\text{salvage}}$ | Total Cost of Ownership over vehicle life | ₹ or $ |
| 11 | $\text{PM emission} = \text{PM} \propto \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{combustion}}}$ | Particulate matter inversely related to combustion efficiency | qualitative |
| 12 | $\text{H}_2\text{O production (FCEV)} = \dfrac{m_{\text{H}_2}}{M_{\text{H}_2}} \times 9 \text{ g}$ | Stoichiometric water from hydrogen oxidation | g H₂O |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above drive:
- **India's FAME-II scheme** (Faster Adoption & Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) — subsidies for 2W, 3W, 4W, and buses.
- **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** emission norms — leapfrogging from BS-IV to BS-VI in 2020.
- **Pune Bicycle Plan, Kochi Metro, Delhi BRT** — Indian implementations of NMT and public transport.
- **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Tesla Model 3/Y** — global FCEV and BEV benchmarks.
- **Shell's "Refulle"** and India's **National Hydrogen Mission (2021)** — building hydrogen ecosystems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Emissions Comparison: ICE vs BEV

**Problem Statement:** A petrol car consumes 6 litres of petrol per 100 km. The Indian grid emission factor is **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**. A comparable BEV consumes 15 kWh per 100 km. Compare the operational (TTW) emissions and discuss WTW emissions.

### Step 1 — Compute TTW (Tank-to-Wheel) Emissions of ICE Vehicle

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel consumed} &= 6 \text{ L/100 km} \\
\text{Density of petrol} &= 0.745 \text{ kg/L} \\
\text{Mass of fuel} &= 6 \times 0.745 = 4.47 \text{ kg/100 km} \\
\text{Emission factor of petrol (TTW)} &= 3.18 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kg fuel} \\
\text{TTW CO}_2 &= 4.47 \times 3.18 = 14.21 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/100 km} \\
\text{Per km: } \text{TTW}_{\text{ICE}} &= 142.1 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute TTW (Tailpipe) Emissions of BEV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEV has NO tailpipe emissions} &\Rightarrow \text{TTW}_{\text{BEV}} = 0 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{However, the electricity source has upstream emissions.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute WTT (Upstream) Emissions of BEV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEV energy consumption} &= 15 \text{ kWh/100 km} \\
\text{Indian grid emission factor (CEA 2023)} &= 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kWh} \\
\text{Upstream CO}_2\text{ (operational equivalent)} &= 15 \times 0.82 = 12.3 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/100 km} \\
\text{Per km: } \text{Operational}_{\text{BEV}} &= 123 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Add Embodied (Manufacturing) Emissions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEV battery size} &= 50 \text{ kWh (typical)} \\
\text{Embodied CO}_2\text{ per kWh of Li-ion battery} &= 175 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kWh (global avg)} \\
\text{Battery manufacturing CO}_2 &= 50 \times 175 = 8750 \text{ kg CO}_2 \\
\text{Assumed vehicle life} &= 150{,}000 \text{ km} \\
\text{Embodied emissions per km} &= \dfrac{8750}{150{,}000} = 58.3 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{Total ICE embodied (steel + aluminium)} &\approx 30 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{Net life-cycle BEV} &= 123 + 58.3 = 181.3 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{Net life-cycle ICE} &= 142.1 + 30 = 172.1 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Net Comparison and "Crossover Point"

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Embodied CO}_2\text{ gap (BEV extra)} &= 8750 - (\text{ICE embodied}) \\
&\approx 8750 - 4500 = 4250 \text{ kg CO}_2 \\
\text{Operational saving per km (BEV vs ICE)} &= 142.1 - 123 = 19.1 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{Crossover distance} &= \dfrac{4250 \text{ kg CO}_2}{19.1 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}} = \dfrac{4{,}250{,}000 \text{ g}}{19.1 \text{ g/km}} \\
&\approx 222{,}513 \text{ km} \\
\text{On India's current grid, BEV} &\approx \text{ICE over typical 150,000 km life.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** With India's coal-heavy grid (still ~70% fossil in 2024), a **BEV only becomes carbon-competitive after ~2.2 lakh km** of driving. This is a frequent KTU exam discussion point — the answer is **"it depends on the grid."**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — EV Energy Consumption and Range

**Problem Statement:** An electric scooter has a **2.5 kWh battery** and consumes **40 Wh/km**. Compute the practical range. If the rider upgrades to a **3.2 kWh battery**, what is the new range?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range}_{\text{old}} &= \dfrac{E_{\text{battery}} \times 1000}{E_{\text{consumption per km}}} \\
&= \dfrac{2.5 \times 1000}{40} \\
&= 62.5 \text{ km} \\
\text{Range}_{\text{new}} &= \dfrac{3.2 \times 1000}{40} \\
&= 80 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Real-world de-rating factor:** Batteries are not allowed to discharge 100% (to protect longevity). Usable SoC window is typically **80–90%**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Realistic range} &= \text{Theoretical} \times 0.85 \\
\text{Realistic}_{2.5 \text{ kWh}} &= 62.5 \times 0.85 = 53.1 \text{ km} \\
\text{Realistic}_{3.2 \text{ kWh}} &= 80 \times 0.85 = 68 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCEV) Water Emission

**Problem Statement:** A Toyota Mirai carries **5.6 kg of hydrogen** at 700 bar. Calculate the mass of water produced and compare with tailpipe CO₂ of an equivalent petrol car doing the same range (~650 km).

### Step 1 — Water from Hydrogen Combustion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chemical reaction:} \quad 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Molar mass of H}_2 &= 2 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Molar mass of H}_2\text{O} &= 18 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Water per kg of H}_2 &= \dfrac{18}{2} = 9 \text{ kg H}_2\text{O per kg H}_2 \\
\text{Total water} &= 5.6 \times 9 = 50.4 \text{ kg H}_2\text{O per full tank}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Equivalent Petrol Car

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Petrol consumption for 650 km} &\approx 39 \text{ L} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ produced} &= 39 \times 2.36 = 92.04 \text{ kg CO}_2 \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ per km} &= \dfrac{92{,}040}{650} = 141.6 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Net Comparison

| Vehicle | Energy Carried | Tailpipe Output |
|---|---|---|
| Toyota Mirai (FCEV) | 5.6 kg H₂ | 50.4 kg H₂O (zero CO₂) |
| Equivalent Petrol Car | 39 L petrol | 92.04 kg CO₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The water vapour from FCEVs is *not* zero environmental impact — it is a greenhouse gas itself, but with GWP ~1 over short timescales (much less than CO₂'s GWP of 1 over 100 years baseline, or higher for short-lived climate pollutants). The upstream hydrogen production (grey/blue/green) is where the real emissions lie.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Biofuel Carbon Neutrality Math

**Problem Statement:** Sugarcane-based ethanol has an emission factor of **1.5 kg CO₂/L** (combustion) but the sugarcane plant absorbed **2.3 kg CO₂/L** during growth. Compute net lifecycle CO₂.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Combustion emissions (TTW)} &= +1.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L} \\
\text{Biomass uptake (biogenic carbon)} &= -2.3 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L} \\
\text{Net bio-CC} &= 1.5 - 2.3 = -0.8 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L (net negative)} \\
\text{But: } \text{Fertiliser, transport, refining WTT} &= +0.9 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L} \\
\text{True WTW ethanol} &= -0.8 + 0.9 = +0.1 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L} \\
\text{Vs petrol WTW} &= 2.36 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L} \\
\text{Percentage reduction} &= \dfrac{2.36 - 0.1}{2.36} \times 100 = 95.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** First-generation ethanol is only **~70–95% carbon-saving** in real life (not 100% "carbon neutral" as commonly claimed) due to agricultural inputs, land-use change, and refining energy.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Green Transport Strategy

| Scenario | Best Green Transport Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Urban commute < 5 km, individual | **E-bicycle / e-scooter** | Lowest lifecycle emissions, no congestion |
| Urban commute 5–15 km, individual | **BEV 2W or compact BEV car** | Zero tailpipe, low energy use |
| Inter-city 50–300 km | **Hybrid/Plug-in hybrid** OR **BEV with fast charging** | Balance of range and emissions |
| Long-haul > 500 km, low utilisation | **Efficient diesel/petrol OR HVO biofuel** | BEV range still limited |
| Heavy freight, long-distance | **Electric rail OR hydrogen truck** | Mass efficiency + decarbonisation |
| Dense city mass transport | **Metro, BRT, electric bus** | Highest passenger-km/kWh |
| Rural/short-haul, low-income | **Biodiesel from used cooking oil (UCOME)** | Decentralised, low CAPEX |
