# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answers below are exactly what a KTU examiner expects: 3–4 crisp lines, key terms underlined (or in bold here), and a closing line connecting to sustainability.**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define green transportation. List any three sustainable transport modes.**

**Model Answer:**
Green transportation refers to mobility systems and technologies that minimize environmental impact across the vehicle life cycle, including low-emission fuels, efficient powertrains, and modal shifts to public or non-motorized transport. **Three sustainable transport modes** are: (i) Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) powered by renewable electricity, (ii) Mass Rapid Transit Systems (Metro/BRT) with high passenger occupancy, and (iii) Non-Motorized Transport (walking and cycling). Collectively they reduce **GHG emissions**, urban air pollution, and fossil fuel dependency, aligning with SDG-11 (Sustainable Cities) and SDG-13 (Climate Action).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**What are biofuels? Differentiate between first-generation and second-generation biofuels.**

**Model Answer:**
Biofuels are liquid or gaseous fuels derived from **biomass** (organic plant or animal matter) that serve as renewable alternatives to fossil fuels. **First-generation biofuels** (e.g., sugarcane ethanol, soybean biodiesel) are produced from food crops and compete with food supply ("food vs fuel" debate). **Second-generation biofuels** are produced from **lignocellulosic biomass** such as agricultural residue, wood waste, or dedicated energy crops, avoiding food competition but requiring more complex conversion technologies like enzymatic hydrolysis or Fischer-Tropsch synthesis. Second-gen fuels offer higher net carbon savings and better land-use efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Generic Answers Lose 4–5 Marks:**
> Students often write a "Wikipedia-style" essay with no structure, no numbers, and no India-specific examples. Examiners *expect*:
> 1. Clear **classification / taxonomy** (use headings, sub-points).
> 2. **At least 2 numerical comparisons** (e.g., g CO₂/km, kWh/km, ₹/km).
> 3. **India-specific case study** (FAME-II, Gati Shakti, BS-VI, National Hydrogen Mission).
> 4. A **balanced conclusion** acknowledging trade-offs (grid dependency, mining impacts, etc.).
> Losing any one of these → you drop to a 9/14 instead of 14/14.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of Green Transportation and describe the **five strategic pillars** of sustainable mobility with suitable examples. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) CO₂ emissions** of a petrol car and a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) on India's current grid. Discuss the role of grid decarbonisation in making EVs truly green. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**1. Definition:**
Green transportation is a **systems-level approach** to mobility that integrates vehicle technology, fuel choice, infrastructure, and traveller behaviour to reduce environmental impact per passenger-km, while remaining economically viable and socially inclusive.

**2. The Five Strategic Pillars:**

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar 1 — Vehicle Electrification** | Replace ICE with electric powertrains | Tata Nexon EV, Ather 450X, Ola Electric S1 |
| **Pillar 2 — Alternative Fuels** | Use biofuels, hydrogen, e-fuels | Ethanol-blended petrol (E20 in India), green H₂ |
| **Pillar 3 — Public & Shared Mobility** | High-occupancy mass transit and shared services | Kochi Metro, Delhi BRT, Uber Pool |
| **Pillar 4 — Non-Motorized Transport (NMT)** | Walking and cycling infrastructure | Pune Bicycle Plan, Mysuru public bike-share |
| **Pillar 5 — Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)** | AI/IoT for traffic, freight, MaaS | Adaptive traffic signals in Bengaluru, FASTag |

**3. Why all five together:** A single pillar is insufficient. EVs alone cannot solve congestion; biofuels alone cannot scale. A **systems integration** yields 60–80% emission reduction.

**4. Conclusion:** Green transportation requires coordinated action across technology, policy (FAME-II, BS-VI norms), urban planning, and citizen behaviour.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Step 1 — ICE Car (Petrol) WTW Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel economy} &= 15 \text{ km/L (assumed)} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ emission factor (petrol)} &= 2.36 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/L} \\
\text{TTW CO}_2 &= \dfrac{2.36}{15} = 0.157 \text{ kg/km} = 157 \text{ g/km} \\
\text{WTT CO}_2 &\approx 30 \text{ g/km (extraction, refining)} \\
\text{Total WTW}_{\text{ICE}} &\approx 187 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — BEV WTW Calculation (India grid):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV consumption} &= 15 \text{ kWh/100 km} \\
\text{Indian grid EF} &= 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kWh (CEA 2023)} \\
\text{Operational emissions} &= \dfrac{15 \times 0.82}{100} = 123 \text{ g/km} \\
\text{Embodied (manufacturing) emissions} &\approx 60 \text{ g/km (lifecycle) } \\
\text{Total WTW}_{\text{BEV}} &\approx 183 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Petrol ICE | Battery EV (India grid) |
|---|---|---|
| TTW (tailpipe/operational) | 157 g CO₂/km | 123 g CO₂/km (grid) |
| WTT (upstream) | 30 g CO₂/km | 60 g CO₂/km (battery mfg.) |
| **Total WTW** | **~187 g CO₂/km** | **~183 g CO₂/km** |
| Local air pollution (PM, NOx) | High | **Zero (tailpipe)** |

**Step 4 — Role of Grid Decarbonisation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If grid EF drops to 0.4 kg/kWh (50% renewable mix):} \\
\text{BEV operational emissions} &= 15 \times 0.4 / 100 = 60 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{Total WTW}_{\text{BEV}} &\approx 120 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km} \\
\text{Saving vs ICE} &= \dfrac{187 - 120}{187} \times 100 \approx 36\% \\
\text{If grid is 100% renewable (0.1 kg/kWh):} \\
\text{Saving vs ICE} &\approx 70\text{–}80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
On India's current coal-heavy grid, BEVs provide **marginal WTW benefit** but **major local air quality benefit** (zero tailpipe PM/NOx). As the grid decarbonises (target: 500 GW non-fossil by 2030 per India's Panchamrit commitment), BEVs will become **substantially greener**. The transition is **a journey, not a switch.**

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**(a)** Discuss **alternative fuels for transportation**: biofuels (1st, 2nd, 3rd generation), hydrogen, and e-fuels. Compare their Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions with conventional petrol. (7 marks)
**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) powertrain**. Discuss the advantages and limitations of BEVs as a green transport solution. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**1. Biofuels — Three Generations:**

- **1st Gen:** Sugar/starch to ethanol (sugarcane in Brazil/India), oilseeds to biodiesel (soybean, palm). **WTW saving: ~60–80%** vs petrol, but food-vs-fuel conflict.
- **2nd Gen:** Lignocellulosic biomass (rice straw, bagasse, wood chips) → cellulosic ethanol via enzymatic hydrolysis. **WTW saving: ~80–90%.** No food conflict.
- **3rd Gen:** Algae-based biodiesel. **Yield per hectare: 10–20× higher than palm oil.** No arable land. Commercial scale still emerging.

**2. Hydrogen:**

- **Grey H₂:** From natural gas reforming. ~10 kg CO₂ per kg H₂. **Not green.**
- **Blue H₂:** Grey + carbon capture & storage (CCS). ~3 kg CO₂ per kg H₂.
- **Green H₂:** Electrolysis using renewable electricity. **~0–1 kg CO₂ per kg H₂.** Truly green.
- Used in **PEM fuel cells (FCEVs).** Tailpipe: only water.

**3. E-fuels (Power-to-Liquid):**

- Green H₂ + captured CO₂ → synthetic methane or e-diesel (Fischer-Tropsch).
- **Carbon neutral IF** both H₂ and CO₂ are renewable/recycled.
- Drop-in replacement for existing ICE vehicles and fuel infrastructure.

**4. WTW Comparison Table:**

| Fuel | TTW Emissions (g CO₂/km equiv.) | WTT Emissions (g CO₂/km) | Net WTW | Green? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Petrol | 142 | 30 | 172 | ❌ |
| Diesel | 130 | 35 | 165 | ❌ |
| 1st-gen Ethanol (E20) | 95 | 50 | 145 | ⚠️ Partial |
| 2nd-gen Cellulosic | 30 | 40 | 70 | ✅ |
| Green H₂ (FCEV) | 0 | 30 (electrolysis) | 30 | ✅ Best |
| E-diesel (renewable) | 130 | 20 | 150 | ⚠️ Partial |

**5. Conclusion:** Green hydrogen (FCEV) has the **lowest WTW emissions**, followed by 2nd-gen biofuels. However, infrastructure for hydrogen is still developing, while biofuels leverage existing fuel distribution.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**1. BEV Powertrain Block Diagram (Functional Architecture):**

```
[Battery Pack] → [Battery Management System BMS] → [Power Inverter] → [Electric Traction Motor] → [Transmission Single Speed] → [Wheels]
       ↑                                                                                                            ↓
       ←←←←←←←←←←← [Onboard Charger OBC ← AC Grid / DC Fast Charger] ←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←← [Regenerative Braking]
```

**2. Component Functions:**

- **Battery Pack:** Li-ion cells, typically 30–100 kWh, 400V or 800V architecture.
- **BMS:** Monitors cell voltage, temperature, state-of-charge (SoC); prevents overcharge/over-discharge.
- **Power Inverter:** Converts DC battery to AC for the motor (3-phase).
- **Traction Motor:** Permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) or induction motor. Efficiency >90%.
- **Transmission:** Single-speed reduction gearbox (no multi-gear needed due to motor's flat torque curve).
- **Regenerative Braking:** Converts kinetic energy back to electrical energy during deceleration.
- **Onboard Charger (OBC):** Converts AC grid supply to DC for slow charging.

**3. Advantages:**

- Zero tailpipe emissions — improves urban air quality dramatically.
- High energy efficiency (motor >90% vs ICE 25–40%).
- Lower operating cost (~₹1/km vs ₹6/km for petrol).
- Low maintenance (fewer moving parts, no oil change).
- Silent operation, low NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
- Enables vehicle-to-grid (V2G) as distributed energy storage.

**4. Limitations:**

- **Range anxiety:** Typical BEV range 250–500 km vs 600+ for ICE.
- **Charging time:** 30 min (DC fast) to 8 hours (AC home).
- **Battery cost:** Battery = 30–40% of vehicle cost.
- **Charging infrastructure:** Sparse in Tier-2/3 Indian cities.
- **Grid dependency:** Emissions shift from tailpipe to smokestack.
- **Battery degradation:** 70–80% capacity after 8–10 years / 1.5 lakh km.
- **Raw materials:** Lithium, cobalt, nickel mining has ecological and ethical concerns.

**5. Conclusion:** BEVs are a **critical pillar** of green transport, especially as the grid decarbonises. Their full environmental benefit emerges only when paired with **renewable electricity generation, ethical battery supply chains, and end-of-life recycling**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before exam — read this 3 times.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Green transportation = low-emission, energy-efficient, socially inclusive mobility across the full life cycle.
- ✅ **Five pillars:** (1) Electrification, (2) Alternative fuels, (3) Public/shared, (4) NMT, (5) ITS/MaaS.
- ✅ **Key WTW equation:** `WTW = WTT + TTW`. Always state BOTH.
- ✅ **Standard emission factors to memorise:**
  - Petrol = **2.36 kg CO₂/L**
  - Diesel = **2.68 kg CO₂/L**
  - Indian grid ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**
  - EV battery manufacturing ≈ **150–200 kg CO₂/kWh**
- ✅ **EV consumption:** Typical car = 15–20 kWh/100 km; e-scooter = 30–50 Wh/km.
- ✅ **Hydrogen FCEV:** Tailpipe = water; upstream = depends on grey/blue/green H₂ source.
- ✅ **Biofuel generations:** 1st (food crops), 2nd (lignocellulose), 3rd (algae).
- ✅ **The 3 Avoid-Shift-Improve principle:** foundational to KTU 2024 answers.
- ✅ **India-specific keywords examiners love:** FAME-II, BS-VI, Gati Shakti, National Hydrogen Mission, E20 blending, SAANS (clean cooking parallel).
- ✅ **Smart mobility keywords:** ITS, ACC, platooning, V2G, MaaS.
- ✅ **A balanced answer** acknowledges trade-offs: BEVs depend on grid, biofuels have land-use issues, FCEVs need H₂ infrastructure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning — DO NOT say "EVs are zero emission" without qualification.** The correct phrasing is **"zero tailpipe emission, but upstream emissions depend on the grid carbon intensity."** Examiners mark this distinction strictly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Chemistry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Chemistry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Green Chemistry** is the design of chemical products and processes that **reduce or eliminate the use and generation of hazardous substances**. It is defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) as the *"design of chemical products and processes that reduce or eliminate the use or generation of hazardous substances."*

Green Chemistry operates on **twelve foundational principles** articulated by Paul Anastas and John Warner in their seminal 1998 book *"Green Chemistry: Theory and Practice"*. It is fundamentally a **proactive, pollution-prevention philosophy** rather than an end-of-pipe treatment approach (i.e., it prevents pollution at the molecular/atomic design stage rather than cleaning it up after it is created).

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen Chef vs. The Dishwasher**
>
> Imagine you are cooking a meal in a kitchen:
>
> - **Traditional Chemistry (with end-of-pipe treatment)** is like a chef who cooks a delicious meal but dirties every pot, pan, and surface in the kitchen, then hires a **dishwasher** to clean up afterward. The waste is *managed* after it is created.
>
> - **Green Chemistry** is like a chef who, from the very start, plans a one-pot meal, uses ingredients that are locally sourced and biodegradable, avoids sharp knives (hazardous substances), and leaves the kitchen almost spotless. There is barely anything to clean up.
>
> In short, **Green Chemistry = Prevent the mess, do not just clean the mess.**

## Key Sustainability Metrics in Green Chemistry

The discipline uses specific quantitative metrics to evaluate how "green" a chemical process is:

- **Atom Economy (AE)** — measures the efficiency of a reaction in incorporating reactant atoms into the final product. **Theoretical maximum is 100%.**
- **E-Factor (Environmental Factor)** — ratio of waste produced to desired product. **Industry ideal: < 1; Pharmaceutical reality: 25–100.**
- **Reaction Mass Efficiency (RME)** — ratio of mass of product to total mass of reactants.
- **Carbon Efficiency (CE)** — fraction of carbon in reactants that ends up in the final product.
- **Process Mass Intensity (PMI)** — total mass used in a process (including solvents) per mass of product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Green Chemistry in Module 4 emphasizes the **12 Principles**, the **atom economy concept**, **green solvents**, **catalysis**, and **real-world industrial case studies** (e.g., adipic acid synthesis, Ibuprofen synthesis, polycarbonate production).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The 12 Principles of Green Chemistry (Anastas & Warner, 1998)

### Principle 1: Prevention
It is better to **prevent waste** than to treat or clean up waste after it has been formed. This is the cardinal rule — *the cheapest way to deal with waste is to not make it in the first place.*

### Principle 2: Atom Economy
Synthetic methods should be designed to **maximize the incorporation of all materials used in the process into the final product**. This is the foundational quantitative principle of green chemistry.

### Principle 3: Less Hazardous Chemical Syntheses
Wherever practicable, synthetic methods should be designed to **use and generate substances with little to no toxicity to human health and the environment**.

### Principle 4: Designing Safer Chemicals
Chemical products should be designed to be **effective while minimizing their toxicity**. Function must be preserved while hazard is reduced (e.g., a pesticide that targets a specific pest receptor).

### Principle 5: Safer Solvents and Auxiliaries
The use of auxiliary substances (solvents, separation agents) should be made **unnecessary wherever possible and, when used, innocuous**.

### Principle 6: Design for Energy Efficiency
Energy requirements of chemical processes should be **minimized**. If possible, reactions should be carried out at **ambient temperature and pressure**. Conduct reactions with microwave, ultrasound, or photochemical activation to reduce energy.

### Principle 7: Use of Renewable Feedstocks
A raw material or feedstock should be **renewable** rather than depleting, wherever technical and economical. (Example: bio-ethanol from sugarcane vs. petroleum-derived ethylene.)

### Principle 8: Reduce Derivatives
Unnecessary derivatization (use of blocking groups, protection/deprotection steps) should be **minimized or avoided** because such steps require additional reagents and generate waste.

### Principle 9: Catalysis
**Catalytic reagents** are superior to stoichiometric reagents. Catalysts are used in small amounts and can carry out a single reaction many times (turnover number). They are preferable to stoichiometric reagents that are used in excess and become waste.

### Principle 10: Design for Degradation
Chemical products should be designed so that at the end of their useful life they **break down into innocuous degradation products** and do not persist in the environment (e.g., biodegradable polymers).

### Principle 11: Real-time Analysis for Pollution Prevention
Analytical methodologies need to be further developed to allow for **real-time, in-process monitoring** to detect and control hazardous substance formation before they are produced.

### Principle 12: Inherently Safer Chemistry for Accident Prevention
Substances used in chemical processes should be chosen to **minimize the potential for chemical accidents**, including releases, explosions, and fires.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Ideal Value** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Atom Economy (AE) | $AE = \frac{\text{MW of desired product}}{\sum \text{MW of all reactants}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | **100%** | Measures how efficiently atoms from reactants are incorporated into the product |
| E-Factor | $E = \frac{\text{Mass of total waste}}{\text{Mass of desired product}}$ | Dimensionless | **0** (ideal), < 1 (excellent), 25–100 (pharma) | Total waste generated per kg of product |
| Reaction Mass Efficiency (RME) | $RME = \frac{\text{Mass of product}}{\text{Total mass of reactants}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | **100%** | Combines atom economy with chemical yield |
| Process Mass Intensity (PMI) | $PMI = \frac{\text{Total mass input (incl. solvents)}}{\text{Mass of product}}$ | Dimensionless | **1** (minimum) | Total materials used per unit product |
| Carbon Efficiency (CE) | $CE = \frac{\text{C atoms in product}}{\text{C atoms in reactants}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | **100%** | Fraction of carbon retained in product |
| Turnover Number (TON) | $TON = \frac{\text{Moles of product}}{\text{Moles of catalyst}}$ | Dimensionless | **High (10³–10⁷)** | Catalyst productivity over its lifetime |
| Turnover Frequency (TOF) | $TOF = \frac{\text{Moles of product}}{\text{Moles of catalyst} \times \text{time (h)}}$ | h⁻¹ | **High (10²–10⁶)** | Rate of catalytic conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Notation:** All conditional/absolute expressions are rendered using $\mid$ or \vert — the standard ASCII pipe character is never used in table cells to preserve Markdown structural integrity.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Atom economy calculations drive the redesign of drug syntheses (e.g., BHC Company's Ibuprofen synthesis, which went from 6 steps → 3 steps, increasing AE from ~40% to ~99%).
- **Polymer Industry:** Use of CO₂ as a feedstock for polycarbonates (replacing phosgene).
- **Petrochemicals:** Replacement of HF (hydrogen fluoride) alkylation catalysts with solid acid catalysts (safer chemistry).
- **Agrochemicals:** Design of biodegradable pesticides that degrade into harmless products.
- **Academic Research:** The Green Chemistry Institute runs the "Green Chemistry Challenge Awards" annually.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## Example 1: Atom Economy Calculation (KTU Board Favorite Problem)

### Problem Statement
Calculate the **Atom Economy** for the following two industrial syntheses of Ibuprofen and identify which route is "greener":

**Old Route (Boot's, 1960s):**
$$C_4H_6O_3 + C_{10}H_{14} \rightarrow C_{13}H_{18}O_3 + H_2O$$
*(Isobutyraldehyde + Isobutylbenzene → Ibuprofen intermediate + water byproduct)*

**New Route (BHC Company, 1990s):**
$$C_4H_6O_3 + C_{10}H_{14} \rightarrow C_{13}H_{18}O_3 + H_2O$$
*(Same overall stoichiometry but only 3 catalytic steps instead of 6 stoichiometric steps)*

Wait — for a more illustrative example, consider the **Diels-Alder vs. addition-then-elimination** comparison:

**Reaction A (Diels-Alder, 100% atom economy):**
$$C_4H_6 + C_2H_4 \rightarrow C_6H_{10}$$

**Reaction B (Substitution with byproducts):**
$$C_4H_6 + Br_2 \rightarrow C_4H_6Br_2 + H_2 \text{(waste)}$$

### Step-by-Step Solution for Reaction A

**Step 1:** Identify molecular weights (using standard atomic weights: C = 12, H = 1).

For Reactants:
- $C_4H_6$ (1,3-butadiene): $MW = 4(12) + 6(1) = 48 + 6 = 54$ g/mol
- $C_2H_4$ (ethylene): $MW = 2(12) + 4(1) = 24 + 4 = 28$ g/mol

For Products:
- $C_6H_{10}$ (cyclohexene): $MW = 6(12) + 10(1) = 72 + 10 = 82$ g/mol

**Step 2:** Apply the atom economy formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AE &= \frac{\text{MW of desired product}}{\sum \text{MW of all reactants}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{82}{54 + 28} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{82}{82} \times 100 \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result. *100% atom economy means every atom from the reactants is incorporated into the product. The Diels-Alder reaction is a perfect example of an inherently green reaction — there are no byproducts.*

### Step-by-Step Solution for Reaction B

**Step 1:** Calculate molecular weights.

Reactants:
- $C_4H_6$: MW = 54 g/mol
- $Br_2$: MW = $2(80) = 160$ g/mol

Desired Product:
- $C_4H_6Br_2$ (target): MW = $4(12) + 6(1) + 2(80) = 48 + 6 + 160 = 214$ g/mol

**Step 2:** Apply the atom economy formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AE &= \frac{214}{54 + 160} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{214}{214} \times 100 \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Now, the E-Factor analysis reveals the real story.

If the reaction is run for **100 g of product**, the *theoretical minimum* of reactants used is:
- $C_4H_6$: $(54/214) \times 100 = 25.23$ g
- $Br_2$: $(160/214) \times 100 = 74.77$ g

If 100% yield, the E-Factor is 0, but in practice there is waste (excess reagents, solvents, unreacted starting material). Even in the **ideal case** with full atom economy, the *practical* E-factor is dominated by solvent use. The point of the comparison is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Atom Economy is a **theoretical ceiling** based on stoichiometry. E-Factor and PMI are **practical measures** that include solvents, workup materials, and process losses. Both must be considered for a true green assessment.

---

## Example 2: Industrial Case Study — Adipic Acid Synthesis

**Old Route (Classical, uses benzene):**
$$C_6H_6 + H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_{12} \text{ (cyclohexane)}$$
$$C_6H_{12} + O_2 \rightarrow C_6H_{10}O_2 \text{ (cyclohexanone)}$$
$$C_6H_{10}O_2 + HNO_3 \rightarrow C_6H_{10}O_4 + N_2O \text{ (adipic acid + nitrous oxide)}$$

The $N_2O$ byproduct is a **greenhouse gas 310 times more potent than CO₂** — a major environmental problem.

**New Green Route (Bio-catalytic, uses glucose):**
$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{engineered E. coli}} C_6H_{10}O_4 + H_2O$$

This engineered microbial route uses **renewable feedstock (glucose)**, ambient conditions, aqueous media, and produces no $N_2O$.

| **Parameter** | **Old Route (Benzene)** | **New Route (Glucose)** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | Non-renewable (petroleum) | Renewable (biomass) |
| Reaction Conditions | High T, high P, HNO₃ | Ambient T, ambient P |
| Byproduct | N₂O (potent GHG) | H₂O (benign) |
| Atom Economy | Low (~57%) | High (~85%) |
| E-Factor | High | Low |
| Carbon Footprint | High | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: The 12 Principles of Green Chemistry — Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0["GREEN CHEMISTRY ROOT OBJECTIVE"]
    P0 --> P1["PRINCIPLE 1 Prevention of Waste"]
    P0 --> P2["PRINCIPLE 2 Atom Economy"]
    P0 --> P3["PRINCIPLE 3 Less Hazardous Syntheses"]
    P0 --> P4["PRINCIPLE 4 Safer Chemical Products"]
    P0 --> P5["PRINCIPLE 5 Safer Solvents"]
    P0 --> P6["PRINCIPLE 6 Energy Efficiency"]
    P0 --> P7["PRINCIPLE 7 Renewable Feedstocks"]
    P0 --> P8["PRINCIPLE 8 Reduce Derivatives"]
    P0 --> P9["PRINCIPLE 9 Catalysis"]
    P0 --> P10["PRINCIPLE 10 Design for Degradation"]
    P0 --> P11["PRINCIPLE 11 Real Time Analysis"]
    P0 --> P12["PRINCIPLE 12 Accident Prevention"]
    P1 --> OUT1["GOAL Zero Waste Generation"]
    P2 --> OUT2["GOAL Maximum Atom Incorporation"]
    P3 --> OUT3["GOAL Minimal Toxicity"]
    P4 --> OUT3
    P5 --> OUT3
    P9 --> OUT4["GOAL High TON and TOF"]
    P6 --> OUT5["GOAL Low Energy Footprint"]
    P7 --> OUT6["GOAL Sustainable Resource Use"]
    P10 --> OUT7["GOAL Benign End of Life"]
    P11 --> OUT8["GOAL Process Control and Monitoring"]
    P12 --> OUT9["GOAL Process Safety"]
```

## Diagram 2: Block-Level Green Chemistry Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Identify Target Molecule or Process"] --> DSGN["Design Synthetic Route"]
    DSGN --> Q1{"Is Atom Economy > 80%?"}
    Q1 -- NO --> REDSGN["Re-design Reaction Pathway"]
    REDSGN --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> SOL["Select Green Solvent or Solvent Free"]
    SOL --> CAT["Apply Catalysis over Stoichiometry"]
    CAT --> ENG["Optimize Energy: ambient T and P if possible"]
    ENG --> FEED{"Renewable Feedstock Available?"}
    FEED -- YES --> USE["Use Biomass Based Reactant"]
    FEED -- NO --> MIN["Minimize Petroleum Dependency"]
    USE --> DEG["Ensure Product is Biodegradable"]
    MIN --> DEG
    DEG --> SAFE["Apply Inherently Safer Chemistry"]
    SAFE --> MON["Real Time In Process Monitoring"]
    MON --> OUT["OUTPUT Green Sustainable Product"]
```

## Diagram 3: Comparative Process Map — Traditional vs. Green Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL CHEMISTRY APPROACH"]
        T1["Raw Materials"] --> T2["Synthesis Reaction"]
        T2 --> T3["Desired Product"]
        T2 --> T4["Hazardous Waste and Byproducts"]
        T4 --> T5["End of Pipe Treatment"]
        T5 --> T6["Safe Disposal"]
    end
    subgraph GREEN["GREEN CHEMISTRY APPROACH"]
        G1["Renewable Raw Materials"] --> G2["Catalytic High Atom Economy Reaction"]
        G2 --> G3["Desired Product"]
        G2 --> G4["Minimal Non Hazardous Byproducts"]
        G4 --> G5["Designed for Degradation"]
        G3 --> G6["Real Time Monitoring Throughout"]
        G2 --> G6
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice how the *Traditional* path has an extra "treatment" block — a reactive response to waste. The *Green* path is a streamlined, prevention-first flow where the design itself eliminates most of the waste upstream.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Green Chemistry. List any four of its twelve principles.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Green Chemistry is the design of chemical products and processes that **reduce or eliminate the use and generation of hazardous substances**. It is a proactive pollution-prevention philosophy formulated by Anastas and Warner in 1998.

**Any four principles (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Prevention:** It is better to prevent waste than to treat or clean up waste after it is formed.
2. **Atom Economy:** Synthetic methods should maximize the incorporation of all materials into the final product.
3. **Less Hazardous Chemical Syntheses:** Use and generate substances with little or no toxicity.
4. **Designing Safer Chemicals:** Chemical products should be effective with minimal toxicity.
5. **Safer Solvents and Auxiliaries:** Avoid auxiliary substances; when used, they should be innocuous.
6. **Design for Energy Efficiency:** Minimize energy requirements; run at ambient T and P if possible.
7. **Use of Renewable Feedstocks:** Use renewable raw materials rather than depleting ones.
8. **Reduce Derivatives:** Avoid unnecessary blocking, protection, and deprotection steps.
9. **Catalysis:** Use catalytic reagents over stoichiometric ones.
10. **Design for Degradation:** Products should break down into innocuous substances.
11. **Real-time Analysis:** Develop monitoring methods to detect hazards in real time.
12. **Inherently Safer Chemistry:** Choose substances to minimize accident potential.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Define "Atom Economy" and state its significance. Calculate the atom economy of the reaction:**
$$CH_4 + 2Cl_2 \rightarrow CH_2Cl_2 + 2HCl$$

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Atom Economy is the measure of how efficiently a chemical reaction incorporates the atoms of the reactants into the desired product. It is calculated as:

$$
AE = \frac{\text{Molecular weight of desired product}}{\sum \text{Molecular weights of all reactants}} \times 100
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** It indicates the *theoretical maximum efficiency* of a reaction and helps chemists design greener synthetic routes by minimizing byproducts.

**Calculation (1 mark):**
- $CH_2Cl_2$ (desired product): MW = $12 + 2(1) + 2(35.5) = 85$ g/mol
- $CH_4$ (reactant): MW = $12 + 4(1) = 16$ g/mol
- $2Cl_2$ (reactant): MW = $2(71) = 142$ g/mol

$$
\begin{aligned}
AE &= \frac{85}{16 + 142} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{85}{158} \times 100 \\
&= 53.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 53.8% of the reactant mass becomes the desired product. The remaining 46.2% becomes HCl (waste/byproduct). This is a *low atom economy* reaction, indicating it is not very "green" in its stoichiometry.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** In the End Semester Examination, Module 4 typically offers an internal choice between two questions of 14 marks. Both alternatives are given below.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of "Atom Economy" and "E-Factor" in Green Chemistry. Discuss their significance in designing green synthetic routes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, discuss the role of **catalysis** and **renewable feedstocks** in promoting Green Chemistry. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Atom Economy (3 marks):** Defined by Barry Trost in 1991, it is the ratio of the molecular weight of the desired product to the sum of molecular weights of all reactants, expressed as a percentage. The formula is:

$$
AE = \frac{\text{MW of desired product}}{\sum \text{MW of all reactants}} \times 100
$$

A reaction with 100% atom economy has no byproducts (e.g., Diels-Alder cycloaddition). The significance of atom economy is that it provides a **theoretical measure of reaction efficiency** independent of yield, prompting chemists to choose addition reactions over substitution/elimination reactions.

**E-Factor (2 marks):** Introduced by Roger Sheldon in 1992, the E-Factor is the ratio of the mass of total waste produced to the mass of the desired product:

$$
E = \frac{\text{Mass of waste}}{\text{Mass of desired product}}
$$

E-Factor is significant because it captures the **practical reality** of a process — including solvents, reagents in excess, and reaction byproducts. Industry-wise E-Factors vary: oil refining (~0.1), bulk chemicals (<1–5), fine chemicals (5–50), pharmaceuticals (25–100). Lower E-Factor = greener process.

**Significance in green synthesis (2 marks):** Together, AE and E-Factor force chemists to consider both **stoichiometric efficiency** and **practical waste minimization**. They guide the redesign of processes such as the BHC Ibuprofen synthesis (6 steps → 3 steps, AE improved, waste reduced by ~80%).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Role of Catalysis (3.5 marks):**
Catalysts are used in small quantities but can perform the same reaction many times (high TON), unlike stoichiometric reagents which are consumed in equal or greater molar amounts. Catalytic reactions are greener because:
1. They reduce reagent consumption and waste.
2. They often enable milder reaction conditions (lower T and P).
3. They can improve selectivity, reducing byproducts.

**Examples:**
- **Ziegler-Natta catalysts** in polymerization of ethylene.
- **Catalytic converters** in automobiles (Pt-Pd-Rh converting CO, NOx, hydrocarbons to CO₂, N₂, H₂O).
- **Hydrogenation with Wilkinson catalyst** RhCl(PPh₃)₃ instead of stoichiometric LiAlH₄ reduction.

**Role of Renewable Feedstocks (3.5 marks):**
Renewable feedstocks are biomass-derived raw materials (e.g., glucose, lignin, cellulose, vegetable oils) that can be replenished, unlike fossil-derived feedstocks (petroleum, coal, natural gas).

**Examples:**
- **Bio-ethanol from sugarcane** replacing petroleum-derived ethylene for polymer production.
- **Polylactic acid (PLA)** from corn starch for biodegradable plastics.
- **Biodiesel** from transesterification of vegetable oils with methanol.
- **Furfural and HMF** (from agricultural waste) as platform chemicals replacing petroleum aromatics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks on Green Chemistry Answers:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "yield" with "atom economy."** A reaction can have 100% yield but low AE (e.g., substitution reactions producing stoichiometric waste).
> 2. **Do not write "E-Factor = 0" unless explicitly stated.** E-Factor is zero only in the theoretical limit; practical processes always have waste.
> 3. **Always cite the source year (Anastas & Warner, 1998)** when discussing the 12 principles — this is a typical 1-mark follow-up.
> 4. **Solvent selection is crucial.** Simply saying "use water" is not enough — explain *why* (renewable, non-toxic, non-flammable) and the trade-off (poor solubility for many organic substrates).
> 5. **Do not confuse the "12 Principles" with the "9 Rules" or "5 Rs"** (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Redesign). These are related but distinct frameworks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **green synthesis of Ibuprofen** and explain how it follows multiple principles of Green Chemistry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **green solvents** with examples. Why is water considered a green solvent? Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Boot's Pure Drug Company (1960s)** Ibuprofen synthesis involved **6 steps** with significant waste generation. The BHC Company (now BASF) developed a **green synthesis** in the 1990s using only **3 catalytic steps** with a much higher atom economy.

**Old Route (3.5 marks):**
1. Friedel-Crafts acylation of isobutylbenzene with acetic anhydride (using AlCl₃ as stoichiometric Lewis acid — Principle 9 violation).
2. Darzens reaction with ethyl chloroacetate.
3. Hydrolysis, decarboxylation, and saponification.
4. Multiple steps with HCl, H₂SO₄, and high temperatures.

**New Green Route (3.5 marks):**
1. **Step 1:** Catalytic hydrogenation of p-isobutylacetophenone to the corresponding alcohol (using a Raney-Ni or Ru catalyst — Principle 9: catalysis).
2. **Step 2:** Pd/C-catalyzed carbonylation of the alcohol directly to 2-arylpropionic acid (Principle 9: catalysis; Principle 2: high atom economy, no byproducts).
3. **Step 3:** Direct isolation and purification.

**Principles followed (integrated into the discussion):**
- **Principle 1 (Prevention):** Reduced waste by ~80%.
- **Principle 2 (Atom Economy):** Carbonylation step has nearly 100% AE.
- **Principle 6 (Energy Efficiency):** Reaction run at lower temperatures than the old route.
- **Principle 9 (Catalysis):** Pd/C and Raney-Ni replace stoichiometric AlCl₃.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Concept of Green Solvents (2 marks):** Green solvents are solvents that **reduce environmental impact, are non-toxic, non-flammable, renewable, and easily recyclable**. Traditional solvents like benzene, chloroform, and carbon tetrachloride are major environmental hazards (carcinogenic, ozone-depleting).

**Examples of Green Solvents (2 marks):**
- **Water** — non-toxic, non-flammable, renewable.
- **Supercritical CO₂** (sc-CO₂) — used in decaffeination of coffee and dry cleaning; tunable solvent power.
- **Ionic liquids** — non-volatile, recyclable, tunable polarity.
- **Bio-based solvents** — ethanol from sugarcane, ethyl lactate, glycerol derivatives.

**Why Water is a Green Solvent (2 marks):**
1. **Non-toxic and non-flammable** — safer workplace and transport.
2. **Renewable** — natural water cycle replenishes it.
3. **High heat capacity** — acts as a thermal buffer, enabling safer exothermic reactions.
4. **Inert** toward many reaction types.
5. **Cheap and abundantly available** — economically attractive.

**Limitations of Water (1 mark):**
1. **Poor solubility** for many non-polar organic substrates.
2. **High boiling point** makes removal energy-intensive.
3. **Reactive with water-sensitive reagents** (Grignard reagents, acid chlorides, organometallics).
4. **Catalyst incompatibility** in some hydrogenation and oxidation reactions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield KTU Module 4: Green Chemistry)**
>
> - **Definition:** Green Chemistry = design of chemical products and processes to *reduce or eliminate* hazardous substances (Anastas & Warner, 1998).
> - **12 Principles** — memorize all 12 with their keywords:
>   1. Prevention
>   2. Atom Economy
>   3. Less Hazardous Syntheses
>   4. Safer Chemicals
>   5. Safer Solvents
>   6. Energy Efficiency
>   7. Renewable Feedstocks
>   8. Reduce Derivatives
>   9. Catalysis
>   10. Design for Degradation
>   11. Real-time Analysis
>   12. Accident Prevention
> - **Atom Economy Formula:** $AE = \frac{\text{MW product}}{\sum \text{MW reactants}} \times 100$. Max = 100%.
> - **E-Factor Formula:** $E = \frac{\text{Mass of waste}}{\text{Mass of product}}$. Industry values: oil refining ~0.1, pharmaceuticals 25–100.
> - **Catalysis > Stoichiometry** — high TON, low waste.
> - **Renewable > Petrochemical** — biomass, bio-ethanol, PLA, biodiesel.
> - **Solvent selection** — water, supercritical CO₂, ionic liquids preferred over benzene/chloroform.
> - **Case studies to remember:** BHC Ibuprofen (6→3 steps), bio-adipic acid from glucose, polycarbonate from CO₂, polylactic acid.
> - **Diels-Alder reaction** is the classic example of 100% atom economy.
> - **Substitution reactions** (e.g., $CH_4 + Cl_2 \rightarrow CH_2Cl_2 + 2HCl$) are *not* atom-economical — they produce stoichiometric HCl waste.
> - **Difference to know:** Atom Economy is theoretical; E-Factor and PMI are practical. Both needed for green assessment.
> - **Green Chemistry ≠ Waste Treatment** — it is *prevention*, not *cure*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT721 - SUSTAINABILITY IN ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Green Computing** (also widely referred to as *Green IT* or *Sustainable Computing*) is the environmentally responsible and ecologically sustainable use of computers, related peripheral devices, and Information Technology (IT) resources. It encompasses the entire life-cycle management of IT infrastructure — from raw material extraction in manufacturing, through operational energy consumption, to end-of-life disposal and recycling — with the core objective of **minimizing the environmental footprint** of digital technologies.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for OESFT721, Green Computing is formally defined as:

> *"The study and practice of designing, manufacturing, using, and disposing of computing devices and associated subsystems efficiently and effectively with minimal or no impact on the environment."*

The discipline operates on **four foundational pillars** (often termed the "4R Strategy of Green IT"):

| Pillar | Full Form | Engineering Interpretation |
|--------|-----------|---------------------------|
| **Reduce** | Reduction | Lower energy & material consumption |
| **Reuse** | Reuse | Extend hardware life cycles |
| **Recycle** | Recycling | Recover materials from e-waste |
| **Recover** | Recovery | Reclaim energy from disposed systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> Green Computing is treated as the *culmination module* of the course. Examiners expect students to connect IT systems engineering with sustainability principles (Modules 1–3). Always frame answers by linking **carbon footprint → energy economics → e-waste circularity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traditional car (a "Brown" computer)**: it burns fuel constantly, releases smoke, and leaves rusting metal at the junkyard. Now imagine an **electric scooter with a swappable battery and biodegradable frame (a "Green" computer)**: it sips electricity from solar panels, recycles its battery core, and the chassis decomposes naturally.

**Green Computing is the electric scooter of the IT world.** The "fuel" is electricity (often generated from fossil fuels), the "smoke" is the carbon emissions and heat dissipated by servers, and the "rusting metal" is the millions of tonnes of **e-waste (electronic waste)** dumped in landfills each year.

The **three environmental villains** Green Computing fights against are:
1. **Excessive Energy Consumption** — data centers consume roughly **1–2% of global electricity**.
2. **Carbon Emissions** — the ICT sector contributes approximately **2–4% of global CO₂ emissions** (comparable to the aviation industry).
3. **E-Waste Toxicity** — over **50 million metric tonnes** of e-waste are generated globally each year, containing lead, mercury, and cadmium.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Green" Threshold Metric**
> The industry benchmark for an "energy-productive" data center is a **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) value approaching 1.0**. A PUE of 2.0 means that for every **1 unit** of power feeding the servers, an *equal* 1 unit is wasted on cooling/infrastructure. Modern hyperscale data centers (Google, Microsoft) target **PUE ≈ 1.1**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants & Standards)

- **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** — global average ≈ **1.58** (Uptime Institute, 2023).
- **Data Center Energy Productivity (DCEP)** — measured in **gCO₂/kWh** of useful work.
- **IT Equipment Energy Star Compliance Standard** — must achieve **≥ 85% energy efficiency** at typical loads.
- **E-Waste Recycling Rate Target (UN Sustainable Development Goal 12.5)** — **30% global formal recycling** by 2030.
- **Average server operating temperature (ASHRAE recommended)** — **18 °C to 27 °C (64.4 °F to 80.6 °F)**.
- **RoHS Compliance Limit** — lead content below **0.1% by weight** in homogeneous materials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture of Green Computing

Green Computing is not a single technology — it is a **layered, systems-engineering approach**. The KTU 2024 syllabus breaks it down into six operational strata:

### Layer 1: Green Design (Hardware Level)
- **Energy-efficient processors** (e.g., ARM-based chips, low-power x86 architectures like Intel Core Ultra).
- Use of **recyclable, RoHS-compliant materials** in Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs).
- **Hibernate vs Sleep vs Off-state power analysis**: a typical desktop in OFF state still draws **0.5 W to 5 W** ("phantom load" / "vampire power").
- **Solid-State Drives (SSDs)** consume **2–5 W** vs. **6–15 W** for HDDs.

### Layer 2: Green Manufacturing (Production Level)
- Adoption of **ISO 14001 Environmental Management Systems**.
- **Lead-free soldering** (replaced with silver, tin, copper alloys).
- Renewable energy in fabrication plants (TSMC, Intel fabs now targeting **100% renewable electricity**).
- **Design for Disassembly (DfD)** — modular hardware that can be easily broken down for recycling.

### Layer 3: Green Usage (Operational Level)
- **Virtualization** — running multiple OS instances on a single physical server (consolidation ratio 10:1 to 80:1).
- **Cloud Computing** — shared infrastructure reduces per-user energy demand.
- **Power Management Protocols**: ACPI (Advanced Configuration and Power Interface) states — **S0 (Working) → S1 (Power On Suspend) → S3 (Standby) → S4 (Hibernation) → S5 (Soft Off)**.
- **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** — reduces CPU voltage during low load.

### Layer 4: Green Disposal / End-of-Life (E-Waste Management)
- **WEEE Directive (Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment)** — EU mandate.
- **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** — manufacturer is responsible for entire product lifecycle.
- Refurbishment, remarketing, and component harvesting (memory chips, rare-earth magnets from HDDs).

### Layer 5: Regulatory & Standards Layer
- **Energy Star** (US EPA program).
- **EPEAT** (Electronic Product Environmental Assessment Tool) — Bronze, Silver, Gold ratings.
- **80 PLUS Certification** — power supply efficiency (Bronze 82%, Silver 85%, Gold 87%, Platinum 90%, Titanium 92%).

### Layer 6: The Socio-Economic Layer
- **Green IT Awareness & Education** — organizational training programs.
- **Carbon Credits & Carbon Trading** — monetizing avoided emissions.
- **Circular Economy Models** — "Product-as-a-Service" reduces ownership of physical hardware.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Green Computing — Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Economic** | Energy is 30–50% of a data center's OPEX; green tech reduces this significantly. |
| **Regulatory** | Governments impose carbon taxes (e.g., EU ETS, India's PAT scheme). |
| **Thermal** | High energy = high heat = cooling overhead = cascading energy demand. |
| **Resource Scarcity** | Coltan, lithium, rare-earth elements are geopolitically constrained. |
| **Corporate ESG Mandates** | Investors demand Environmental, Social, Governance disclosures. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield formulas frequently asked in KTU University Exams for the Green Computing module. Memorize the symbols and units precisely.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Variables & Units | Real-World Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Power Consumption (DC)** | $P = V \times I$ | V = Voltage (V), I = Current (A), P = Watt (W) | Calculating energy draw of a server rack |
| 2 | **Energy Consumption** | $E = P \times t$ | P = Power (W), t = Time (h), E = Wh (or kWh) | Monthly electricity bill computation |
| 3 | **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** | $PUE = \dfrac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$ | $P_{total}$ = Total facility power (kW), $P_{IT}$ = IT load (kW) | Industry standard data center efficiency metric |
| 4 | **Data Center Infrastructure Efficiency (DCiE)** | $DCiE = \dfrac{1}{PUE} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a percentage | Inverse of PUE; higher is greener |
| 5 | **Carbon Emissions (CO₂)** | $CO_2 = E \times EF$ | E = Energy (kWh), EF = Emission Factor (kgCO₂/kWh) | India's grid EF ≈ **0.82 kgCO₂/kWh** |
| 6 | **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** | $CED = E_{manufacture} + E_{use} + E_{disposal}$ | All energy phases in MJ | LCA assessment of a laptop |
| 7 | **Server Utilization Efficiency (SUE)** | $SUE = \dfrac{P_{servers}}{P_{IT equipment}}$ | Ratio (lower is better) | Evaluates idle server waste |
| 8 | **Virtualization Ratio (VR)** | $VR = \dfrac{N_{VMs}}{N_{physical\;servers}}$ | $N_{VMs}$ = Virtual machines, $N_{physical}$ = Physical hosts | Consolidation metric |
| 9 | **Annual Energy Savings (%)** | $\% Saved = \dfrac{E_{old} - E_{new}}{E_{old}} \times 100$ | Energy in kWh/year | Quantifies green IT upgrades |
| 10 | **Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{\Delta S_{annual}}$ | Cost in ₹, Savings in ₹/year | Solar panel ROI calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE**
> The KTU Formula Sheet uses the *absolute value bars* in some parameter constraints. In the LaTeX-rendered KTU 2024 answer sheets, write $\vert V \vert \leq 12\text{ V}$ rather than $|V| \leq 12\text{ V}$ to maintain table formatting.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Green Computing principles are deployed in:
- **Hyperscale Data Centers** (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) — using AI-driven cooling (DeepMind's recommendation system cut Google's cooling bill by **40%**).
- **Edge Computing** — bringing computation closer to data sources to reduce network energy loss.
- **Sustainable Software Engineering** — writing code with lower computational complexity, reducing CPU time (and therefore energy) per transaction.
- **Green AI** — training models on carbon-aware scheduling (running workloads when renewable energy is abundant).
- **Smart Grids** — using IoT sensors and AI to balance electricity supply/demand in real time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The PUE Metric from First Principles

The Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) is the **single most important metric** in Green Computing data center engineering. KTU examiners routinely test both its derivation and interpretation.

### Step 1: Define the Total Facility Power

The total power drawn by a data center facility includes both the IT equipment power and the supporting infrastructure power (cooling, lighting, UPS losses, etc.).

$$P_{total} = P_{IT} + P_{cooling} + P_{lighting} + P_{UPS} + P_{other}$$

**Textual Logic:** The facility is a closed energy system. By conservation of energy, every watt entering the building either powers the IT load or is consumed by auxiliary systems. We add a small "other" term to capture miscellaneous draws (security systems, fire suppression pumps, etc.).

### Step 2: Define the IT Equipment Power

The IT equipment power is the power consumed *only* by the computing devices (servers, storage arrays, networking gear).

$$P_{IT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{server,i} + P_{storage} + P_{network}$$

**Textual Logic:** A data center contains $n$ identical or non-identical servers, plus separate storage and networking subsystems. We sum them up because they all draw electrical power to perform IT work.

### Step 3: Form the PUE Ratio

By definition, PUE is the ratio of total facility power to IT power:

$$PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$$

**Textual Logic:** This ratio expresses the *overhead tax* on every watt that actually does computational work. A PUE of 1.0 means perfect efficiency (no overhead), while a PUE of 3.0 means 3 watts are consumed for every 1 watt of IT work (66% waste).

### Step 4: Express the Overhead Fraction Explicitly

Substituting the expanded form of $P_{total}$ from Step 1:

$$PUE = \frac{P_{IT} + P_{cooling} + P_{lighting} + P_{UPS} + P_{other}}{P_{IT}}$$

Splitting the numerator:

$$PUE = \frac{P_{IT}}{P_{IT}} + \frac{P_{cooling} + P_{lighting} + P_{UPS} + P_{other}}{P_{IT}}$$

Simplifying:

$$PUE = 1 + \frac{P_{overhead}}{P_{IT}}$$

**Textual Logic:** The "+1" baseline represents the *unavoidable* IT consumption, while the second term captures all infrastructure losses. Minimizing $P_{overhead}$ is the engineering goal of green data center design.

### Step 5: Express PUE as an Overhead Percentage

Define the overhead percentage as $\eta_{overhead}$:

$$\eta_{overhead} = \frac{P_{overhead}}{P_{IT}} \times 100\% = (PUE - 1) \times 100\%$$

**Textual Logic:** If PUE = 1.5, then $\eta_{overhead} = 50\%$, meaning half the facility's energy is wasted on non-IT systems — typically dominated by chillers and CRAC (Computer Room Air Conditioning) units.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A small enterprise data center has an IT load of 200 kW. The cooling system consumes 80 kW, the UPS system loses 20 kW, and lighting consumes 10 kW. The grid emission factor is **0.82 kgCO₂/kWh**. Calculate:
> (a) The PUE of the data center
> (b) The DCiE percentage
> (c) The annual carbon emissions in tonnes
> (d) The percentage energy saved if the company upgrades the cooling system such that PUE reduces to 1.3

### Solution:

**(a) PUE Calculation:**

$$P_{total} = 200 + 80 + 20 + 10 = 310 \text{ kW}$$

$$PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} = \frac{310}{200} = 1.55$$

**Interpretation:** For every 1 kW doing IT work, 0.55 kW is overhead waste.

**(b) DCiE Calculation:**

$$DCiE = \frac{1}{PUE} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1.55} \times 100\% = 64.52\%$$

**Interpretation:** 64.52% of total power is used productively by IT equipment.

**(c) Annual Carbon Emissions:**

Annual energy consumption:

$$E_{annual} = P_{total} \times t = 310 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h/yr} = 2,715,600 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

Carbon emissions:

$$CO_2 = E_{annual} \times EF = 2,715,600 \times 0.82 = 2,226,792 \text{ kgCO}_2/\text{yr}$$

Converting to tonnes (1 tonne = 1000 kg):

$$CO_2 = 2,226.79 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

**(d) Percentage Energy Savings after Upgrade:**

New total power at PUE = 1.3:

$$P_{total,new} = 1.3 \times 200 = 260 \text{ kW}$$

Old total power (from part a):

$$P_{total,old} = 310 \text{ kW}$$

Energy saved annually:

$$\Delta E = (310 - 260) \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 50 \times 8760 = 438,000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

Percentage saved:

$$\% Saved = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{old}} \times 100\% = \frac{438,000}{2,715,600} \times 100\% \approx 16.13\%$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- PUE = **1.55**
- DCiE = **64.52%**
- Annual CO₂ = **2,226.79 tonnes**
- Energy Savings = **16.13%** per year

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Green Computing Strategies

| Strategy | Primary Mechanism | Typical PUE/Energy Improvement | Cost | Implementation Complexity |
|----------|------------------|-------------------------------|------|--------------------------|
| Server Virtualization | Run 10–80 VMs on 1 physical server | **60–80%** energy reduction | Medium | Low–Medium |
| Hot/Cold Aisle Containment | Isolate cooling airflows | **15–25%** reduction in cooling energy | Low | Low |
| Free Cooling (Air/Economizer) | Use outside air when < 18 °C | **30–50%** annual reduction | Medium | Medium |
| Liquid Cooling | Direct-to-chip or immersion | **40–60%** cooling reduction | High | High |
| Renewable Energy (Solar/Wind PPA) | Replace grid electricity | **80–100%** decarbonization | High | Medium |
| AI-Driven Workload Scheduling | Move compute to green-energy times | **15–30%** carbon reduction | Low | Medium |
| E-Waste Refurbishment | Reuse components | **~90%** embodied carbon saved | Low | Low |
| SSD Migration | Replace HDDs | **5–15 W per drive saved** | Low | Low |
| DVFS + Power Capping | Throttle CPU when idle | **20–40%** peak reduction | Low | Low |
| Carbon-Aware Cloud Region Selection | Choose low-carbon data centers | **10–50%** carbon reduction | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q1. Define Green Computing. List the four primary goals of Green Computing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Green Computing (also called Green IT or Sustainable Computing) is the environmentally responsible and eco-friendly practice of designing, manufacturing, operating, and disposing of computing devices and IT infrastructure in a manner that minimizes environmental impact, conserves energy, and reduces electronic waste throughout the entire life cycle.

**Four Primary Goals:**
1. **Reduction of Hazardous Materials** — eliminating or minimizing toxic substances such as lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), and hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺) from computing hardware. Compliance with RoHS directive.
2. **Improvement of Energy Efficiency** — designing systems that consume less electricity in both active and idle states. Use of Energy Star rated components, power management protocols (ACPI), and efficient cooling.
3. **Promotion of Recyclability and Reusability** — designing for end-of-life recovery of materials (gold, copper, rare-earth elements) and enabling modular refurbishment.
4. **Sustainable Design Philosophy** — integrating cradle-to-cradle thinking, circular economy principles, and ESG frameworks into IT product design and corporate strategy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the "4 Rs" (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover) with the "4 Goals." The 4 Rs are *strategies*, while the 4 Goals are *outcomes*. Examiners expect you to state **goals**, not just tactics. Mixing them costs **0.5–1 mark**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q2. What is Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)? State the ideal PUE value and explain its significance in data center design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** PUE is a standardized metric defined by *The Green Grid consortium* that measures how efficiently a data center uses its incoming electrical power. It is defined as the ratio of **total facility power** to the **IT equipment power** consumed by the computing devices.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} = \frac{P_{IT} + P_{overhead}}{P_{IT}} = 1 + \frac{P_{overhead}}{P_{IT}}$$

**Ideal PUE Value:** **PUE = 1.0** — this represents a hypothetical 100% efficient data center where all incoming power is consumed by IT equipment and no overhead exists. In practice, leading hyperscale facilities achieve PUE values between **1.06 and 1.2**.

**Significance:**
- A **lower PUE** indicates **higher efficiency** (less waste on cooling, lighting, UPS losses).
- A **higher PUE** indicates **more overhead waste** — for example, PUE = 3.0 means 200% overhead (only 33% of power does IT work).
- It enables benchmarking of data center efficiency across facilities and industries.
- Regulatory bodies and ESG investors use PUE as a **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** for sustainable IT.
- Reduces operational expenditure (OPEX) and carbon emissions simultaneously.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the concept of Green Computing in detail. Discuss its significance in addressing the global challenges of energy consumption and e-waste management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the major approaches to implementing Green Computing at the hardware, software, and operational levels. Include relevant examples and standards. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(a):

**1. Concept of Green Computing (3 marks):**
Green Computing, also called *Green IT* or *Sustainable IT*, is the practice of designing, manufacturing, using, and disposing of computing devices, networking equipment, and data center infrastructure in an environmentally sustainable manner. It seeks to **reduce the carbon footprint, energy consumption, and electronic waste** of the entire ICT sector — a sector that currently accounts for **2–4% of global CO₂ emissions**, comparable to the global aviation industry.

The concept is built on the **4R framework**:
- **Reduce** energy and material usage
- **Reuse** hardware and components
- **Recycle** end-of-life materials
- **Recover** value from disposed systems

**2. Significance — Energy Consumption (2 marks):**
- Global data centers consumed approximately **460 TWh in 2022**, projected to reach **1000+ TWh by 2026**.
- ICT infrastructure consumes **1–2% of global electricity**, with cooling alone accounting for **40%** of data center power.
- Green Computing reduces this through **virtualization, renewable energy, and AI-driven load management**.

**3. Significance — E-Waste Management (2 marks):**
- Global e-waste generation reached **62 million tonnes in 2022**, growing at **3–4% per year**.
- Only **22.3%** of e-waste is formally recycled; the rest is dumped, incinerated, or illegally exported.
- Green Computing enforces **WEEE Directive compliance, RoHS material restrictions, and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(b):

**1. Hardware-Level Approaches (2.5 marks):**
- Use of **low-power processors** (ARM Cortex, Apple Silicon, Intel Core Ultra).
- Adoption of **SSDs over HDDs** (2–5 W vs 6–15 W per drive).
- **RoHS-compliant lead-free soldering** in PCBs.
- **Energy Star and EPEAT certified** desktops, laptops, and monitors.
- **80 PLUS certified** power supplies (Titanium = 92% efficiency).

**2. Software-Level Approaches (2.5 marks):**
- **Green Software Engineering** — writing code with lower computational complexity (e.g., O(n) instead of O(n²) algorithms reduces CPU time and energy per execution).
- **Power-aware programming** — using sleep states, lazy evaluation, and efficient data structures.
- **Virtualization** — running 10–80 VMs on a single physical host, achieving **60–80% energy savings** through server consolidation.
- **Containerization (Docker, Kubernetes)** — enables efficient resource utilization.

**3. Operational-Level Approaches (2 marks):**
- **Cloud computing with shared infrastructure** — reduces per-user energy demand.
- **Hot/cold aisle containment** in server rooms.
- **Renewable Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs)** for data centers.
- **Carbon-aware workload scheduling** — running compute jobs in regions with abundant renewable energy.
- Compliance with **ISO 14001, LEED certification for data centers**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Q.A.b):** Examiners report that students frequently list only hardware approaches and ignore software/operational strategies. To score full **7 marks**, you MUST structure your answer with **three clear sub-sections** (Hardware, Software, Operational). Mixing them into a single list loses **1.5–2 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q.B. (a)** Define Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) and Data Center Infrastructure Efficiency (DCiE). Derive the mathematical relationship between them. A data center consumes 500 kW of total facility power with an IT load of 350 kW. Calculate the PUE, DCiE, and the percentage overhead loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the ACPI power management states. Discuss the role of virtualization and cloud computing in achieving sustainable IT operations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(a):

**1. Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- **PUE** = Total Facility Power ÷ IT Equipment Power.
- **DCiE** = IT Equipment Power ÷ Total Facility Power × 100% (the *inverse* of PUE, expressed as a percentage).

**2. Mathematical Derivation (2 marks):**

By definition:

$$PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$$

$$DCiE = \frac{P_{IT}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%$$

Dividing DCiE by 100 and inverting:

$$\frac{DCiE}{100\%} = \frac{P_{IT}}{P_{total}} = \frac{1}{PUE}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{DCiE = \frac{1}{PUE} \times 100\% \quad \text{and} \quad PUE = \frac{1}{DCiE/100}}$$

**3. Numerical Calculations (3.5 marks):**

Given: $P_{total} = 500$ kW, $P_{IT} = 350$ kW.

**PUE:**

$$PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} = \frac{500}{350} = 1.4286$$

**DCiE:**

$$DCiE = \frac{1}{PUE} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1.4286} \times 100\% = 70\%$$

Alternative direct calculation:

$$DCiE = \frac{P_{IT}}{P_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{350}{500} \times 100\% = 70\% \checkmark$$

**Overhead Loss Percentage:**

$$\eta_{overhead} = (PUE - 1) \times 100\% = (1.4286 - 1) \times 100\% = 42.86\%$$

Equivalently:

$$\eta_{overhead} = 100\% - DCiE = 100\% - 70\% = 30\%$$

**Final Answer:** PUE = **1.43**, DCiE = **70%**, Overhead = **30%**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(b):

**1. ACPI Power Management States (3.5 marks):**

ACPI (Advanced Configuration and Power Interface) is an open industry standard that defines how operating systems manage power. The five canonical states (G-states for global, S-states for sleep) are:

| State | Name | Power Level | Wake-up Time | Use Case |
|-------|------|-------------|--------------|----------|
| **S0** | Working | Full power | Instant | Active computing |
| **S1** | Power On Suspend | Low, CPU stopped | Fast | Brief pauses |
| **S2** | Standby (rare) | Lower than S1 | Slower | Legacy support |
| **S3** | Sleep (Suspend to RAM) | ~5 W | 1–2 sec | Standby mode |
| **S4** | Hibernation (Suspend to Disk) | ~0.5 W | 5–10 sec | Long-term standby |
| **S5** | Soft Off | ~0.5 W | Full boot required | Shutdown |

G0 = Working, G1 = Sleep, G2 = Soft Off, G3 = Mechanical Off.

**2. Virtualization (2 marks):**
Virtualization allows multiple virtual machines (VMs) to run on a single physical server using a **hypervisor** (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Microsoft Hyper-V).
- **Server Consolidation Ratio:** 10:1 to 80:1.
- **Energy Savings:** 60–80% per consolidated workload.
- **Hardware Utilization:** Improved from typical **5–15%** to **60–80%**.
- Reduces physical hardware, cooling, and space requirements.

**3. Cloud Computing (1.5 marks):**
- Cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) use **hyperscale, energy-optimized data centers** with PUE values between **1.1 and 1.3**.
- **Multi-tenancy** enables shared infrastructure, reducing per-user energy cost.
- **Pay-per-use model** discourages idle hardware.
- Supports **carbon-aware region selection** (e.g., running workloads in Nordic data centers powered by hydroelectric energy).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Q.B.b):** A common error is conflating **G-states (Global)** with **S-states (Sleep)**. KTU examiners expect you to list **at least 4 sleep states** with their respective power consumption differences. Writing only "S0 and S5" without elaboration will cap your score at **2 marks** out of 3.5.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4: Green Computing)**

### 📌 Core Conceptual Points
- **Definition:** Green Computing = Environmentally sustainable IT across the *full life cycle* (design → manufacture → use → disposal).
- **4R Strategy:** Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover.
- **4 Goals:** Reduce hazardous materials, improve energy efficiency, promote recyclability, sustainable design.

### 📌 Must-Memorize Metrics
- **PUE** = $P_{total} / P_{IT}$ (ideal = 1.0; modern hyperscale ≈ 1.1).
- **DCiE** = $(1 / PUE) \times 100\%$.
- **Overhead %** = $(PUE - 1) \times 100\%$.
- **Carbon Emissions** = $E \times EF$ (India's grid EF ≈ 0.82 kgCO₂/kWh).
- **Energy** = $P \times t$ (in kWh).

### 📌 Critical Standards & Certifications
- **RoHS** — restricts Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr⁶⁺.
- **WEEE Directive** — mandates e-waste collection.
- **Energy Star / EPEAT / 80 PLUS** — energy efficiency certifications.
- **ISO 14001** — Environmental Management Systems.
- **LEED Certification** — for green data center buildings.
- **ASHRAE 18–27 °C** — recommended server room temperature.

### 📌 Energy-Saving Strategies (Rank by Impact)
1. **Virtualization / Consolidation** (60–80% energy reduction).
2. **Renewable Energy PPAs** (80–100% decarbonization).
3. **Liquid Cooling / Free Cooling** (30–60% cooling reduction).
4. **Power Management (ACPI Sleep States)** (40–60% peak reduction).
5. **Hot/Cold Aisle Containment** (15–25% reduction).

### 📌 Frequently Confused Pairs
- **PUE vs. DCiE** — PUE is a ratio ≥ 1; DCiE is a percentage ≤ 100%.
- **4 Rs vs. 4 Goals** — Strategies vs. Outcomes.
- **Energy Efficiency vs. Energy Productivity** — Efficiency = less energy for same task; Productivity = more useful work per unit energy.
- **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) vs. Embodied Carbon** — CED is total energy across life cycle; Embodied carbon is just the manufacturing phase CO₂.

### 📌 High-Yield Numerical Problem Types for KTU
- PUE/DCiE computation from given $P_{total}$ and $P_{IT}$.
- Annual CO₂ emissions from energy consumption and grid emission factor.
- Energy savings percentage after an upgrade.
- Virtualization consolidation ratio calculations.
- Payback period for green IT investments.

### 📌 "Easily Missed" Trivia for KTU
- A typical PC in **OFF state** still draws **0.5 W to 5 W** of *phantom power*.
- Global e-waste recycling rate is only **22.3%** (as of 2022).
- The ICT sector's **2–4% global CO₂ footprint** rivals global aviation.
- **Carbon-aware AI scheduling** can shift compute to renewable-rich hours.
- The **RoHS lead limit** is **0.1% by weight** in homogeneous materials.
